## HG Amitasana Dasa

### WHO IS DEAR TO GOD?  | Bhagavad Gita 12.16- 12.20 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai

#### devotion, love of God, Bhagavad Gita
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Zp1jWkwYFo?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Do not try to see the Lord, act in a way that the Lord sees you."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces the topic of who is dear to Krishna, based on the last verses of the twelfth chapter of the Gita, explaining that the lecture will cover verses sixteen to twentieth.

The topic is, who is dear to Krishna? This is the last few verses, last six verses of the twelfth chapter of the Gita where Krishna is saying, who is dear to me? How many of us want to be dear to the Lord? Not that kind of, Bhagwan ko piyare ho gaye ho. So it is used in the connotation of, he has reached God, he has died. So Krishna is saying, they say, do not try to see the Lord, act in a way that the Lord sees you. Now Krishna is giving his opinion, who is very dear to me? What can we do, what kind of person we can become or be, which will be very very pleasing to Krishna. He will be very happy with us and such kind of person is very dear to him. So this is the discussion which we are going to have today, who is dear to God? We are covering sixteenth to twentieth verse of the twelfth chapter and this ends the twelfth chapter.

We are covering sixteenth to twentieth verse of the twelfth chapter and this ends the twelfth chapter. So we almost see the end of the twelfth chapter. Sixty-six person through with the Gita and next week we will start with the thirteenth chapter based on the teachings of Srila Prabhupada, our beloved spiritual master. Translation of the sixteenth verse, a devotee who is not dependent on the ordinary course of activities, who is pure, expert, without cares, free from all pains, and does not strive for some result, is very dear to me. #### devotion, karma yoga, meditation, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Zp1jWkwYFo?t=118
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "When we become attached to the results of our work, we get entangled."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the different levels of devotion described by Krishna in the twelfth chapter, starting with complete dedication and progressing to regulative principles, doing Krishna's work, detachment from results, meditation, and cultivating knowledge.

We have covered in the last two sessions. Krishna says the highest is somebody who is always thinking of me. Completely dedicated. Then he says, knowing that not everyone can fall in this bracket, not everyone can practice this. He says, if you cannot do this, then try to practice the regulative principles and develop a desire to attain me, to love me. Then he further says, if you cannot do this, anyone can help me with an answer, if you cannot do this, do my work. Krishna has a work, yada yadahi dharmasya glanir bhavati bharata. Krishna is doing that work and his representatives are doing that work. So we can do Krishna's work by ourselves being part of his mission or helping others, those who are broadcasting his glories. Then Krishna says, if you cannot do this, then try to be detached from the results of your activities by offering, by using the results of your activities. Some kind of philanthropic work. When we become attached to the results of our work, we get entangled. When we work with the spirit of getting the results and enjoying them, which is also called karma, lust, that is the sinful enemy of the living entity. So he says, even if we cannot give it to Krishna, some people may not have that kind of inclination, that kind of a desire. Okay, give it to some kind of charitable work. Then if you cannot do that also, then Krishna says, you can do meditation. There are lot of people who are doing meditation, meditation on void, meditation on some point, some candle light. At least they have some desire to control the mind, senses. And then Krishna says, if you cannot do this, just cultivate knowledge. There are lot of philosophers who are trying to inquire, trying to figure out, what will make one happy? So, different gradations are there.

He is not dependent on ordinary course of activities. Who is pure, internally and externally, we will go one by one. Who is expert, without cares. We all have lot of anxieties, lot of worries, heavy burden on our head. So he is that way light, free from all pains, who does not strive for results. We are always greedy for results, in anxiety. #### purity, cleanliness, spiritual practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Zp1jWkwYFo?t=319
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Likewise we have to take spiritual bath everyday."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the concept of purity, both internal and external, emphasizing the importance of cleanliness in bhakti yoga and the need for a daily spiritual bath through devotional service.

He is very dear to me. Now pure, Prabhupada in the parbhupada explains, a devotee takes bath. So purification is both internal and external. Regulative principles of bhakti yoga. Because we are not pure, shuchi or cleanliness is given lot of importance. They say cleanliness is next to godliness. So invariably in the temples, especially where deity worship is involved, a lot of prominence is given to cleanliness. Even I have mentioned earlier, the devotees who go into the deity room, they have to invariably take bath and go. If they have gone to clear their stomach or pass water, they have to take bath. At least a brahmana who is trying to purify himself, it is recommended to take minimum of twice, two times bath in a day. And rises early in the morning for devotional service. For purifying his consciousness. Engaging in activities which will purify the dust of materialism which has. So there are two kinds of dust or dirt. One is which accumulates on our body. Which we get purified of by taking our daily bath. And there is another kind of dirt. What is that kind of a dirt? Which covers our pure consciousness. Like we talked about, root cause of our misery is our identification with the body. Dehatma buddhi. So as we, just like we take our bath and then in course of the day we accumulate dirt, we perspire, we become dirty. And then next day again we take a bath. Likewise invariably we will get dirty as far as our consciousness is concerned. So just like we all have got used to, if one day we don't take bath, we feel so uneasy, so uncomfortable, we feel so unclean. Likewise we have to take spiritual bath everyday. And what is that spiritual bath? Start the day with devotional service. Shravanam, Kirtanam, remembering the Lord, chanting the holy names. There are many people who are very comfortable not taking a bath. Maybe they take bath once in two days or three days. Or they take bath sometime late in the evening. They don't feel the necessity or probably they are quite comfortable without it. Same is the case, a person may not feel the necessity of chanting, reading, hearing.

Or clearing his consciousness. He is comfortably okay. So naturally a devotee who is engaged in these activities, taking bath, purifying his consciousness on a regular basis, daily basis, over a period of time he becomes cleansed both internally and externally. A devotee is always expert because he has the complete overall picture of things. He knows who he is. He knows the purpose of this creation. He knows why he has come in this world. He knows from where he has come. He knows where is he going to go after death. The overall context is there. Bearings are there. And as a result, whatever activities he does, because the context is there, he is not lost. He does that activity in knowledge, with the right context, with the right understanding. He is never pained.

#### suffering, bodily conception of life, soul
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Zp1jWkwYFo?t=550
**Verse References:** BG 2.11
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Actually all the challenges what we face, all the difficulties what we face is because of that root disease."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the root cause of misery is identifying with the body, referencing Arjuna's dilemma in the first chapter of the Gita and Krishna's subsequent teachings about the soul in the second chapter, emphasizing that all challenges stem from the bodily concept of life.

As we started our discussion today, the root cause of our misery, even in the first chapter of the Gita, Arjuna was dilemma, confused, bewildered. What will happen to my kinsmen? The dynasty will be Lord. Women will become unchaste. Varna Sankara will be born. And if we analyze, morally speaking, ethically speaking, all his arguments were quite wise. But they were stemming from a very very deep rooted disease and that was identifying ourselves with the body. And that's why in the second chapter of the Gita, Krishna says, you are speaking like a wise man. There is no doubt about it. You are speaking like a wise man. But how come these impurities have come upon you? So this is an impurity. To think that I am the body. Therefore, without addressing the actual problems which Arjuna mentioned, should I fight, not fight, Krishna in the second chapter started talking about the soul. In one sense, it looks out of context. Battle is about to begin. Two armies have gathered. The conch shells have been blown. And it's not an ordinary setting. The battle is about life and death situation for crores and crores of people who have accumulated there. And Krishna starts talking about the soul. So for many of us also, the discussion what we have here may look something impractical. We are dealing with real challenges in our world, in our lives. And here we are talking about some philosophical concepts. Soul, body, Krishna, life after death. Actually all the challenges what we face, all the difficulties what we face is because of that root disease. And the root disease is bodily concept of life. I am the body. So beginning of spiritual life.

#### soul, identification, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Zp1jWkwYFo?t=683
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Beginning of purification is when one starts understanding that I am not the body. I am a soul."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the beginning of spiritual life is understanding that one is not the body but a soul. He uses the analogy of being a cricket fan to illustrate how identification with something external can affect one's happiness and distress, contrasting it with a state of detachment where external events don't have the same impact.

Beginning of purification is when one starts understanding that I am not the body. I am a soul. So because a devotee of his internal purification, because of his pure consciousness, because of pure knowledge, he cultivates the understanding that I am not the body. I am a spirit soul. There may be ups and downs in the body. Body may get diseased. There may be pain in the body. But because he is not identified with the body, that pain does not touch him. This is a very good example. I have repeated n number of times. Some of you would have got bored listening this. Can anyone help me what example I am going to share? Those who have been regular would have heard at least 5-6 times. Like when I was young, I was very fond of cricket. Every match, every ball. Now Indian team is in Australia. So there were time when I would get up at 4 o'clock in the morning. Benson & Hedges Cup. Krishnamachari Shrikant. Ravi Shastri, yes. And every ball would excite me or What did he do? Why did he do that? He should have been a little more patient. What was the hurry? Every ball was affecting my happiness and distress. Because I was identifying with the body. Identifying with the Indian team. What Indian team was doing there was affecting my happiness and distress. Now take it in right stride. It's not that you cannot enjoy cricket or cannot watch cricket. Now still Indian team is playing. Still it is winning or losing. But because I have not identified closely associated with that team. They may win or lose. But it is not affecting me. Can you see the identification? How does it help? Identification. Because you have identified with the team. They win, you feel like you have won. They lose, you feel you have lost. Actually if you have not identified. If you don't watch television. If you don't watch a cricket match. Indian team is still win or lose. But your happiness is unaffected by that.

#### duality, desire, envy, material world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Zp1jWkwYFo?t=854
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "They fell in this world of duality."
**Summary:** The speaker transitions to the story of Adam and Eve in the Garden of Eden, explaining how their desire to enjoy something forbidden led to their fall into the material world of duality, characterized by desire, envy, and the experience of happiness and distress.

So this is a crude example. Now this is a story in the Bible. Of Adam and Eve. They say the first God created Adam and Eve. Which is something very similar which we also have in our Vedic literatures. So they were in the garden of Eden's. And the Lord told them. That I have provided everything what is required for your happiness. Be happy. Enjoy life. There won't be any scarcity whatsoever. There were trees which were giving ample amount of fruits. There were rivers. There were profuse supply of water. Everything they required to live happily. But the Lord instructed. There is one tree in this whole garden of Eden. An apple tree. Just avoid eating apple from that tree. Just one tree. Avoid eating apple from that tree. So okay. No problem. So they were busy enjoying in that garden of Eden. And one day while Adam and Eve were passing through that garden. Nearby that tree. A snake which was living in that tree. Hissed in the ears of Eve. And said why don't you have an apple? So she remembered the instruction. No, no. I am not supposed to have apple from this tree. So the snake said. Actually you know. What is the harm if you have an apple? You know the reason why you have been forbidden. Why the Lord has told you not to have an apple? Because if you have apple from this tree. You will become God. And God does not want others to become like him. So this is the reason why. This is the actual reason why the Lord has forbidden you to eat apple from this tree. So having given this understanding. The snake offered the apple. And the Eve got tempted. And took that apple. Also the Adam took that apple. Moment they ate the apple. Two things happened. What happened? Anyone can help me with the answer. Two things. They fell down from Garden of Eden to Eden Gardens in Calcutta. To this material world. So that is one thing which happened. And second thing is. They. Fell in this world of duality. Man. Women. Good. Bad. Happiness. Distress. Which is something very very similar which is there in our Vedic literature. Ichcha. Ichcha means desire. Desire to enjoy. Something which is forbidden. Forbidden fruit. And Dvesha. Envy. Why God? Why I cannot be the God? And Ichcha. Dvesha. And Ichcha. Dvesha. Envy. And Ichcha. Dvesha. Samuthena. Dvandva. Dvandva means duality.

#### duality, detachment, desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Zp1jWkwYFo?t=1071
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The goal is to have the right kind of desires."
**Summary:** The speaker continues explaining that the material world is a world of duality, where people constantly seek happiness and avoid pain. He contrasts this with the spiritual world, which is pure bliss. He then states that a pure devotee does not endeavor for material gains, but is ambitious for the service of the Lord, clarifying that the goal is not to become desireless, but to have the right kind of desires.

They fall in this world. This world is a world of duality. Pain and pleasure. Good and bad. Male and female. Fame and infamy. So all what we are trying to do in this world is the world of duality. We want happiness. We want happiness and we are trying to avoid pain. We want fame, avoiding infaming. In the spiritual world, there is no duality. It's pure bliss. So, he does not endeavor for material gains. A pure devotee does not endeavor for material gains. Now, that does not mean that he is lazy, he is inert, has no aspirations, has no goals, has nothing to look forward to. That's not the right understanding. Not for his material gains. Whatever the Lord has given, he is satisfied. Many of us are geared up to improve our material situation. More money so that I can enjoy better. I can have a better house, a better car and things like that. A pure devotee is callous about all these requirements. But for the service of the Lord, he is very ambitious. In other words, when it comes to building a palatial house for himself, he is not interested. But when it comes to building a gorgeous temple for the Lord, he is very, very charged up. The understanding is not that the goal has become to become desireless. The goal is to have the right kind of desires. Lust is desire to enjoy independent of the Lord. And prema is to have desires to serve the Lord. You are object of love. So, that way a devotee is not inert, he is not lazy, he is not, so to say, lazy. Completely not interested in anything. One who neither grasps pleasure or grief, who neither laments nor desires, and who renounces both auspicious and inauspicious things, is very dear to me.

#### detachment, transience, duty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Zp1jWkwYFo?t=1224
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Because he is disidentified with the body, and is culturing the understanding that he is a soul, he is unaffected by all these things."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that a pure devotee is not affected by material gains or losses, good times or bad times, because they are disidentified with the body and understand that they are a soul. He uses the analogy of a train journey to illustrate how understanding the transient nature of things can prevent unnecessary conflict and attachment.

A pure devotee is neither happy nor distressed over material gains or loss. There may be some good times, there may be bad times. There may be some gains, material gains. There could be some material loss. The body may go through some turbulence. He may have some diseases. He is not distressed. The example I gave, because he is disidentified with the body, and is culturing the understanding that he is a soul, he is unaffected by all these things. He is equipoised. If he loses anything which is very dear to him, he does not lament. Because he has understanding of transient nature of this world, has understanding that this is temporary. I will share this example again. Let's say if you are going in a train from here, Mumbai to Pune. And unfortunately, you had to travel in a unreserved compartment. So invariably in unreserved compartment, you know how the booking happens. How the booking happens in unreserved compartment? By putting your handkerchief on the seat. And assuming you are sitting in an unreserved compartment, and you happen to go for a restroom or some, and somebody comes, you come back and you know, you find one very hefty, strongly built person sitting on your seat, who has occupied your seat. The seat which, you know, was your seat. You were sitting in that seat. You just went for two minutes. And imagine there is some kind of altercation, fight happening. No, this is my seat. You get up. No, I was sitting in this. This is an unreserved seat. How can you say it's my seat? And some kind of fight happens. So wise man, knowing that it's a short journey, very soon I am going to, the train is going to reach Pune. Anyway, okay, I will sit. If we do not have that context, a person can get into fist fight and even, end up beating that person. I want to sit that. He is attached to that seat, without having understanding that another couple of hours he will be in Pune.

**Key Quote:** "Whatever happens is happening as per the will of the Lord for our good."
**Summary:** The speaker shares the story of a king and his minister to illustrate the concept that whatever happens is according to the Lord's will and for our good, even if we don't understand it at the time. The story highlights how seemingly negative events can ultimately lead to positive outcomes.

So because a devotee has that context, that this life is temporary, with this understanding, he will do his duty. Again, we have to avoid two extremes. One extreme is that, you know, somebody is trying to take your property and says, anyway it's temporary. Take it. He will do his duty. He will try to protect to the best of his ability. But he does not loses his sleep over such things. So there was a king. He was a devotee of the Lord. And he also had a so called minister. Both of them were somewhat godly. Somewhat god conscious. And this minister per say, always said that whatever is happening is happening as per the will of the Lord for our good. Whereas the king, again maybe because of different levels of consciousness, was not always agreeing to what his minister was saying. So one day this king went to the forest for hunting. And on his way back, fell from his horse. And he got injured badly. And his hand got injured badly. And as a result, his finger got amputated. So when this happened, the minister who was with him, you know this king was in great pain. I said, why did the Lord do this to me? So the minister said, whatever happens is for our good. The Lord has a higher plan. So the king became little angry. What is the higher plan you are talking about? My finger has got cut and my hand has got injured badly. What is the higher plan in this? So he got so wild at the minister that he said, take him away.

#### divine plan, devotion, acceptance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Zp1jWkwYFo?t=1601
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "A pure devotee of the Lord always have the understanding that whatever is happening to me, there is a higher plan of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes the story of the king and his minister, emphasizing that a pure devotee understands that whatever happens is part of the Lord's higher plan. Even in apparent difficulty, they believe they deserve more but receive a token reaction, leading them to engage in the Lord's service with heart, mind, and words.

And got him captured, got him imprisoned, sent him behind the bars. Few days later, again when the king had gone on hunting, he was captured by some dacoits. In fact some kind of a tribal people. And they captured him and took him to a place where they were offering a sacrifice. And as part of the sacrifice, they had to offer a human being. Narabali. And while that offering was on, they had captured the king and they were about to offer, about to chop him off. Narabali. One of them pointed, wait, wait, wait, wait, we cannot offer this king. And the reason being that he is not complete. He does not have a finger. So when they examined him, they actually found out that yes, finger was missing and they somehow said that he is not the right person to be offered in sacrifice. They released him and he was sent back. Then the king was reflecting back, on his way back, actually, if my finger had not got cut, I would have today lost my life. So what my minister was saying actually was right. Whatever happens, the Lord is doing for our good. There is a higher plan. We may not be able to understand. So he went back to the minister and somehow, you know, asked forgiveness and got him released. Now I understand why you were saying that Lord had a higher plan. It is for our good. But one thing I am not able to understand that you innocently, for no fault of yours, although you were telling the truth, you got imprisoned. And you were, and you were in the prison. How can you say that it is a higher plan of the Lord? So to this the minister said, had I not got imprisoned and if I were accompanying you and if we both had got captured and if they figured out that you are not complete for the Bali, I would have got sacrificed. So a pure devotee of the Lord always have the understanding that whatever is happening to me, there is a higher plan of the Lord. We always, otherwise if we are not a devotee, we always, why did the Lord do this to me? Why me? Why I have to go through all this situation? A pure devotee of the Lord, even if he goes through apparent difficulty, he thinks that actually I deserve a lot more. But the Lord has given me a token reaction. For all what I have done, I should be suffering much much more. But what the Lord has given is a small token reaction. So he doesn't rebel. He is always Hriddha Vaag Vapubhir Viddaan Namaste. With his heart, with his mind, with his words, engages in the service of the Lord, glorifies the Lord, thanks the Lord. Jeeve Taayo, one who lives with this consciousness, Mukti Pade Sadaya Vaag, he is the rightful here for liberation. If he does not get what he desires, he is not distressed. He has the understanding there is a higher plan of the Lord.

#### transcendence, equanimity, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Zp1jWkwYFo?t=1723
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If we seek fame, invariably we will also have to go through infamy."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that a devotee is transcendental to auspicious and inauspicious activities, accepting risks for the Lord's satisfaction. He emphasizes equanimity in the face of praise and blame, happiness and distress, understanding that these are two sides of the same coin.

He is transcendental in the face of all kinds of auspicious, inauspicious and sinful activities. In the Gita, Krishna talks about karma, auspicious, pious karma, then Punya karma, then Vikarma, impious activities, or inauspicious activities and then he talks about Akarma. He is prepared to accept all kinds of risk for the satisfaction of the Supreme Lord. We have examples of great devotees who have, again we cannot imitate them, for the service of the Lord, they have even at times broken some moral codes. Although it is not recommended, we cannot artificially imitate. There were Aalwas who used to even steal one specific Aalwa with the sole intention of building a temple for the Lord. We have to discuss that in a context. One who is equal to friends and enemies, who is equipoised in honor and dishonor, heat and cold, happiness and distress, is equal to infame and infamy, is very dear to me. See what happens is we all seeking honor and if somebody blasphemes us, speaks bad about us, we get so hurt. Happiness and distress, we are always seeking happiness and trying to ward off distress. A person who is a pure devotee of the Lord is equipoised. And Krishna is saying, such a person is dear to me. Sometimes one is praised and sometimes one is defamed. That is the nature of human society. These are two sides of a coin. If we seek fame, invariably we will also have to go through infamy.

#### materialism, spiritualism, perspective
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Zp1jWkwYFo?t=1861
**Verse References:** BG 2.69
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "For a materialist, a spiritualist is a crazy person. And for a spiritualist, a materialist is a crazy person."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts materialists and spiritualists, defining a materialist as someone who believes matter is all there is and a spiritualist as someone who believes in the soul and God. He points out that each group views the other as crazy, referencing Krishna's statement in the Gita that what is night for one is day for the other.

It doesn't come freely. Broadly speaking, there are two kinds of people, materialist and spiritualist. Now one end of the spectrum are pure hardcore materialists, the other end of the spectrum is pure spiritualists, pure devotees. And in between, as I said, there are many gradations. Materialists, the definition of a materialist, a pure materialist, hardcore materialist, can anyone help me, what is the definition, how do we define a materialist? This is Charvak philosophy. So, the materialist is a person who thinks that matter is all in all. There is no afterlife, there is no soul, there is no God. You don't have to be accountable for your actions. Eat, drink, be merry and enjoy life. And a spiritualist is a person who thinks that I am a spirit soul, I am amsa of God, and I am eternal. Now interesting thing is, for a materialist, a spiritualist is a crazy person. And for a spiritualist, a materialist is a crazy person. That's why in the Gita, Krishna says, what is night for a materialist, is a day for a spiritualist. And what is day for a spiritualist, is night for a materialist. Many of you would have seen, some of you have friends or acquaintance who are very materialistic, they may genuinely feel that you coming here, spending two hours is a waste of time. Yes or no? You are wasting your time here. And some of the things which they may be doing, playing some video games and getting completely engrossed, some things, they are wasting time. They may think you are crazy, you may think they are crazy.

#### sanity, insanity, perspective

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### WHO IS MOST PERFECT AS PER GOD? | Bhagavad Gita 12.01- 12.05 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### devotion, spiritual paths, perfection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7buqG0G64tI?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "According to him, who is the perfect one?"
**Summary:** The lecture begins by introducing the topic of who is considered most perfect according to God, as explained in the Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 12, verses 1-5, based on the teachings of Srila Prabhupada.

The topic for these five verses is who is most perfect as per God. Who is doing the right thing in terms of this aspiration for spiritual growth? There are so many kinds of spiritualists, there are so many paths even in our scriptures, and we have so many religions. So here Sri Bhagavan, our Krishna himself, is going to say according to him, who is the perfect one. So based on the teachings of Srila Prabhupada, we are going to cover these verses. 12th chapter, text number one.

#### personalism, impersonalism, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7buqG0G64tI?t=51
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Personalists are those who believe that God is a person, impersonalists are those who believe that God is some power or energy but not a person."
**Summary:** Arjuna's question about the superiority of devotional service versus worship of the impersonal Brahman is presented, distinguishing between personalists who believe in a personal God and impersonalists who believe in a formless, energetic God.

Arjuna inquired, "Which is considered to be more perfect: those who are properly engaged in your devotional service, or those who worship the impersonal Brahman, the unmanifested?" There are some people, some transcendentalists, some spiritualists who are engaged in worshiping the Lord, serving the Lord, like for example, hearing about him, chanting his names, visiting the temple, making some offerings for the Lord, could be some eatable, could be a garland, could be so many different things. And there are some who are just meditating on something void. They believe that, yes, Lord is there, there is some supreme power, there is some supreme being, who that is, that clarity is not there, but they are more focused on meditating, controlling the senses, controlling the mind. Broadly speaking, transcendentalists can be divided in two categories. One are called the personalists. Can anyone help me with the second word, the second category? Impersonalists. Personalists are those who believe that God is a person. Impersonalists are those who believe that God is some power or energy but not a person.

---
#### personalism, impersonalism, Brahman
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7buqG0G64tI?t=154
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Brahman is all-pervading impersonal aspect of the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the concepts of personalism and impersonalism, defining Brahman as the all-pervading impersonal aspect of the Supreme Lord, using the analogy of sunshine and the sun to illustrate the partial understanding of the Supreme through its energy.

Those who worship the Supreme Lord considering him as a person directly by devotional service are called personalists. Just like you and me are a person, we have feelings, we have emotions, we have love, we have relationships, likewise, Lord is considered a person who also has feelings, who also has love, emotions, relationships. And those who engage themselves in meditation of impersonal Brahman are called impersonalists. So broadly speaking, two categories of transcendentalists. Now the question is who is better, which path, whether we meditate on impersonal Brahman. What is impersonal Brahman? What is Brahman? We covered in one of the sessions, what is Brahman? Brahman is all-pervading impersonal aspect of the Supreme Lord, just like sunshine is the impersonal, all-pervading aspect of the sun. Sun and then sunshine is pervading all over when sunshine penetrates into your room through the window, has come. But actually sun has not come. Sun's energy has come. So there are some who believe in God as some powerful force, some divine force, some energy. It's energy of that Supreme Lord, but that's a partial understanding of the Supreme. Just like to perceive sunshine, sunshine has no existence without sun. So now here we will clarify the distinction between the impersonal and personal conceptions of absolute truth. So both are conceptions.

#### understanding, analogy, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7buqG0G64tI?t=291
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "All of them are talking about train, but their understanding of train, one of them has a complete understanding, the other one has a partial understanding."
**Summary:** An analogy of three villagers encountering a train is used to illustrate different levels of understanding the Supreme, with one having a complete understanding and others having partial understandings.

Now this understanding that Lord is Supreme Brahman is not wrong. I gave this example, I think some of you have heard it. Three villagers, this was the time when the trains were very newly introduced way back in the 20th century, the beginning of. So these three villagers were inquisitive to understand more about how the train looks like, how does it function. So they all started off from the village together. They had some faint idea and one of them happened to hear the train coming from a far of distance. Chuk! Chuk! Chuk! Chuk! Chuk! Chuk! Chuk! Chuk! And a beam of light on a track. He rushed back, he wanted to be the first one to go and tell the villagers that I have seen the train. So they were very inquisitive. They asked, what does train look like? So he says, it's a beam of light very focused on a track and it makes noise like Chuk! Chuk! Chuk! Chuk! So nothing wrong. This is one conception of the train. The second one was little more patient. He saw the train had many, many bogeys, many, many, sorry, engine bogeys and then it was moving on a track, it was making the sound what the first person heard and also it had a beam of light. So he rushed back and then he gave his version of what the train looks like. And the third person was the most patient, he waited for the train to come to the station, it halted, he saw actually bogeys where people are coming down, people are going up, there are compartments, there are places where people can keep their luggage, can go and sleep, the driver is there. So he came and explained. So all of them are talking about train, but their understanding of train, one of them has a complete understanding, the other one has a partial understanding.

So now here it says, the blessed Lord said, he whose mind is fixed on my personal form, Sri Bhagavan uvaca, always engaged in worshipping me with great transcendental faith is considered by me to be most perfect. I repeat, the Lord said, he whose mind is fixed on my personal form, always engaged in worshipping me with great and transcendental faith is considered by me to be most perfect. I repeat, the Lord said, he whose mind is fixed on my personal form, always engaged in worshipping me with great and transcendental faith is considered by me to be most perfect. So here it is said, who concentrates on his personal form and worships him with faith and devotion. Now, a pure devotee, understanding that Lord is a person, he is engaged in all his senses, his mind, in the service of the Lord.

**Summary:** The discussion shifts to the root cause of suffering in the material world, clarifying that it is not desire itself but material or lusty desires that cause misery, contrasting this with the Buddhist concept of Nirvana.

Now, can anyone tell me what is the root cause of our suffering in this material world? So, it says the root cause of our material existence, of all the miseries we encounter in our life is desire. Is this a right statement? Is this statement right? The root cause of all our suffering is desires. Is this right statement? How many of you say yes it is right? How many of you say it is wrong? How many of us say I abstain from making a decision? Okay, so the answer is the root cause of our suffering is not desires. Buddha said this. The root cause of our misery is desires and Nirvana means we annihilate all desires, become desire-less. Is it possible to be desire-less? So, the root cause of our misery is not desire, it is material desire or lusty desires. Okay.

#### material desire, spiritual desire, sense gratification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7buqG0G64tI?t=638
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Desire to satisfy our own body and mind is material desire."
**Summary:** The speaker differentiates between material and spiritual desires, defining material desire as the desire to satisfy one's own body and mind, and spiritual desire as the desire to satisfy the spiritual master and Krishna, emphasizing that the intent behind actions determines whether a desire is material or spiritual.

Now desires broadly speaking are two categories, one is material desires, other one is spiritual desires. Now what is material desire, can anybody find what is material desires? What is material desire? Root cause of all our misery is material desire. What is material desire? Whatever takes us away from the Lord is material desire. Okay. Desire to satisfy our own body and mind is material desire. It could take different forms and shapes but broadly speaking desire to satisfy our own body and mind, it could be extension of the body, my relatives, my family, my country, my society all is material desire. And what is spiritual desire? Desire to satisfy the spiritual master and Krishna or the Lord is spiritual desire. Now this material desires also are of two categories, so first we had desire, desire has two categories, what are the two categories? Material and spiritual. Material means? Desire means? The desire to satisfy the Lord. Desire that I live in a palatial house, it's a material desire. To live in a palatial house is always a material desire, yes or no? If it is for your own sense gratification, feel good for your senses and mind, it is material desire. If you live in a palatial house with the intent of keeping Lord in the center and Lord being the master of that house and you being the servant, it is not a material desire. There were great devotees who were living in palaces, Raja Rishis. The goal is not that we have to live in a thatched house and that too only is good for our spiritual growth. The desire that Lord is to be glorified, the Lord has to be satisfied, the Lord has to be served.

#### material desire, spiritual desire, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7buqG0G64tI?t=796
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "You can be living in a broken hut and be full of material desires."
**Summary:** The lecture continues by explaining that one can live in a palace and cultivate spiritual desires, or live in a hut and be full of material desires. Prematurely giving up family and going to the forest without giving up the desire to lord over will not lead to spiritual growth. Material desires are categorized into good and bad, and the source of these desires is questioned.

The Lord has to be made the center is a spiritual desire. You can be living in a bungalow, in a palace and be cultivating spiritual desire. You can be living in a broken hut and be full of material desires. So Prabhupada said prematurely if we try to give up our family and go to a forest, if that desire to lord over, to enjoy is not given up, we can go underneath a tree and get attached to that tree and try to give up our family and go to a forest. If we try to give up a tree and get attached to that tree and try to do your good which has created a lot of problems for us, we will have to do those things and the truth is just that, all these are material desires developed for us to make ourselves comfortable. So material desires also are in two categories, good desire and bad desire. Desire to do good to others, which is philanthropic work and desire to do bad to others. Let say a murderer he also has a desire to do something, so it is a material desire which is, now how are desires generated, what is the source of these desires? Desires why some people have good desire some people have bad desire why some people have lot of material desire some people have lot of spiritual desires we also if you see we are bundles of desires now some of us may have 80% material desires 20% spiritual desires six days in a week material desire Sunday Gita life spiritual desire or you know 70 30 what determines what percentage of material or spiritual desires we will have and mind you desire is the root cause of our suffering and material existence the reason why we have a material body is because of desire the reason they are souls getting 84 lakh species of life is because of desires.

#### desire, karma, association

Some soul who have a desire to I want to fly I like flying you get a body which is appropriate to fulfill that desire somebody likes to be in water the nature provides under the supervision of Lord karma not they even a trainer to get a body which is very conducive to fulfill that desire so desires are generated by the modes of nature. If we are in Tamoguna, we will be sending a lot of desires for name, fame, prosperity. If we are in Satuguna, we will have one kind of desires. And if we are transcendentally situated, we will have spiritual desires. And another thing which helps generate different desires is the company we keep. Sangat Sanjayate Kama. If we associate with a hard core materialist, we will be motivated materially. We will develop that kind of desires. If you associate with a person who is very very pure, spiritually surcharged, full of spiritual desire, full of spiritual thoughts, you associate with him, you will also develop those desires. So here we are coming, we associate with the words of Krishna. We associate with the words of Prabhupada. We hear these things. We are cultivating some spiritual desires.

#### purification, spiritual progress, desirelessness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7buqG0G64tI?t=1048
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The real purpose of human form of life is to purify those desires."
**Summary:** The real goal of human life is to purify desires, moving from material to spiritual, with the ultimate goal being Anya Abhilashita Shunya, a state of pure spiritual desire, not desirelessness.

Now goal of human form of life, real advancement, real progress is not how much money you have, is not the size of bungalow you live in, because these all are temporary. In one life you have it, next life you may not have it. It is not your permanent credit. The degrees, bank balance, money, the body we have, the health we have, it's all temporary from the perspective of soul. So the real purpose of human form of life is to purify those desires. And the ultimate goal is Anya Abhilashita Shunya, Shunya means zero, Anya, Anya means others, Abhilashita, in other words all spiritual desires. So when we start on this journey, we are 100% material desire, 0% spiritual desire. And as we tread this path, Sangat, Sanjayate, Kama, we do more of satsang then it becomes 90-10, 80-20, like that. And ultimate goal, the perfection is, who is perfect? As per the Lord, is 100% engaged, 100% spiritual desire, Anya Abhilashita Shunya. The goal is not to become desire-less, the goal is not to become a stone, stone is desire-less, inert.

So 1933 when Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur came to Allahabad, somebody you have, a known person who is you know very favourable. And you know who has helped you a lot. So it is the desire that you know you go and introduce that person to your higher ups. So Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur came to Allahabad and these devotees invited Abhay Charan who is Prabhupada, his name was Abhay Charan to come and meet Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur. So when Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur was introduced Abhay Charan, so he told, you know this gentleman, I know this gentleman, he has met him when, 1922, he says I have noted him, he hears very attentively, he does not go away, Srila Prabhupada later says, my only qualification is I heard him attentively. I never asked any questions. In fact the only question I asked him, how can I serve you?

Now modern education if you see, earlier time the Vedic education was moving us in the direction of spiritual elevation, purification. That's why the first 25 years the learning was how to control the mind, how to control the senses. Which is that ashram called? Brahmacharya Ashram. And then knowing that we have unbridled desires, so many material desires, a person enters into Grahasta Ashrama, there is a license to fulfill the desires, but in a regulated way. Because unregulated desires, fulfillment is animal life. Dharmavihina Pashubhisamana. So after that training a person enters Grahasta Ashrama, where he has a license to fulfill material desires, or quote unquote lusty desires, but in a regulated way. Within the framework of Dharma.

#### impersonal worship, knowledge, material nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7buqG0G64tI?t=1804
**Verse References:** BG 16
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This material existence is a wonderful place designed by the Lord, which is teaching us lot of things."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Krishna from the Gita, stating that after many lives, a person in knowledge comes to Him, and explains that material existence is designed by the Lord to teach us through consequences, like violating laws and being penalized.

But those who fully worship the unmanifested, that which lies beyond the perception of the senses, the all-pervading, just like sunshine is all-pervading, inconceivable, fixed and immovable, the impersonal conception of the absolute truth, by controlling the various senses and being equally disposed to everyone, such person engaged in the welfare of all, at last, achieve me. Krishna in the Gita, there's a verse, later on, in the 16th chapter where Krishna says, bahunam janmanam ante, after many, many lives, gyanvaan, a person who is in knowledge, what happens to him? Maam prapadyante, will come to me. Vasudevam sarvam iti samahatma dulabha That Atma which has come to the realization or the understanding that Vasudevam sarvam iti samahatma, that Atma is very, very dulabha, very, very rare. So what happens is this material existence is a wonderful place designed by the Lord, which is teaching us lot of things. We do the wrong thing, and we get a slap. We want to be happy, but we try happy being the wrong way, not only we'll be happy, we'll get a slap. Just like you have, you try to violate a law, you'll be penalized. You try to violate a traffic signal, you'll be penalized. So that you don't violate. So that is how the material nature is designed.

---
#### experience, knowledge, realization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7buqG0G64tI?t=1901
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "After many many lifetimes of experience, a person who is sufficiently experienced, what will he conclude? Vasudevam sarvam iti."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that through repeated experiences and consequences in material life, one gains knowledge and eventually concludes Vasudevam sarvam iti, even if not engaged in the personal aspect of the Supreme Lord.

Just like a child touches fire, knowingly, unknowingly it will burn. So and when we learn, suppose I violate a traffic law, I'm penalized, I go through that experience and I learn. What do I learn? I should not be violating, otherwise I'll be penalized. So we are getting more and more experienced, what will make me happy? What will not make me happy? Before marriage we have idea, what will make me happy? After marriage we realize, what will not make me happy? So bahu naam janma naam antya, after many many years, many many lifetimes of experience, gyanvaan, a person who is sufficiently experienced, what will he conclude? Vasudevam sarvam iti. Even if he is not engaged in the personal aspect of the Supreme Lord, controlling the senses, engaged in welfare of others, equally disposed, but he is worshipping the impersonal aspect, after many many lives, he'll come to the conclusion, Vasudevam sarvam iti.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### WHO IS PULLING MY STRINGS? | HG Amitasana Das | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### free will, control, choice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=56
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Are we being controlled, who is controlling us, and through what?"
**Summary:** Introduction to the topic of who is pulling our strings, exploring the feeling of being controlled and not completely free to make choices.

The topic is, who is pulling my strings? Do we have some kind of a feeling that we are being controlled by someone? We are not completely free to do whatever we like doing. We are not free to make the choices which we intend to make. Many times we know that I should not be doing this, but still I end up doing it. Many times we set up an alarm, I will get up at 4 a.m. in the morning, or whatever time we decide, and then there's some force which keeps us in the bed, and we keep pressing the snooze button. So what is controlling us? Why are we not free to make the choices which we have thought that we should be making? So today's session is all about that. Are we being controlled, who is controlling us, and through what?

---
#### control, desire, regret
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=309
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Much against our wish, we end up doing things which we later on regret."
**Summary:** Analogy of a magnetic needle being controlled by a magnetic field, and Arjuna's question to Krishna about the force that makes us do things against our wish.

You take a magnetic needle. If you put that needle in a magnetic field which is emanating from magnets, that needle helplessly gets aligned according to the field. In other words, the needle is being controlled by the field. So is there something which is controlling us? Is there some force which is binding us, which is forcing us at times to do wrong things? In fact, Arjuna asked this question to Lord Shri Krishna, why is it that sometimes much against our wish—all of us might have experience of this—much against our wish, we end up doing things which we later on regret. We know that we should not be doing. We know that I should not be eating these things. We have made resolutions, but still we end up doing it. So Arjuna asked Krishna, what is that force? Much against my wish, I end up doing something. What is that force?

---
#### variety, nature, gunas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=386
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "This disparity comes about because of a force, something which is acting on us, acting on our consciousness."
**Summary:** Introduction to the concept of gunas and their influence on our consciousness, explaining the variety in the world and differences in behavior.

So today we are going to discuss in detail what is that force, what is that energy, just like the magnetic field which makes the needle loses its individuality and helplessly gets aligned according to the field. What is that force? So like we have been doing, we will cover three aspects. The first aspect is, please make a note of it, why so many varieties? We see around us people with different natures, different behavior, different temperaments, different attitude. Some people are very, very short-tempered, some people are very calm, composed, peaceful, contended. Why? Sometimes we see differences within a particular family itself. It's not that, okay, Indians behave in a particularly, African behaves in another way. That is not okay, but it depends. Within the same family, two children, they behave completely differently. Why? Same parents, same upbringing, same environment, but still the behavior is different. We see a lot of variety in this world. Some people are very cruel, some people are very kind-hearted, very gentle, very mild, very timid. Some people are very bold, outrageous. They don't mind hurting people. They pass sarcastic remarks recklessly without caring for others' feelings. There's a lot of variety. How does this variety come about? In fact, Krishna says that I am equal to everyone. We all are sons, part and parcels, but we see this lot of disparity. So this disparity comes about because of a force, something which is acting on us, acting on our consciousness.

---
#### gunas, maya, influence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=520
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We all are bound in this world with gunas, gunas are ropes, modes of material nature."
**Summary:** Explanation of gunas (sattva, raja, tamo) as ropes that bind us in this world, and how Maya acts on us through these gunas.

And this for Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita talks about gunas. Gunas means a rope, just like a rope. If you have to bind someone, we end up using a rope, although we can bind a person through various ways. And this rope has generally strands, and typically a rope is made of three strands which are intertwined, and that rope can in turn be further intertwined to make a thicker rope. And like house, you can have ropes of different thickness. So just like a rope is made of three strands, likewise we can consider that we all are bound in this world with gunas. Gunas are ropes, modes of material nature. Gunas are also called modes of material nature. This material energy, Maya, acts on us through these gunas. And we all know, I'm sure as Indians, we all have heard about sattva guna, raja guna, and tamo guna. Sattva guna is mode of goodness, raja guna is mode of passion, and tamo guna is mode of ignorance. So all of us are influenced by the modes, unlike a pure devotee. Should the sattva, he has transcended the modes. He has come out of the clutches of the modes, like Narada Muni, like Shila Prabhupada, like exalted acharyas. They are not under the grip of modes. We all are. We all are behaving differently, having different attitudes, having different temperament because of the modes influencing us, influencing us.

---
#### gunas, influence, analogy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=647
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Like four colors can create innumerable shades, likewise these three modes...mixed in different proportions...can bring about different consciousness."
**Summary:** Analogy of four colors in printing to explain how the three modes (sattva, raja, tamo) mixed in different proportions can create different consciousness.

I'm sure all of us are aware that when we do color printing, for color printing, we all see beautiful calendars, brochures, very colorful. Actually go to printing press, they only use four inks in the printing press to produce those prints. And if you use a magnifying glass and you see that picture, four colors in four color shirt printed photos chawords all those magnifying glass and you see that picture mathwa Lee us pig the color image, although it looks very seamless with lot of gradients, lot of colors, different shades, but actually if you use a magnifying glass, you can do that. If you have a magnifying glass, you put it on a top of picture, you will see many, many pixels. That's why we have this mega pixels. So pixels, pixels means a dot, and that dot of ink can be either of these four colors. It could be cyan, magenta, black, c m y k yellow. So these dots, depending on how closely they are placed and the thickness of the dots, it produces different color combinations, innumerable, just by these four colors. Just like here, right in front of us, we have example that four colors can produce innumerable shapes. If you go to a, you know, buy paint, you want to paint the house, the walls, these days you have innumerable options, different kinds of paints, different color shades, and you have an option of, you know, telling them, you can mix, they can mix the colors and right there give you a customized paint. So like four colors can create innumerable shades, likewise these three modes, sattva guna, raja guna, and tamoguna, mixed in different proportions, different combinations can bring about different consciousness.

#### consciousness, gunas, birth
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=773
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Depending on the modes influence...the soul takes birth in sad asad janma yonishu."
**Summary:** Explanation of how the influence of different modes on consciousness leads to birth in various species, including the four lakh species of human beings.

We talked about charasi laak yonyi, 84 lakh species, and in that we have four lakh as human beings. And we talked in our previous session, this four lakh is not based on nationality, based on the family in which we have created such a person, taken birth, based on our color, creed, caste. No, it's based on our consciousness. If depending on the modes influence, karanam guna sangasya sad asad janma yonishu. Karanam guna sangasya, guna sanga means association. When the consciousness is influenced by different modes, the soul takes birth in sad asad janma yonishu, janma birth, sad good birth, birth in animal species, different other species. In human beings also there are four lakh species gradations basis that one will get the birth, karanam guna sangasya sad asad janma yonishu.

#### gunas, symptoms, behavior
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=848
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Bhagavad Gita briefly gives us the symptom of a person who is predominantly influenced by sattva guna: he will be peaceful, he will be calm, he will be enlightened."
**Summary:** Description of the symptoms of a person predominantly influenced by each of the three gunas: sattva (peaceful, calm, enlightened), raja (passionate, greedy, hankering), and tamo (lazy, docile, inert).

So now this should be clear. Just like four colors can produce innumerable shades, these gunas, sattva guna, raja guna, tamo guna, in various combinations, permutation and combination, influencing our consciousness can bring about various temperaments. Now Bhagavad Gita briefly gives us the symptom of a person who is predominantly influenced by sattva guna. He will be peaceful, he will be calm, he will be enlightened, he will have knowledge, he'll have a sense of contentment, satisfaction. These are the symptoms of a person who's predominantly in the mode of sattva guna. A person who is predominantly in passion, raja guna, he will have intense hacking, passionate, wants to do things, wants to achieve things, want to show to the world. He's greedy, hankering, wanting to achieve more and more, never satisfied, want to show off, prove that he is great, looks for praise, looks for name, fame. These are the symptoms of a person who is predominantly in passion. And a person who is in ignorance or tamoguna, the symptoms are he'll be very lazy, very docile, he will be very inert, not feel like doing things. He would like, he would, given a chance, given an opportunity, he would like to sleep for 12 hours, no drive, no initiative, just wants to laze around, watch television, just sleep, just enjoy.

#### gunas, compatibility, chemistry
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=1059
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Just like if you take acid and take another acid and you mix them, there is no reaction...If you take an acidic solution and take an alkaline solution and mix them together, there will be a reaction."
**Summary:** Analogy of mixing acidic and alkaline solutions to explain why people with different gunas may not get along well, while those with similar gunas feel comfortable together.

Sometimes we can't, I am sure all of us have experienced, we can't gel with certain people. We generally use the term, uske saath meri chemistry match nahi hoti. I somehow don't like his company. I am sure every one of us have come across and some people we feel very comfortable with. We always like to be with such people and kehte hain ke you know chemistry match ho gayi hai, humari chemistry bahut achchi match hoti hai. The chemistry match is, I will give you an example. I will give you some insight based on what we are talking now, the modes. Just like if you take acid and take another acid and you mix them, there is no reaction. They just seamlessly mix. You take acid, take an alkali, alkaline solution, alkaline liquid and take another alkaline, two alkaline liquids, you mix them. There is not much of a reaction. If you take an acidic solution and take an alkaline solution and mix them together, there will be a reaction. So likewise, when a person who is predominantly influenced by Sattvaguna, he comes in the company of a person who is very tamasik, he will feel uncomfortable. There will be a reaction. There will be sparks. There will be fights. They will not get along well. Their chemistry will not match. They may not like each other.

---
#### gunas, compatibility, experience
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=1162
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Since we have to stay together, definitely over a period of time, we end up into some kind of, you know, collaboration or some kind of an understanding."
**Summary:** Personal story about sharing a room with someone who had different tastes in music, illustrating the challenges of living with someone with different gunas and how they eventually reached an understanding.

Imagine a person who likes getting up in the morning. He is predominantly influenced by Sattvaguna. He is very regulated. He is staying in the same room of a person who is very dirty. He wants to get up at 8 o'clock in the morning, sleeps at 1 o'clock in the night. He is very dirty. He gets up, does not take bath. He is very... And he does not feel anything wrong about it. When I was in the hostel, I had some friends who were very fond of rock music. You know, in music also, you have different types of music. You have heavy metal. You have heavy rock. And you have pop. You have various kinds of classical music. Different varieties of music are there and music lovers are there. So, there was a person who was staying in my room in the first year when I joined the hostel. And he was from Mumbai and fond of heavy metal. And again, to add to fire, he was very fond. He would not study. He could not study unless that music is on. Imagine a person studying. Something with heavy metal rock music. It is almost like screaming. And the person can very nicely study the music is on. And for me, it was diametrically opposite. If I have to study, it has to be quiet. It has to be pin drop silence. And forget about liking that music. I used to hate that music. And since we have to stay together, definitely over a period of time, we end up into some kind of, you know, collaboration or some kind of an understanding. Over a period of time, there were some sparks. There were some fights. If not physical, at least verbal fights. And later on, we reconciled and we worked out something. Okay, you cannot play from this time to this time. After 9 pm, strict no-no. Or when I am out during this time, you can play. Something we worked out and it worked out.

---
#### gunas, association, preference
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=1293
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Birds of same feather flock together...You will never find crows and swans together."
**Summary:** Explanation of how people are drawn to others with similar gunas, using the analogy of crows and swans to illustrate the difference between those in tamo guna and sattva guna.

So, just like we have, you know, people loving heavy metal, heavy rock. And sometimes people loving classical music. Some people like, you know, disco songs with heavy lights, heavy music. And some people like classical dance. Different kinds of people are there. It is because of the modes influencing. There is a saying, birds of same feather flock together. Prabhupada used to say, you will never know. You will never find crows and swans together. Generally, you will find crows where there is filth, garbage. There is a dead corpse. Something has died. All the crows will, you know, try to hover around that place. And it is a place of pilgrimage for them. If they see a dead corpse rotting, the crows will come and, you know, try to have a feast out of that dead corpse. Whereas swans who stay in crystal crows, stay in crystal clear water with lotuses and lilies, they will never be in company of crows. So, birds of same feather flock together. This is an English saying. So, likewise, people who are in sattva guna, they will be very comfortable with people with similar mindset, with similar temperament, with similar attitudes, talking the same things. And people who are tamasik, they will be comfortable with each other.

#### devotion, elevation, association
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=1387
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "At times we feel that we are not getting along with our previous friends...we are not able to resonate with."
**Summary:** Explanation of how devotees elevate from lower modes to higher modes and may find it difficult to relate to their previous friends, sharing a personal experience of a college reunion.

So, generally when we become devotees, we elevate from the lower modes to the higher modes, from passion to, you know, sattva guna. And then even we transcend to shuddha sattva. So, at times we feel that we are not getting along with our previous friends. The kind of talks, kind of words they are using, the kind of discussions they are having, we are not able to resonate with. So, we had a college reunion. I passed out from NIT Kurukshetra and we had a reunion, 20 year in, about few years back. And I had gone there, some friend of mine insisted. I told him, see, I will not be, I know people will be able to gel with me because again, now I am in a different dress, completely different, quote unquote, work what I am doing compared to what they are into. So, anyway he insisted and I went. I met all my previous friends and somehow they were quite accommodative. They knew that I am in ISKCON and I am a full time missionary devotee.

#### modes, preferences, food
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=1478
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "If a person is influenced by Sattva Guna, he will invariably like to eat fresh fruits, vegetables, salads...Person who is in Raja Guna, he likes spicy food...And a person who is in Tama Guna, he will enjoy filthy foods."
**Summary:** Explanation of how the modes of material nature influence our preferences, particularly in food, with examples of what people in sattva, raja, and tamo guna typically enjoy eating.

So, this is the reason that how, when modes of material nature influence us, we tend to have certain preferences, certain behaviors, certain attitudes, certain likings and it influences us. If a person is influenced by Sattva Guna, he will invariably like to eat fresh fruits, vegetables, salads, not something which is very fried and very, you know, spicy. Person who is in Raja Guna, he likes spicy food. He likes fast food. He will like fried foods, canned foods, tinned foods. And a person who is in Tama Guna, he will, he will enjoy, he will enjoy, in all, filthy foods. Decomposed. I talked about Nafi in Burma.

#### food, gunas, influence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=1558
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "This is the influence of modes, all of us without an exception are being influenced by the modes of material nature."
**Summary:** Story about Prabhupada's visit to Burma and how the neighbors were agitated by the smell of ghee, illustrating how the modes influence our perception and preferences.

But for them it's a delicacy. So Prabhupada said when he went to Burma, so one time devotees had arranged, it was a festival and, you know, just like in a festival we have feast, so we cook lot of nice puris, kachoris and halwa and rice and different sabjis offered to the Lord and celebrate the festival. A feast is part of the festival. So devotees had arranged a big festival, a big feast and they were, they were frying puris, puris in pure ghee and pure ghee for people, for many people it is very very nice, very tasty and very pleasant in smell, pure ghee. It so happened that when the devotees were frying the puris in pure ghee, all the neighbours, you know, they started getting agitated, irritated, what this foul smell is coming now? They have never smelled something so obnoxious like this and they, some of them even, you know, came and said, what is happening? What are you doing? It is smelling so bad, actually it was the smell of a ghee. Now imagine a person who likes nafi, for him ghee is very obnoxious, for a person who likes the smell of a ghee, nafi is unthinkable. You cannot even go near it, forget about tasting it or forget about drinking it. So this is the influence of modes, all of us without an exception are being influenced by the modes of material nature and the permutation and combination of these modes influencing us defines our temperament, defines our behaviour, defines our attitude at different times of the day, different days of the year and different years of our lifetime.

#### progress, change, material world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=1672

**Content Type:** philosophy

**Summary:** Explanation of how our behavior and temperament change as we progress and advance in sattva guna, and how we have fallen into this material world.

It keeps changing. If you are progressing, we advance in sattva guna and our behaviour, temperament changes. So, Prabhupada also gives, just like sparks of fire, when they, when they fall off from the fire, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu in Sishtashtra says, Oh Lord, somehow, I have fallen into this material world. Kin karam, although I am your servant, I am your part and parcel, but I have fallen into this material world. Vishwame, Vishwame means this material, ocean of material existence. Bhavam buddho bhava. Bhavam buddhi, the ocean of birth and death. So, we have fallen into this.

#### gunas, analogy, influence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=1733
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This dry grass is like sattva guna...This green grass is like raja guna and this water is like tamo guna."
**Summary:** Analogy of a spark falling on dry grass, green grass, and water to explain how different gunas influence our ability to ignite our original spiritual qualities.

Now, just like a spark of a fire, if it falls away from that main fire, sparks, you know, sometimes that fire is there, sparks come out of that fire. The sparks can fall on dry grass. Although it has fallen from the main fire, if it falls on the dry grass, there is a good possibility that the dry grass gets ignited and that spark becomes a fire. The same spark can also fall on green grass, normal grass and in due course of time, that spark will get extinguished, loses its potency. The spark, if it falls on water, it will completely get extinguished. So, this dry grass is like sattva guna. This green grass is like raja guna and this water is like tamo guna.

---
#### gunas, qualities, ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=1803
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When we are under the influence of sattva guna...our original quality can easily get ignited."
**Summary:** Explanation of how sattva guna allows our original spiritual qualities to be ignited, while raja guna leads to those qualities being doused, and tamo guna results in complete forgetfulness of our true identity.

When we are under the influence of sattva guna, like the dry grass, our original quality can easily get ignited. Original quality of sat, shith, ananda, enlightenment, self-realization, understanding who I am can easily be aroused by the right association, by some practices, by some things which acharyas have recommended. If we do those things, if we abide by their instructions, it's much easier. If a person is in raja guna, then it is relatively difficult and in due course of time, his fiery qualities, his sat, shith, ananda, the knowledge about who he is, what's the purpose of life, who God is, what's my relationship with God gets easily doused, finished. And a person in tamo guna is like the spark falling on water. He is completely forgetful of his true identity. He is completely in ignorance. That's why tamo guna is also called the mode of ignorance. He is ignorant of his true position. He is ignorant of the laws operating in this world. He is ignorant of why there is disparity in this world. What is the purpose of life? What he should be doing? What he should not be doing? What he should be eating? What he should not be eating? Completely oblivious of these things.

---
#### gunas, bondage, goal of life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=1897
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Although Satva guna is better than Raja guna and Tamo guna, but still it binds us."
**Summary:** Explanation that all three gunas bind us, even sattva guna, and the goal of life is not to remain in sattva guna but to transcend it to shuddha sattva.

So, these are the three modes which is influencing us. Every one of us are under the grip of these modes. Although, although Satva guna is better than Raja guna and Tamo guna, but still it binds us. The example is given. Satva guna is compared to a golden chain. Raja guna may be compared to a silver chain and Tamo guna may be to an iron chain. If you see the value of the chains, it is quite different. golden chain is quite valuable it is very precious compared to an iron chain but the function of a chain if the chain is used to bind a person they all bind the person if a person is bound by golden chain he is as bound as a person who is bound by an iron chain so in that sense all these modes all those getting is better than Rajya Guna and Tamo guna still we are bound still we are entangled still we are in this material world still we are in the clutches of repeated birth and death. so goal of life is not to be in Satan
