#### passion, senses, enjoyment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/47lhlQ1UMMs?t=3626
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "For a person in passion, he thinks the goal of life is eat, drink, be merry, enjoy life."
**Summary:** Description of the mode of passion, characterized by intense attraction, inability to control senses, and hankering for material enjoyment.

The mode of passion is characterized by intense attraction between man and woman. Woman has attraction for man and man has attraction for woman. We will be unable to control our senses, we will be unable to control our sensual urges, urges of mind, innumerable desires. This is called the mode of passion. And when the mode of passion is increased, one develops the hankering for material enjoyment. He wants to enjoy sense gratification. His senses start dictating him. The urges of senses become very prominent. In fact, he does not even have knowledge that there is something wrong with gratifying the senses, unlimitedly. He thinks that's the goal of life. For a person in passion, he thinks the goal of life is eat, drink, be merry, enjoy life.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### WHY DONATE? AND TO WHOM? - CASE STUDY | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### donations, religion, seva
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/to0qk6Fj8A8?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The right understanding is 10% of everything, including my time."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the concept of donations in various religions, using the example of tithing in Christianity and a conversation with a Christian man about his religious practices.

So we have the concept of donations. In every religion, there is a concept where the people who are practicing that particular religion are encouraged to donate, to contribute, to do some seva. And many religions have that in an organized form. For example, in Christianity, what is it called? Tithing, yes. Where every devout Christian is encouraged, if he is part of the church attending the Sunday Mass, 10% of what he earns he gives to the church. In fact, I was speaking to one gentleman. I was traveling with him and I happened to strike a conversation with him. And then I asked him, have you visited our temple in Bangalore? So then he said, no, I'm a Christian. I haven't visited your temple. So then we continued our talk. And then he says, I go to the church. And then he reminded me of the Saint Bethel's Church, which is very close to our place. And then I said, you know, I started asking a few things about him, his religion, and what the Bible says. So then I was curious to know about this concept of tithing. So I asked him, is this a fact? All those who are very strongly associated with the church, do they really follow this? And is this true? So he said, yes, I'm a Christian. And then I had not visited the church. You are supposed to do that, 10% of what you earn, you are supposed to contribute to the church. He corrected me. In fact, he says it's not just 10% of what we earn. The right understanding is 10% of everything, including my time.

#### knowledge, surrender, inquiry
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/to0qk6Fj8A8?t=181
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When you approach a bona fide authorized spiritually evolved personality, first you surrender."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the process of gaining knowledge, emphasizing the importance of approaching a bona fide spiritual personality, surrendering, and inquiring submissively, even if the questions seem foolish.

If you have to gain an understanding of this knowledge, first you have to follow a process: pranipatina. What is the process? Pranipat. First you have to approach a bona fide spiritually evolved personality. Pranipat means, anyone can help me with the word pranipat? Surrender. When you pranipat, when you surrender to a very spiritually evolved person, then the second thing is pariprasna. Pariprasna means inquire submissively. There is nothing wrong in asking questions. It could be, you know, sometimes people are reluctant to ask a question, what will people think? It could be a foolish question. What will people think? There is nothing wrong about it. In fact, Arjuna also asked many questions. When Krishna says, I spoke to the sun god, we were one, then Arjuna asked an apparently foolish question, that you are my contemporary, we are contemporaries, how come you spoke to the sun god, we were one millions of years ago? It's almost like a foolish question because on one side he is seeing that we are contemporaries and Krishna is saying so Arjuna is asking that nothing wrong in asking even a foolish question.

#### seva, surrender, intelligence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/to0qk6Fj8A8?t=321
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Surrender with intelligence. We have got intelligence, we could discriminate."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the importance of rendering service (sevaya) and cautions against blindly surrendering to religious organizations, emphasizing the need to use intelligence and discrimination.

What is the third one? Sevaya, render service. Although yes, I agree, in present times many so-called religious organizations which are not bona fide have exploited their so-called followers, become very, very rich, very, very opulent, and at times we hear so and so scandal happened and so-called god man is behind bars. They all went with the right intention, maybe they surrendered, maybe they inquired submissively, they rendered service, but unfortunately they feel cheated. And as a result, it's a fact that people are very cautious. It's a fact that we have to be cautious. We don't have to surrender our intelligence. What is the right use? Surrender with intelligence. Krishna has given us intelligence. We should not lock up our discretion and discrimination and blindly surrender. Krishna is also not asking us to blindly surrender.

#### seva, money, dedication
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/to0qk6Fj8A8?t=452
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When we give a portion of the result of our work to the Lord, it's as good as giving that work to God."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the four categories of seva: prana (life), artha (money), dhiya (intelligence), and vacha (words), focusing on the significance of giving money as a form of service and how it connects one's work to God.

Okay, the four kinds of sevas are prana, means your life. You can serve the Lord with your life, dedicate your life for the mission of the Lord. There are personalities, no other work but to serve the Lord. Not everyone can do that. Even if someone wants to, the circumstances may not permit. Next comes arthayar. Arthayar means money. Service is important. Seva is important. Four ways we can serve the Lord: pranayama, arthayar. Arthayar means with the money. Why money? Not that God wants our money. God is not poor. In fact, the money what we have belongs to God. It is coming from God. It is God. He is the true proprietor. Arthayar means when we give money, because we are attached to money, all the work we do gets translated as a result in the form of money. We go to the office, we have our jobs, we work morning to evening, five days, six days a week. It translates into something. That's the result of our work. When we give a portion of the result of our work to the Lord, it's as good as giving that work to God. In mathematics, I've shared this equation many times. A is equal to B, B is equal to C. What is the final derivative? A is equal to C. Working in the office gets me some result. Some portion of the result I give it to the Lord, which means that some portion of what I have worked, proportionate to what I have given to the Lord, is actually God's work, immaterial of what I have done in the office. I could be tightening screws and nuts and bolts in automobile industry, but that work becomes God's work because the result of that work I have given it to God.

Her family is completely against Gita live, her attending Gita live sessions, and feels she is wasting her time going for the classes or chanting every day. There are so many good things to be done, so many other productive ways of utilizing the time, going and sitting and hearing that man who stands on the stage and does a lot of, there is a better way of using time. Amidst this resistance, she still manages to pursue her spiritual journey with all kinds of hurdles. See, somehow she is managing, she is coming, she is attending, she is wanting to chant more, read more, come more often. Ekta has been contributing 10% of her income every month towards HKM, she has been contributing. Her husband and in-laws are totally against the idea of donating.

#### charity, wealth, prudence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/to0qk6Fj8A8?t=841
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "How does my contribution which is going into feeding Prasadam to affluent people who are already obese, already into dieting, how does it help me?"
**Summary:** The speaker presents questions raised by Ekta's family regarding her donations, questioning the value of donating to a seemingly wealthy institution and feeding affluent people, and suggesting that giving to the poor would be more appropriate.

But the recipients, the beneficiaries are affluent people. When we give food to a beggar or probably a person who is underprivileged child, it makes sense to us that yes, the person does not have, that my contribution is making a difference. But how does my contribution which is going into feeding Prasadam to affluent people who are already obese, already into dieting, how does it help me? What kind of charity is that? Also why contribute towards an institution that is already cash rich? Prabhuji comes in a car. I saw the other day. He was using a laptop. I myself don't have, you know, some of the gadgets which Prabhuji is using. So why contribute to an institution which is already cash rich? Her family is not against donating for a cause but they feel it's not prudent in donating to HKM which is already cash rich.

#### abilities, caretaker, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/to0qk6Fj8A8?t=974
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Wise men understand that my abilities, my talents, my skill is coming from the Lord and it actually belongs to the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that all abilities and talents come from the Lord and ultimately belong to Him, emphasizing the concept of being a caretaker and detaching from the results of one's work.

So now Krishna says, matta sarvam. Everything what we see. Matta sarvam. Everything what we see, aham sarvasya prabhu, it is coming from me. Our abilities, our talent, our skill. Today somebody is intelligent, he gets 5 lakh rupees a salary per month, there are some people getting 5000 rupees a month. Did we consciously choose our intelligence? We have been blessed with that intelligence, we have been blessed with that ability, that's why that ability is fetching us that money in the market. If we have a market value. So, wise men understand that my abilities, my talents, my skill is coming from the Lord and it actually belongs to the Lord. I'm just a caretaker. It is not my ability, it is given to me and at any moment it can be withdrawn. So when that ability, understanding that it is coming from the Lord, that ability, the result of that work, the result of that ability, the rightful enjoyer is the Lord. Ideally everything should be given to the Lord because it is the Lord's, but yes, we need something for our sustenance, something for our living, something for our enjoyment, so we keep that and give that due result to the Lord. Many, many places in the Gita, Krishna says you have the right to do your duty, do not be attached to the results of your work because if we are attached and enjoy the result of the work with the understanding it is mine, then we get more and more entangled in this world.

#### mission, transparency, faith
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/to0qk6Fj8A8?t=1154
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When we serve the Lord, the Lord also provides ultimately he is the giver."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the missionary spirit behind the organization, highlighting its financial transparency and the belief that the Lord provides when one serves Him, using the example of the Akshaya Patra program.

This is being headed by missionaries. We are not here to make money. Believe me, many of us are from backgrounds where we could be earning lakhs of rupees in terms of this thing, but we are here for a cause and because there is a zeal, there is a mission that brings in the force. In fact, sometimes people wonder how is it that day after day when we started off Akshaya Patra program, it is the Lord's mercy, it's the Lord's blessings, it is not our doing. We strongly believe that when Akshaya Patra started with 1500 children and our chairman shared the vision that we are going to soon feed 30,000 children, I was the one who said don't do that, it will be a financial pressure on our institution, where will the money come? He said the Lord is the one who is doing this, he will provide. And today we are feeding 17 lakh children and we're going to very soon touch 25 lakhs. We are 33 kitchens are going on right now and not one day, believe me, sometimes and we have funds, if you see our balance sheet, we have funds which can support, we have good balance, but it can support the running of operation for next two months only, assuming the inflow stops. Assuming inflow stops, we can run only for next two months, but last 17 years not one day we have stopped the program because of paucity of funds. So when we serve the Lord, the Lord also provides ultimately he is the giver.

A very fundamental thing, the Lord is not poor. With that logic, the children of the Lord also cannot be poor. A rich father's son has to be rich. Krishna is shad aishwarya, unlimited strength, unlimited wealth, unlimited knowledge, unlimited fame, unlimited beauty, unlimited renunciation and we are his amshas. So then the question is if our father is so rich, thousands of Laxmi, we are looking for favor of Laxmi and Laxmi is serving eager to serve the Lord, if the Lord is so rich and we are his sons then why there is poverty and we want to elevate that poverty without understanding why there is poverty in the first place. If a son of a rich man is loitering in the street, it's an unnatural situation if you know that son of. So spirituality makes us understand although we may look for temporary quick fix solution just like when we have fever we can take paracetamol and get a temporary quick fix solution but why there is fever in the first place body is not designed to be unhealthy what is the root cause because of which I am getting fever again and again so the so-called anomalies what we see in the society poverty illiteracy corruption actually our scriptures point out is because of there is no shortage in this world the only shortage is of God consciousness.

Genuine God consciousness because we have rebelled against the Lord because we are acting whimsically without following the instructions of the Lord order of the Lord we are attracting misfortune in our life the misfortune what we see all around us is our own doing for having acted in defiance of the Lord's instructions and forgetfulness of Lord so the highest duty a person should who is orphan, struggling in the streets, not having food to eat, the highest duty is to reconnect him back to his father. #### karma, punya, akarma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/to0qk6Fj8A8?t=1667
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When we serve the Lord, that action has neither good reaction or not bad reaction in the material domain."
**Summary:** The speaker distinguishes between papa (sinful activities), punya (pious activities), and akarma (serving the Lord), explaining that serving the Lord transcends material reactions and elevates the soul spiritually.

By the way, contributing money for the mission of the Lord is not Punya. There are three kinds of activities. That's why I said I'll add my two bit of insights into what you're contributing. There are Papa. Papa means sinful activities. The result of sinful activities is suffering in this world. Then there is Paapam. Punya means pious activity. Opening an orphanage, feeding a poor underprivileged. The result of Punya or pious activity is good reaction because of which we enjoy in this material world. Janma, Aishwarya, Shrutashri. We get good birth, we get opulence, prosperity, Shrutah, good education. That's the result of Punya activities. But Papa or Punya has a reaction. And reaction, good or bad, in this material world, you have to take birth to enjoy or suffer the reactions. When we serve the Lord, that action has neither good reaction or not bad reaction in the material domain. It has the highest reaction in a spiritual domain which elevates the soul. So it is not Punya.

#### karma, punya, akarma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/to0qk6Fj8A8?t=1771
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It is much, much, much, much higher than Punya."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to distinguish between punya and akarma, explaining that punya leads to good births in the material world, while akarma leads to spiritual emancipation.

It is much, much, much, much higher than Punya. Punya means you will get good birth in this material world. You will get, you move from C-class prison house to A-class prison house. But still, Janma, Mrityu, Jara, Vyadi, death, old age, disease and birth is still there. Even for a rich man in America, living in you know, very palatial house with all comforts, all luxuries, still he grows old, still he dies. So, when we engage in spiritual activities, service of the Lord, that activity is called Akarma. Punya Karma, Papa Karma and Akarma. That does not give us a material reaction, it gives us a spiritual reaction. And that spiritual reaction is for the soul. Material reaction gives us good body, good intelligence in a material domain, good birth. Is that clear? So, it is not highest Punya, it is greatest, which gives us transcendental results for spiritual emancipation.

#### health, disease, soul
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/to0qk6Fj8A8?t=1946
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Because of soul is diseased, the soul has misused its free will."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the concept of three types of diseases: tanrog (disease of the body), manrog (disease of the mind), and bhavrog (disease of the soul), arguing that bhavrog is the fundamental disease that needs to be cured.

So opposite of health is disease. Now, we generally, when we talk about health, we are predominantly concerned with physical health. And we want to be healthy, and we try to, if there is some disease of the body, we try to get cured and get hospitalized and take a medicine and all that. That ill health, which we are trying to find a solution, is called tanrog. Tan, tan you understand? Tan means? Body. It's called tanrog. And then, not only body, in the healthy body you should have a healthy mind. You could have a healthy body, but the mind is not healthy. And that's called manrog. And even much deeper rog is bhavrog. What is bhavrog? Disease of? Disease of? The soul. Because of soul is diseased, the soul has misused its free will. Have rebelled against the Lord. Have defied the instructions of the Lord. We are in bhavrog, the cycle of birth and death. Punar api janma, punar api marna. And as a result, we have tanrog and manrog. So fundamental rog, which we have to get cured to be healthy is bhavrog.

---
#### charity, disease, bhavrog
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/to0qk6Fj8A8?t=2033
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "All material solutions can just give temporary relief. But the real relief to a real problem of Janma, Mritya, Ujara, Vyadi has no solution."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that while other forms of charity address tanrog and manrog, serving the Lord addresses bhavrog, the fundamental disease of the soul, and uses the example of a wealthy, bedridden man to illustrate the limitations of material solutions.

And what we are talking about may not solve tanrog, may not solve manrog, but it solves bhavrog. All other forms of charity, you open a hospital, it addresses tanrog. Somebody is in distress, you give him comfort, emotional support, manrog. But so long as he has bhavrog, tanrog and manrog will continue. Okay, you open a hospital, you give him medical care, 20 years he will be good. 25 years, he will be good. After 25 years, what happens? Again tanrog will not leave him. He will become old, eyes will get blurred, he'll not able to walk, he will be bed-ridden. Somebody has to attend to him, even for his basic evacuation. In Bangalore happened to visit a place he was in the 1930s when Britishers were ruling he was like a big Viceroy kind of a thing very very eminent Indian but eminent position in the British government and when we visited I can't remember I can't forget that scene completely bedridden he was about 100 years two nurses permanently attending to him doesn't remember anything his mind almost like a vegetable and when I saw him definitely couldn't recognize me on the bed two nurses attending to him it's going on It's Tanrog. Rich man living in a palatial house, RMV extension in Bangalore. Very scenic bungalow next to a lake. Anyone knows Bangalore? RMV extension, Raj Mahal, it's a very like, it's like you know, the place all rich eminent people stay there. Big bungalow next to this thing. That is the state. Can you help it? Can't. Medical science as a solution? No. Bhavrog, you're caught in a cycle of birth and death. It's inevitable. Just a question of time. You can postpone, you can just drag a bit. All material solutions can just give temporary relief. But the real relief to a real problem of Janma, Mritya, Ujara, Vyadi has no solution.

#### charity, intelligence, symptomatic relief
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/to0qk6Fj8A8?t=2198
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We should do charity with intelligence."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that addressing tanrog and manrog is also important, advocating for charity with intelligence and understanding the limitations of symptomatic relief, using the example of Akshaya Patra.

So, when we engage in service of the Lord, that gives us solution to Bhavrog, which is a fundamental rog. Bhavrog is the one where we have got separated from our rich father and we're loitering in this material world, suffering various kinds of miseries. So, the point here is, does that mean that we close our eyes to Tanrog and Manrog? Is that your point? Yeah, which is very true. We should not close, but we should do charity with intelligence. For example, Akshay Batra. You take that example. It is addressing actually the need of our underprivileged children. We are not going teaching Gita to that school. Knowing that children are not in a platform where they can understand this knowledge. Khaali ped mein kya sika ho gaya aap? Maslow's laws of human need. Pyramid. The fundamental need is food, clothing, shelter. Yes, if somebody does not have food, clothing, shelter, they will not be able to do it. But if they do not have food, clothing, shelter, they will not be able to do it. So yes, if somebody does not have that, provide that. But with the right understanding. If you're giving symptomatic relief, be very clear you're giving symptomatic relief. Don't be in illusion that it's going to permanently solve the problem. Find the real problem and attack that also, while you're giving symptomatic relief. That is the philosophy. So, we do address symptomatic symptoms also, but with the understanding of real problem. Is that clear?

**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/to0qk6Fj8A8?t=2409
**Verse References:** —

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of sacrifice in devotion, stating that unless one sacrifices what is dear to them, their devotion remains theoretical, and that Krishna values the sacrifice itself more than the amount given.

Otherwise, we say, I love God, my devotion to God, it's all lip service. Unless we sacrifice what is dear to us, what we are attached to us, it's all theory. It's all theory. We can do lip service. Yes, some, something is better than nothing. But if real, we have also seen, believe me, I'm not telling to cajole anyone indirectly to contribute. Those who have started contributing, they get more fixed up. Fixed up in the sense, they come more often, their taste in the Krishna consciousness continues. Some people come, they're very cautious, very this thing, I want to place it. Slowly, it remains theory. Swamiji toh bahut bolte hain, higher taste, this taste, nothing is there, yahaan pe toh, fizzle out. It is practical. Practical devotional service. If you want to strike a relationship with someone, some element of investment of time, energy and sacrifice is involved. And Krishna in the Gita's, just one minute. Krishna in the Gita says, he's, believe me, not interested in a wealth per se. He's interested in, are we willing to sacrifice what is dear to us for him? For a person who is earning 100 rupees and giving 10 rupees is more dear to Krishna, for a person who's earning a lakh of rupees and giving five thousand rupees. If I were to share with you what's going on in the heart of Krishna, that 10 rupees is more dearer to Krishna than five thousand rupees from a person who's a millionaire or billionaire. Because what he's saying is Bhavagrahi Janardana.

Everything actually belongs to Krishna. All our abilities, talents, intelligence is coming from Krishna. When we go to Ganges, Ganga ki Pooja karte hain, actually the right way of doing Ganga Pooja is we lift the water with the palms and offer it back to this thing. That's exactly what we are doing when we give money to the Lord. The Ganges water, we offer it back to Ganges. When we give the right consciousnesses, it is Lord's money and I am giving it back to this thing. And let me tell you one thing. This is my conviction. Every one of us will go from this world, I can be very sure, don't ask me how I can be sure. Every one of us will go from this world empty handed. Can I be sure? 100%? Kuch nahi leke jayenge. The flight is not booked. And anyway flights are getting delayed because of rains. Okay, so, we will go empty handed. Sab kuch chhod ke jayenge. Jitna humne Bhagawan ko de diya, that becomes our permanent credit. Wo hum leke jayenge. Wo atma ke saath aayega. Baki sab chhoot jayega yahan pe. Kitna bhi tijori mein aap baand kar ke rakh lo. Everything will be left. But what's gone from our hand for the service of the Lord that comes with us, with our soul, because it's a permanent credit. So the real thing is, Jitna ho sakhe, mein dedu. Anyway I have to leave everything. Jitna ho sakhe dedu, that becomes my permanent credit.

#### karma, death, childhood
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/x9oIHHhOdJs?t=702
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public

**Summary:** The speaker recounts a personal experience from his childhood where he witnessed the death of a young girl being crushed by a bus, highlighting the seemingly random and tragic nature of such events.

I remember when I was in school, maybe in fifth class, one day we were all, after our school, it's over, the bus used to come and we would all wait for the bus to arrive. And invariably we all were, as children we were all very playful, notorious. Invariably all of us would gun for the window seat. It was a long drive back home, about one hour drive. And we would gun for the window seat. Window seat is the seat which is next to the window. And when the bus would come, invariably we would climb the bus from the sides and try to put in some water bottle or a handkerchief to book the seat. We see even now, even adults doing it in unreserved compartment. So, one such time all of us flocked and we were trying to jump on the bus from the side and try to block the seats. And in that rush, in that commotion, one girl, she was, I think in the second standard, 3-4 years younger to me, got pushed and got crushed. First time I saw death so closely. Right in front of my eyes I saw the wheel passing on the head of that girl. Two year old girl. I still remember the name. The name was Neelam. And the tyre got passed on the head right in front of my eyes, got completely smashed. And there was no question of spot dead.

The story goes there was a Muni by name Manduka Muni and this Manduka Muni was living in a small hermitage in a simple hut simple dwelling and doing his regular morning puja's his tapasya his penances very God conscious living a very holy life and one day it so happened that some of the dacoits who had committed dacoity stolen lot of valuables were fleeing were running away and the police was trying to chase them and they somehow got into this particular village where Manduka Muni was staying and they were looking for a hideout where they could just hide from the eyesight of the police so they entered this hermitage this this dwelling of Manduka Muni and hid themselves Madhuka Muni tried inquiring but then he could understand that they are trying to flee and the police is chasing them and somehow allow them to be in their house the police identified that this is a particular house in which they have gone into he has gone in Pahlajęc. into the criminals, the dacoits and they surrounded that house and somehow entered it. And along with the dacoits, they also caught hold of Manduka Muni. Somehow they could not differentiate. They thought maybe this Muni is also part of that gang who had committed dacoity. So, the trial happened. To cut the story short, Manduka Muni was.. The magistrate ordered that he should be executed, be killed and he would be pierced with a lance in his rectum. There are different ways of killing a person. A person could be hanged. So, Manduka Muni was supposed to be pierced with a lance, some kind of a sword with a sharp pointed edge from his back and he would be killed.

That the laws of nature are very stringent, even as a child, even when we may be ignorant of a law. For example, let's say if you're driving on a road and you say that I did not know that I'm supposed to stop. I did not know that there's a law that you have to drive one side of the road. I did not know that you are supposed to wear a mask. I did not know that you're supposed to wear a helmet. So ignorance of law is not an excuse. #### omnipresence, karma, consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/x9oIHHhOdJs?t=1525
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "I went far and wide, I went deep in the forest, I went to the caves, but I could not find a place where no one was watching."
**Summary:** The speaker tells a story about a guru who gives his disciples a final test: to eat an apple in a place where no one is watching. One disciple returns having failed, because he could not escape the eyes of the Lord.

So the Guru gave them a final test. What was the final test? The test was, each one of them was given an apple and they were told that you have to go to a place where no one is watching you and you eat the apple and come back. So all the disciples were quite happy, excited. They expected a very hard test. But they were very happy and excited because the test was relatively very simple. All you have to do is find a place where no one is watching, eat the apple and come back. So in next one hour, most of them came back, almost all of them but one came back. Very excited, with a smile on the face, having passed the test, according to them, eaten the apple, made sure no one is watching and told the Guru that yes, we have done it. In fact, some of them said we passed the test. The judgement was done. But one person, one disciple, did not come back. It was late in the evening, everyone got worried what happened to him. Finally, finally, the person came back, very sullen face, very morose, with an apple in his hand and said, sorry, I am very sorry, I have failed the test. The Guru said, I am very sorry. I have failed the test. So Guru asked, why, what happened? He said, I went to all the places. I went far and wide, I went deep in the forest, I went to the caves, but I could not find a place where no one was watching. So one person, one of his colleagues, he immediately said, you should have come with me. You should have come with me. I had gone to a cave, pitch dark. Not one soul was watching there. You could have come with me. I could have taken you there. So this person said, I even went into that cave, what you are referring to, it was pitch dark, but I always saw a pair of eyes watching me. And those eyes were eyes of the Lord. I could not escape those eyes. And hence, I have come back with an apple in my hand. That I have failed the test, I could not eat the apple, could not find a place where no one is watching me.

#### omnipresence, witness, Supersoul

**Verse References:** SB 11.5.42
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "And super soul himself, are all witness to the activities of the living entity."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the sun, moon, fire, sky, air, demigods, and the Supersoul are all witnesses to our actions, emphasizing that nothing is done in secret.

So the Guru hugged this disciple and said, you have actually passed the test. You are the one who have realized the true imports of what I have been teaching all throughout during the training. So in the Bhagavatam, it is said. Bhagavatam. It is said that the sun, we see everyday sun rising, sun setting, the fire, the sky, the air, the demigods, different devatas, moon, daytime there is sun, nighttime there is moon, evening, day, night, directions, water, land, water. What is that? What is that? What is that? And what is that? And super soul himself, are all witness to the activities of the living entity. Sometimes we may wonder who is witness? Sometimes we may do somethings in closed door, closed rooms, no one is watching. Delete the history and think that no one has watched, I can go scot free? So here it is said, there is no shortage of witness. The sun, the moon. The people. Earth, directions, air, water, sky and ultimately the Supersoul, Paramatma who is seated in our hearts is witness to all our actions.

#### karma, cause and effect, responsibility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/x9oIHHhOdJs?t=1778
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "But ultimate cause. The ultimate cause is our own deeds sanctioned by the Lord under the influence of the stringent laws of nature also called the law of karma."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that while there may be immediate causes for suffering, the ultimate cause is our own past deeds sanctioned by the Lord under the law of karma, emphasizing the importance of taking responsibility for our actions.

So, when we see something happening in our lives, good or bad, always, always, always there is an immediate cause. It could be my boss who is making my life miserable in my office. It could be my spouse in the office. In the home. It could be children because of whom I may feel that I am miserable. It could be because of some near and dear one who parted, who left untimely and because of which today I am miserable. It could be anything. The immediate cause could be anything. But ultimate cause. The ultimate cause is our own deeds sanctioned by the Lord under the influence of the stringent laws of nature also called the law of karma. Everyone is getting what is due to him or her as per the deeds a person has done in this life or previous life.

---
#### acceptance, cause and effect, Supreme Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/x9oIHHhOdJs?t=1864
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "As you sow, so shall you reap."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the importance of acceptance and understanding the law of cause and effect, tracing the chain of causation back to the Supreme Lord, who sanctions everything according to the infallible law of karma.

This understanding brings in an element of accountability or responsibility. Otherwise, we can always play the blame game. In fact, people who are quite wise, even though they may not be very spiritual, may not be very religious, they have also come to the understanding that, the understanding of the law of acceptance. We have to accept. Good or bad, what is manifest in our life, we have to accept. There is no use crying over the spilt milk. These are all things which people have come to an understanding based on logic, based on wisdom, based on some knowledge. So, first is the law of acceptance. We accept. Yes, it is my doing. It is because of me. Immediate cause. Just like the immediate cause of rain is the clouds. Why it rains? Because of clouds. But the clouds are just the immediate cause. It is not the ultimate cause. How are the clouds caused? The clouds also have another cause. What is that? The water getting evaporated because of heat. What is the cause of that heat? It is the sun rays. So, like that if you see, there is series of cause and effect. Every effect has a cause and every cause also has an effect. That has further another cause, a super cause. And if you trace back, the ultimate cause is the Supreme Lord, who says that I am equal to everyone. And we are put in this world where there is a stringent law operating. As you sow, so shall you reap.

#### material existence, three modes, desire

**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** Narrating an experience of seeking donations from a businessman who was too engrossed in his work to be receptive, illustrating the materialistic mindset.

And that's all in the middle of a battlefield many years back it was the Janmasthani time and we were on a drive to collect some donations for Janmasthani so somebody gave me a reference or a friend of his but he said please do not tell my name Janmasthani many people want to give reference but somehow they're not very comfortable giving a reference so he said he is a very nice man he will definitely donate to kindly approach him so this office was in a multi-story building I somehow reached the building but the security is not allowed and I could see that there was strict security and everyone who was coming they were supposed to fill a slip and you know to call those people and they were supposed to fill a slip and you know to call those people say well want to bring the be but somehow I could enter that building don't ask me how sometimes you have to торkun fahru, something beyond communication, the civil- Incorrectipe millions of traveling year. sometimes you have to not illegally but you have to use intelligence so I said by the fantastic winter and I was directly the office of this gentleman was supposed to be very rich very big business man. And the moment I reached the office, there was a glass door, he saw me, I opened the door and said, may I come in? He was not very happy, he said, and it was quite evident from his face, although he didn't speak, it was quite evident, how did you manage to enter this office? Because he knew there was filter 1, filter 2, filter security guard was there, reception was there, somehow I could manage to reach that. And then I could see from that glass door, he was having a phone in his hand, and then the other hand was writing something, the secretary was standing right in front of him, he was trying to speak to her, give her some dictation, casually doing many things, and it was around 11.30 in the morning. So he was a little irritated seeing me because it was peak time for him. Business hours, peak time, 11.30 in the morning, I just entered the office, was making some important calls, important business transactions were happening, and I could also sense that had he had some 3 more hands or 4 more hands, all the scientists were busy. They were so busy. And then he said, how did you get this job done? Then I said, no, it's a must. And then he quickly, you know, without mincing words, he took out some 2, 500 rupees, no, I mean, he took it out with him and he literally threw it at me. So I said, take it out. Is there a reason for this? Do you want a donation? Take it out. It took 2 minutes. Now take it out. Time is money for me. It's business time. Do not disturb. I could understand. It's business time, peak time. Time is money. In that, every minute he is projecting lakhs of rupees.

#### Association, Materialism, Degradation, Intelligence

**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Unfortunately, in today's world, bad association is available to all of us at the fingertips. That's where we have to use our intelligence, use our discretion to choose the right association."
**Summary:** Highlighting the abundance of bad association in today's world and the need to use intelligence and discretion to choose the right association.

Sangha, Sanjayati, Karma. And so, he stands for the right association. Unfortunately, in today's world, bad association is available to all of us at the fingertips. Right in our house, the television, most of the time, most of the things which we bought could be degraded. That's where we have to use our intelligence, use our discretion to choose the right association. What is materialistic association? What is material association which will degrade us? Who is a materialist? At least we should know what association to aspire for, what association to keep away from.

#### Materialist, Spirit, Intimate Association, Enemies
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4Vb0SrJwC3k?t=1392
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Who thinks that matter is all in all? There is no spirit, there is no God, there is no previous life, there is no afterlife. This life is all in all. Goan of life is all in all. All is sweet, green, greenery and joy. There is no Satya, there is no sadhana. Whatever gives you happiness, do it. Just do it. And joy not. We may interact, we don't have to make anyone our enemies, but don't intimately associate."
**Summary:** Defining a materialist as someone who believes only in matter and not in spirit, and advising against intimate association with materialists while emphasizing that we don't need to make them our enemies.

Who is a materialist? We have a word called spiritualist, we have a word called materialist. Who is a materialist? Who thinks that matter is all in all? There is no spirit, there is no God, there is no previous life, there is no afterlife. This life is all in all. Goan of life is all in all. All is sweet, green, greenery and joy. There is no Satya, there is no sadhana. Whatever gives you happiness, do it. Just do it. And joy not. So what would we see, of course, need not be an atheist, need not be an atheist. Now, We cannot completely be away from such people, we cannot, we have to interact. So, Bhagavatam gives the answer, yes, we may interact, we don't have to make anyone our enemies, but don't intimately associate.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### What is Conditional Life? | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### conditional life, material nature, ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1qe-DAT_KHU?t=0
**Verse References:** BG 15.7
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Conditioning means slowly you get used to it."
**Summary:** The lecture explains conditional life as being attracted to the three modes of material nature, contrasting it with our original state of Sat, Chit, and Ananda, and uses the analogy of a hog living in filth to illustrate how ignorance prevents us from realizing our true condition.

So here in this verse, the difference between conditional life and life in Krishna consciousness is being explained. When the living entity is attracted by the three modes of material nature, it is called conditional life. So here Prabhupada mentions that Ganeshu or Maya consciousness involves attachment to the three material modes of nature, under which one works sometimes in goodness and knowledge, sometimes in passion, and sometimes in ignorance. Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita mentions that as living entities, we are His amshas, and we are of the same quality of the Supreme Sat, Chit, and Ananda. But if we analyze our present state, it is quite in contrast to our original quality of being Sat, Chit, and Ananda. Everyone is struggling. Some have realized it. Some are so much in ignorance that they don't even realize it. Prabhupada gives the example of a hog living in a filthy place. Apparently, it is happy. It doesn't even recognize or realize that it is in such an abominable condition. Sometimes we see people, you know, completely dirty, not taken bath, completely torn clothes, sometimes beggars or people living in slums. I mean, when we see that, it's difficult for us to comprehend how they can live like that. Such dirty clothes, not taken bath, body is full of, you know, you can see physically a black layer on the skin. We can't even contemplate living like this even for half a day. So, that is the meaning of conditioning. Conditioning means slowly you get used to it.

#### virtues, knowledge, education, secularism, religion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DQYfjvpJSj4?t=0
**Verse References:** BG 10.4
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Real education is which enables us to discriminate between matter and spirit."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the topic of the source of all virtues, discusses the defects in modern education, and emphasizes the importance of understanding the soul and the science of religion.

So, today the discussion is on the topic, the source of all virtues. We will be discussing four verses, 10th chapter, verse number 4 to 7, based on the teachings of His Divine Grace, A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Srila Prabhupada. Anyone can help me with the answer, what does A.C. stand for? A.C. I thought it was air conditioner. A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Srila Prabhupada. So, let's chant this verse together, fourth verse of the 10th chapter. Please repeat after me. Bodher Jnanam Asamoha Shama Satyam Damaha Shamaha Sukham Dukham Bhavo Abhavo Bhayam Chaabhayam Evacha. Translation: Intelligence, knowledge, freedom from doubt and delusion, forgiveness, truthfulness, self-control and calmness, pleasure and pain, birth, death, fear, fearlessness, non-violence, equanimity, satisfaction, austerity, charity, fame and infamy, are created by me alone. How many of us would like to develop some good qualities? We all want to be truthful, to be honest, to be clean, to be tolerant, yes, no. How many of us would like to cultivate a lot of good qualities? So that people can say, bahut acha admi hain, bahut acha vyakti hain. So, today we'll get a clue how these qualities can develop in our character, in our personality. How we can genuinely develop good qualities. So, we'll go one by one. So, Srila Prabhupada in the purport mentions that real knowledge means, anyone has read today's verses? Before coming, anyone? What does real knowledge mean? So, Srila Prabhupada starts the purport saying that real knowledge means, Yes, very good. So, Srila Prabhupada says, real knowledge, there is some defect in modern education. There is some defect in modern education. There is some defect in modern education. Although we have many, many schools, many, many colleges, many, many, you know, institutes all over the world. But Srila Prabhupada mentions, where is a department, where is an institution which makes us know more about the real self, which is the soul. This body, we take so much care of this body, do so much for this body. And when that soul leaves the body, we cremate that body, we just burn it, we bury it. So, what makes this body valuable is actually the presence of soul. Minus soul, this body is a dead body. We just cremate it. So, where is that department of knowledge? We are studying about the birds, we are studying about all different forms of life, we are studying so many departments of knowledge are there. Srila Prabhupada, when he went to MIT, he said, where is the department which studies about the soul? So, real education is which enables us to discriminate between matter and spirit. Enables us to realize the self. Enables us to understand what is the real person within this body. And Srila Prabhupada says, modern education, unfortunately, in the name of so-called secularism, we have kept this body of knowledge aside. And actually, if you see, lot of people are confused about religion. In fact, lot of people fighting in the name of religion. And one of the reason is that this science, actually it's a science. It's a great science. Imagine if we were not taught physics, chemistry and maths through well-defined textbooks. I give this example quite often. When I say that this presenter or slide mover has atoms, molecules, it has electrons, electrons are moving in their respective orbits. All of you will agree with this. Because we have systematically studied atoms, molecules, subatomic particles through well-defined textbooks. It was not left to our own speculation or understanding. Because we studied through certified textbooks and they were consistent in their approach. Whatever is being taught, we have a standard understanding when it comes to chemistry or maths or physics.

#### truthfulness, compassion, self-control, fearlessness, non-violence, equanimity

**Summary:** This segment discusses the importance of truthfulness, even when it's painful, using the analogy of a mother scolding her child out of love and compassion, and explains the meaning of self-control, fearlessness, non-violence, and equanimity.

When it comes to spirituality, we all have knowledge in bits and pieces from different sources. Somebody through Chanda Mama, something from our grandparents, something we watched in television. And we have all connected few dots and we have our own understanding of who is God, what is the purpose of life, what is religion. And although we have different religions in the world, there is a purpose why different religions are there. Because there is some kind of lack of proper understanding, systematic study of this religion. Invariably there is some kind of friction or fight between different people professing different religion. Fanatically claiming that my religion is better than your religion. So to avoid any confusion. It is better to keep everything away from our curriculum. If you were to teach Bhagavad Gita then why not Quran, then why not Bible. So just keep everything away in the name of secularism. And so called understanding which is very very profound, which is very very important. Which is very much required in human form of life. We are completely devoid of. We are missing that important education. So here the word asamoha. Asamoha means a quality whereby one is freed of delusion and doubt. In the beginning of Gita, Arjuna is in delusion. He is full of doubts. When he sees that opponents are his own kinsmen, own relatives. He is bewildered. Should I fight? Should I not fight? He is overcome by, overwhelmed with attachment to his near and dear ones. All of us also at times in our life will go through this phase where we are full of delusion and doubt. And just like we see Arjuna when he heard the message of Gita from Krishna. All his doubt was so to say washed away. All that bewilderment, all that confusion what he had all subsided. He had clarity of thought. He was very clear about what he should do. And he executed what was asked to him to. And he won the battle. So we will come to that. How this quality develops. When one practices the principles of Krishna consciousness. As taught by the Lord in the Gita. This delusion and doubts what we have. We will be able to overcome them. Satyam, truthfulness. So Srila Prabhupada explains Satyam does not mean that you should speak things which are always palatable. Sometimes truth has to be spoken even if it is painful. Just like sometimes doctors have to give an injection. Or sometimes a surgery has to be performed. Likewise a genuine saint, a spiritual master will at times chastise using harsh words. How many of us have heard, have come across harsh words like fools and rascals in Srila Prabhupada's books? Please raise your hands. Others? Not come across? Okay. So Srila Prabhupada has also used harsh words at times. Not only Srila Prabhupada, even Krishna. Can anyone help me with one or two words which Krishna has used in the Gita which are harsh? Mudha. Then? I can't hear. Okay. So he also uses harsh words. Mudha. Then there is a verse where it says, Fools deride me. And Smriti Vibramaha. The knowledge has been overpowered by illusion. And then Dushkritinaha. Dushkrita means, very sinful of men. Dushkritinaha. Mudha. Like that. Many words are there. Now why does saintly people at times use harsh words? I met a person who used to come to our session. And he was very put off when he heard this word rascal first time in Srila Prabhupada's books. He was very put off. He says, how can Sadhu use this kind of a word? Anyone would like to answer? Why? A Sadhu can be very pleasing. Speak something which is very palatable to us. Very nice to hear. It feels very good to listen to us. Why a Sadhu at times uses harsh words? Truthful. But why harsh words? It can hurt us at times. It can hurt us. It can give us pain. Okay. Okay, let me give you an example which all of you will be able to relate with. Let's say if you have somebody, let's say a mother. She goes up the terrace and she finds a child, you know, busy flying the kite. And it happens to be a terrace which we generally don't see, but otherwise it's having an incline. It's a slant there. And the child is completely, you know, fully busy flying the kite. And is completely oblivious of the fact that he's on the rooftop and he's almost reached the edge of the roof. And the mother reaches the top of the roof and she sees the child in that state. It's completely busy flying the kite and completely oblivious of the fact that it's reached the edge of the roof. And next moment it can just fall down. And even lose its life. Now what will the mother do? Give a chocolate to the child? What will the mother do? Scream? Yes, Mr. Deepak? Yeah. Yeah. So, Buddu, are you mad? Are you mad? You know, assuming that it's a safe distance. Not that after screaming he'll fall away. Are you mad? What are you doing? That will be an instant, this thing. Out of compassion, out of love. You don't want that child, you know, out of mistake, just fall off. Not knowing that he's in the edge of a roof. Are you stupid? Don't you have any sense? Don't you understand? Yes or no? Mothers use this kind of word or not? Out of love. Worse. Yes, worse. You're right. Are you stupid? Because the mother can see the foolishness in the act of the child. Next moment he'll be falling off from the roof and die. He doesn't know about it. Doesn't have a sense. Can you not see that he's in the edge of a roof? Now imagine, the saintly people who can see through, they have that vision which we don't have. They're self-realized soul. They can see the predicament of the soul which has got this human birth, is wasting this human birth unnecessarily. Just losing this opportunity. So at times they're harsh. Fools. And at times to shake us up. Now imagine the mother, sometimes the mothers are harsh to their children because they want to teach them that lesson. They want them to realize that is sheer stupidity. Otherwise if you say, Son, why are you on the side? Very palatable, very soft. The child may not get the message clear. Child may not understand that there was something really wrong in what he was doing.

#### compassion, charity, fame, satisfaction, austerity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DQYfjvpJSj4?t=1200
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The greatest charity is to contribute for the purpose of the mission of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment continues with a personal anecdote about stealing coupons as a child and being punished by his father, then transitions to a story about a king who shows compassion to a boy who accidentally hit him with a stone, and discusses the qualities of a devotee, including self-control, charity, and satisfaction.

In fact, the more I remember, this reminds me when I was a child, you know we used to go to a place where those days used to have gold spot and thumbs up. And in the place where we were staying we used to have, you know, the coupons. Where you can give a coupon and you can get those gold spot and thumbs up cold drinks. So one time it so happened that the person who used to dispense these cold drinks, he went for a place and, you know, we could as some children friends get hold of the coupons which he had collected from others. Some eight or ten coupons. In fact, we stole those coupons. And after some time, we again went back to the same person with those coupons and actually took the gold spot and thumbs up. I came back home and, you know, I was very excited. I had a lot of fun. Five gold spot, two thumbs up. Never had had so much maja. And happened to unfortunately disclose that to my father. First time in my life, first time in my life, I got a cold beating. He took the belt and I can't forget that day. Never seen my father so angry. Never seen my father beating me. Took that belt and he was, it was difficult for him to digest the fact that I have stolen the coupons. So likewise, an acharya who is very, very compassionate. On one side, Srila Prabhupada uses harsh words. Other side, imagine he left Vrindavan which is such a spiritually surcharged place. He goes in a cargo ship with 40 rupees in his pocket and having seasickness, dizziness, heart attacks, going to America, 40 days in the ocean. For what? For giving this knowledge. Not because he was proud that he knows something and they don't know and they are all. Out of compassion. To distribute, disseminate this knowledge which is utmost necessity, especially in the human form of life. So when a saintly person uses harsh words, it is not out of pride. It is not out of some kind of ghamand. It is out of compassion. It is to shake us up. It is to break our false ego. Fools and rascals use these words so that it hits us. We realize our mistake. So we should understand the spirit in which these words are used. This is not to show that I am great, you are small. The idea is to shake us up. Self-control. Self-control means we use our senses within a framework. Senses are meant for a purpose. But if we unrestrictedly enjoy the senses and think that goal of life is to maximize sense enjoyment, this is a big mistake. So self-control means we use our senses, gratify the senses within a framework. Abhayam. What is the meaning of abhayam? Bhaya means fear. Abhayam means fearlessness. A person who is on the path of Krishna Consciousness, who is practicing the principles of devotional service, he will be blessed with abhayam. And meaning is fearlessness. A devotee is naturally, in fact, Srila Prabhupada's name, AC, A stands for Abhay. A devotee is fearlessness. Srila Prabhupada, when he went to the West, was fearless. Where he is going to stay, how he is going to survive there, how he is going to preach this message, who will listen to him, was having faith in the Lord, in the protection of the Lord. Ahimsa. What is the meaning of ahimsa? Non-voilence. Typically, the understanding we get is if we don't kill animals or kill unnecessarily. Real ahimsa means we don't kill the inclination of the soul to serve Lord or the inclination of the soul for self-realization. If the soul is restricted or refrained or taken away from this real path, that also is one kind of ahimsa. Samatha. Equanimity. Freedom from attachment and aversion. So Krishna in these verses is saying all these qualities emanate from me. They are coming from me. Just like when iron is in touch with fire, iron becomes hot. At times it becomes red hot. When we are in touch with the Lord, as reservoir of all these qualities, we also naturally develop these qualities. Many times we want these qualities. Humility. Tolerance. Pridelessness. Equanimity. Fearlessness. And sometimes our effort is at a sentimental level. At a psychic level. At a mental level. With some kind of positive affirmations. I am fearless. I am great. It is at a mental level. But when a person is on this path, the more advances, more he advances in his Krishna consciousness, these qualities naturally manifest in his character and behaviour. One time, there was a king living in a palace. Had a beautiful garden in that palace. And had a huge wall, boundary wall, all across the palace. And just outside the palace, in his kingdom, you know, there were all kinds of people staying. One time it so happened, a Brahmin and his son happened to pass by, by those walls surrounding the palace. And they happened to see a mango tree, which was planted within the palace, within the garden, in the palace. And had some branches protruding out of the boundary wall. By the way, how many of us have in the childhood done all those gimmicks? Tree in the neighbour's house and especially those fruits are very sweet, am I right? Stolen fruits or fruits which we, you know, comes with all kinds of efforts. So, so he happened to see this mango tree with a, laden with all good juicy mangoes. And some of those branches were protruding out from those wall and this child was very hungry. And naturally as a child, seeing a mango tree with lot of mangoes and he feeling hungry, he took a stone and just threw it at the branch. And luckily, you know, it struck the mango and the mango fell. He was very happy. But unfortunately, unfortunately, unfortunately, can anyone guess what happened? Stone went inside the palace and hit, unfortunately, the king. So, you know, it hit on his head and it started bleeding. So, immediately the ministers and soldiers, they got alerted and, they rushed outside to see who has thrown the stone. And, you know, here this boy is there with the mango in his hand, you know, very satisfied that, you know, he has got this juicy mango after hitting the tree on the right place. So, they rushed and says, who is this one who has thrown this stone? And they caught hold of this boy and this Brahmin. They brought him inside the palace, presented in front of the king. And all of them were very furious. He is the one who has thrown the stone. And this Brahmin and this son, was shivering, especially when they came to know that the stone has hit the king and the head is bleeding. So, the king, instead of getting angry, called this boy in the front. He says, please come. So, he was, you know, I am sorry, I am sorry, I didn't knew this. He says, what is there in your hand? So, he was surprised with this question. He says, mango. Where did you get this mango from? He said, from that tree. They said, how did you get this mango? So, he said, very reluctantly, he said, you know, I had thrown the stone. So, I got this mango. So, then, the king heard this. And then, without getting angry, he ordered for his minister. He says, please leave them. And also, unnecessarily, they are in pain. Please get some gold coins and give it to them. So, the ministers and all these attendants were quite surprised with the behavior of the king. Here is a man who has thrown the stone and hit the king and the head is bleeding. And here he says that, you know, leave them alone and give them some gold coins. So, they were, one of them became curious and asked the king, why so? Why are you leaving them, going them scot free?

Tit for tat, just because he has hit me. Should we not learn some lessons from this tree, that we cut it, we, you know, hit it, finally with all the thing it gives us the leaves, the fruits, the bark, the trunk. So, then all of them, hearing the king, got the message what the king was trying to give. Like Arjuna, after having tried all means, when there was a last resort, to fight, yes, he did fight. Sometimes people say, if you are tolerant, people will take undue advantage. Let me tolerate things, which I cannot, change. And, the most important thing, anyone? Give me the discretion, the ability to discriminate between the two. What I should change, and what I should, tolerate. Tushthi, what does Tushthi means? Satisfaction. So, devotee, automatically, reaches a stage, where he is, satisfied, self-satisfied. Because, we don't experience, Tushthi, there is a constant, rat race, constant running, always wanting, more and more, more and more, more and more, hoping against hope, that if I have this, I will be happy, I will experience Tushthi. Tapas, Tapas means, austerity, penance, Divya, Tapo Divyam, a devotee, automatically, is in a platform, where he, practices, some kind of, Tapas, or austerity. Yashaha, what is the greatest, fame, which we can achieve? The greatest, fame, which we can, achieve, is, the love, the love, the love, the love, the love, the love, love, what is the greatest, fame, which we can, aspire for? In fact, fame, which, is, really, meaningful? Anyone? Everyone is trying, to be famous. Spiritual happiness, to be remembered, after we die, but, we won't enjoy it, we will leave, what is the true, fame, which we should, aspire for? Everyone is happy, we are also happy, anything else? So true fame, which we should, aspire for, where, we can be recognized, by the Lord, as a devotee, as a devotee, that is the fame, which we should, aspire for. Charity, a genuine, a person, who is spiritually, advanced, who is in spiritual, consciousness, he will be, charitably, disposed. Why he will be, charitably, disposed? Because, he understands, that whatever, is with me, is not mine. Srila Prabhupada, in the purport, explains, grahasthas, especially, those who are, living in the family, they should, give, their, substantial portion, of the income, for, good causes. And then, Srila Prabhupada, explains, since Krishna, is good, his cause, is also, good. And, there are Brahmanas, they are devotees, of the Lord, who are, spreading the mission, of the Lord. The greatest, charity is, to contribute, for, the purpose, of, the mission, of, the Lord. Those, who are, spreading, the message, of the Lord. And then, he explains, that how, the Brahmanas, they have given up, everything, for this message, to propagate, this message, to disseminate, this message. And, the grahasthas, should support, these Brahmanas, and, this charity, is, the highest charity. Sukham, Sukham is, aspiring, for happiness, which is, anyone would like, to complete? Aspiring, for happiness. We cannot, not aspire, for happiness. Sat, Chit, Ananda. It's a quality, of the soul, to seek, happiness. Seek, happiness. Anyone, would like, to complete? Which, happiness? Keeping, the Lord, in mind. Seek, happiness, which is, favourable, which is, conducive, for our, spiritual, growth. The Lord, of happiness, in this world, broadly speaking, material happiness, and, spiritual happiness. Spiritual happiness, is, nectar, in the beginning, but, it could be, himsa, for the soul. That, happiness, which is, so called, displeasing, so called, painful, so called, poison, in the beginning, but, could be, elevating, the soul. And, that kind of, activity, is called, tapaha. Tapo, devyam, shudhyet. What happens, when you do, tapasya, or austerity? Srila Prabhupada says, when we, are chanting, let's say, we start chanting, and, we struggle, to hear, it's very difficult, for us to focus, our mind. That very act, that very activity, of japa, is tapasya. And, when we do tapaha, what will happen? Tapo, devyam, shudhyet, we'll get, purified. Our consciousness, will get, purified. Our mind, will get, purified. Then, what happens? Shudhyet, brahma, saukhyam, tu anantam. Real happiness, which is, anantam, which is not, temporary, which is, unending, which is, eternal. So, saukhyam, we are not, against saukhyam. We should look, for sukha. But, that sukha, which is, permanent, that sukha, which will, last. That, will, last. That, will, last. That, that, that sukha, which is, real. And, that comes, when, tapo, divyam, shudhyet. Now, one who wants to advance, in Krishna Consciousness, when one treads, this path, gradually, he will see, these qualities, automatically, starts, manifesting, in his behaviour. Srila Prabhupada, in one place, says, harav, abhaktasya, kuto, mahat, dhyana. In fact, this is, sometimes, especially, when you travel, in the, western countries, you see, everything is, very disciplined, very neat, clean, people are, very principled, they don't, speak lies. And, India, so called, dharmbhoomi, we see, everything happening, the reverse. Yes, or, no? People, throw, things, everywhere, you know, it's not, very clean. And, here, we see, people, so called, ekadashyam, vrata rakhte hain, there, people are, eating beef. So, how do you, reconcile? On one side, we have, people eating, all kinds of things, which we say, are abominable, are sinful, and, behaving, properly, they are truthful, they are clean, they are disciplined, they follow, the rules. Other side, we have, people here, were so called, dharmic, and, unclean. I was in a place, in Vrindavan, we had gone, to register, some, some, land. And, we was just, sitting outside, the registered office. And, that place, especially, that, it was a winter season, and, you know, in North India, we have, the groundnuts, available. And, people were sitting, and, as if, you know, the whole place, belongs to them, just eating freely, the groundnut, and, khaya, and, bhenka, khaya, and, bhenka, all around, you know, those groundnut peels, like that. So, can anyone help me, how do we reconcile? You understood, what I am saying? Yes, we seem to be, quite disciplined, quite clean, quite well mannered, no, but, what I am trying to say is, yes, but, how do we, on one side, we say, you know, you know, from the perspective, of God Consciousness, there could be some people, who are, at least, but, very, very clean, the other side, you know, you go to some, so called, holy places, you will see them, very dirty, and, sometime, it can be very bewildering, how do you reconcile? Everyone is? Okay, anybody else, would like to, share, or, share, or, share, share, share, share, share, share, share, share, share, share, share, share, share, share, share, share, share, share, share, share,
