## HG Amitasana Dasa

### When does real purification happen? | HG Amitasana Dasa | SB 6.1.18 | 06-12-2019
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### purification, devotion, karma-khanda, jnana-khanda
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=0
**Verse References:** SB 6.1.18
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Such a person cannot be purified without at least a slight touch of devotional service."
**Summary:** This segment introduces Srimad Bhagavatam 6.1.18, emphasizing that atonement processes are ineffective without a touch of devotional service, contrasting karma-khanda and jnana-khanda with devotion.

Reading from Srimad Bhagavatam, Canto No. 6, Chapter No. 1, Text No. 18: PRAYAS CHITTANI CHIRNANI NARAYANA PARAN MUKHAM, NANESHKUNANTI RAJENDRA RAKUMBHAM IVAPGAH. Translation and Purport by His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Shri Prabhupada. Translation: My dear King, as a pot containing liquor cannot be purified even if washed in the waters of many rivers, non-devotees cannot be purified by processes of atonement even if they perform them very well. Purport: To take advantage of the methods of atonement, one must be at least somewhat devoted, otherwise there is no chance of one's being purified. It is clear from this verse that even those who take advantage of karma, khanda and jnana-khanda, but are not at least slightly devoted cannot be purified simply by following these other paths. The word PRAYAS CHITTANI is plural in number to indicate that both karma-khanda and jnana-khanda. Narottam Das Thakura therefore says karma-khanda, jnana-khanda, kevala, vishera, bhanda. This Narottam Das Thakura compares the path of karma-khanda and jnana-khanda to pots of poison. Liquor and poison are in the same category. According to this verse from Srimad Bhagavatam, a person who has heard a good deal about the path of devotional service, but who is not attached to it, who is not Krishna conscious, is like a pot of liquor. Such a person cannot be purified without at least a slight touch of devotional service. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta.

---
#### devotion, materialism, ethics, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=111
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It is very important for us to understand why it is important to be a devotee of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the importance of devotion over mere ethical behavior, addressing common arguments that being a good, honest person is sufficient, and questioning the value of devotion when devotees exhibit negative qualities.

So here in this verse it is mentioned that non-devotees cannot be purified by processes of atonement, even if they perform them very well. So Prabhupada mentions about karma-khanda and jnana-khanda. So there are many ritualistic ceremonies, so-called religious performances mentioned in the Vedas. But Prabhupada mentions here that there is no chance of one's getting purified without at least a slight touch of devotional service. So this is a very important point. If one performs these processes diligently, very sincerely, like here it says, processes of atonement, even if they perform them very well, unless there is a tinge of devotion or surrender to the Supreme Lord, unless there is a slight touch of devotional service. We have come across many people, when we preach, more so in present times, there are a lot of people who say that I am spiritual but I am not religious. Or we may come across people who say that for me, work is worship. Or we may come across people who may say that I don't go to temples I don't worship but I am honest. I am a good human being. And very sarcastically, basically they are referring to so called devotees, so called religious people, who may be chanting, who may be doing all kind of so called devotional activities, but may be corrupt, may be indulging in bribery, may be speaking lies. So referring to such devotees, they say that better I am, I may not go to temples, I may not worship, I may not chant the names of Lord, but I am honest, I am truthful, I am ethical, I don't hurt anyone, I try within my means to help others. And they actually believe that that position is superior.

---
#### devotion, materialism, goodness, supremacy of the Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=265
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "But the most important thing is a person should recognize the supremacy of Supreme Lord and recognize one's subordination, that I am subordinate, I am Lord."
**Summary:** This segment explains why being a devotee is essential, emphasizing the recognition of the Lord's supremacy and our subordination to Him, contrasting this with material goodness, which is considered mental speculation from an absolute perspective, using the analogy of dry and wet stool.

So it is very important for us to understand why it is important to be devotee of the Lord. Why just being a good human being is not enough? Why although superficially it appears that yes, what is the use of so called devotion, a person is telling lies, he is getting angry. The other person is calm, composed, a good human being. Sometimes we can also get bewildered. So many years he is chanting, but his behavior is like this, but other person, you know, he doesn't chant, he doesn't go to the temple, he doesn't do any so called, but he is very calm, he doesn't get angry, he is very loving, very kind, very gentle. So sometimes we may even doubt, you know, is the process right? Is it really working? Am I following the right process? So let's first understand why it is important, why a devotee, why even a so called, just like here it says, the so called process of attunement, even if they perform them very well, even if a person is assuming not a devotee of the Lord, but he is a very nice human being, even though no matter how good he may be, ornamented with some so called good qualities in the material mundane sense, he is honest, he is loving, he is kind, he is gentle, he is soft spoken, he is well mannered, he doesn't hurt anyone, he is helpful. Although these qualities are considered very highly in this world, which is no doubt has to be there, these qualities are good, we are not, so to say, denying the fact that these qualities are very much wanted. But the most important thing is a person should recognize the supremacy of Supreme Lord and recognize one's subordination, that I am subordinate, I am Lord. We are not talking about being a pure devotee where a person realizes that I am Krishna's. Whatever strength I have, whatever ability I have, whatever talent I have, whatever I have, my possessions, all belong to Krishna. That's a very elevated stage of consciousness. So being devoted means to understand the supremacy of Supreme Lord, in some sense, may not be in a pure form, I belong to the Lord, I have to serve the Lord. So here it says, unless one has a tinge of devotional service, even the so called atonement done very nicely has of no use. Even the same question Parikshit Maharaj asks, what is the use of Kunjara Sochavata, elephant taking bath in the river getting cleansed, and then it comes out and again sprays dust on the body.

---
#### materialism, goodness, absolute perspective, analogy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=473
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "There is nothing good in that sense, from absolute perspective, in the material world, so called goodness is also, Prabhupada gives example of dry stool and wet stool."
**Summary:** This segment uses the analogy of shopping in a mall to illustrate the importance of recognizing the Lord's ownership, comparing a devotee who acknowledges this to a customer who pays for their items, and a non-devotee to a thief who tries to take without paying.

So Prabhupada explains, when one is on a material platform, ae bhalo, ae manda, this is good, this is bad, is all mental speculation. There is nothing good in that sense, from absolute perspective, in the material world, so called goodness is also, Prabhupada gives example of dry stool and wet stool. It appears that dry stool, one side is dry, but both are stool. Why this element of recognition of the supremacy of the Lord and we subordinate to the Lord is important? We see people go for shopping in malls, go to a shop. In malls, we have unlike traditional shop where you stand across the counter and you tell the shopkeeper he picks the items. In mall, when you enter, you have trolly, basket and then you keep walking in the mall freely and pick up whatever items you want. In mall, you have, key is a full boxed choice of food. You come and go to a shop, we analyze, one common aim is to drink. Inside a shop helping the rich to cook, one should also watch from face to dedication in order to survive the non Conscious and Self-25. You can't touch items. Tell the shopkeeper, he will come and give it to you. But in this case, you freely walk around in a shop, in a mall and pick up items. And in case your trolley gets filled, another sales person will come up with another trolley. You want something more? So in other words, he wants you to pick up items. And after you have picked up all the items, whatever you want, at the end, it is expected that you recognize that these items belong to that mall. And because you want to take it with you, you have to pay the price. So at the billing counter, they quickly bill whatever items you have taken and you pay the price and you walk out. And with that, with great respect and actually these days they are trained to treat customer like a king, customer loyalty. They are very happy that you have picked lot of items. And they will also say thank you sir and they may even, again depends, they may even want you to come back again to give them more business. But imagine a person who walks into that mall. He also picks up items. But not a full trolley or a basket. Just two or three items. And he tries to come out of the mall without paying for it. In every mall there is a terminology they use in accounting. It is called shrinkage.

---
#### analogy, devotion, materialism, ownership
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=671
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "What differentiates between a good customer and a thief is a person recognizes that it is his property malls property. And since I am taking, I have to pay for it."
**Summary:** Continuing the mall analogy, this segment contrasts a paying customer with a thief, highlighting that the key difference is the recognition of ownership and the obligation to pay, relating this to recognizing the Lord's ownership of our abilities and possessions.

Shrinkage means, you know, just like clothes when you wash it shrinks. So in every mall it is understandable that there will be some shrinkage. Shrinkage is because of people who go and flick some items. When we were in college, so there were some friends from very rich family. Very rich family. And not that they were dearth of scarcity of money. But it was adventure for them to go into a shop and pick up some greeting card. Archie's greeting card. That love becomes even more, you know, you have picked up a greeting card for your lover. And with great adventure, with lot of risk, you know, it really adds more rasa to the whole. Valentine day or whatever. So lot of this to do that, flick the items. Now you see, and if a person is caught, there was one Indigo pilot. It was in the news. It was in a very senior position, a pilot. And he, you know, flights layover happens when they go to a particular, they have flown for 16 hours, 20 hours. So they overnight stay there in the destination. So this person in a duty free shop in the airport while coming, he picked up some item and did not pay for it. Which later on CCTV cameras captured that. And finally, he was removed from job. He was suspended. Probably he went to the court or something like that. That suspension was revoked. And finally, net net was he lost his job. A pilot, very reputed airline, international flights. And he lost his job. So what's the difference between a person who takes items, these two different individuals. Both of them are picking up items from the shop. A person, trolley load or many, many baskets of item picks up and he walks out. Whereas a person just one or two items, one or two items, he's also picked up and is trying to walk out without paying for it. He's called a thief. You see, many things are common. Both are walking in the mall. Both are looking at items. Both are wanting some items. Both are picking some items. Both are coming out of the mall. Many things are common. But what differentiates between a good customer and a thief is a person recognizes that it is his property malls property. And since I am taking, I have to pay for it. Whereas a thief wants to enjoy that without recognizing that it is malls and I have to pay for it. That's the essential difference.

---
#### devotion, materialism, humility, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=880
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That is the essential difference between a materialist and a devotee."
**Summary:** This segment defines devotion as recognizing the Lord and acknowledging that our abilities come from Him, contrasting this with the materialist's belief in their own self-derived abilities, which can lead to pride and atheism, and reiterating that without devotion, atonement and other practices are useless.

So likewise, without a tinge of devotion. Devotion means being a devotee. Devotion means to recognize the Lord. If I have some ability, if I have some talent, if I have this body. If I am able to do something, if I am able to see something, if I am able to lift something. That ability is coming from the Lord. Yes, when a person is a pure devotee, he recognizes that I am not doing anything. The Lord is doing everything. I am just as insignificant Jeeva puppet in the hands of the Lord. Just like Prabhupada when he is going to the West. He is writing, I am an insignificant beggar. I have no power to speak. I don't know how to convince them of your message. That is the consciousness of a pure devotee. But a materialist so-called who is morally, ethically upright. He believes, he believes that his ability is his. If he is very intelligent, he believes that he is intelligent. In fact, if he is too intelligent, he becomes arrogant. He becomes proud. So-called intelligent people at times even become atheists. Because they see that they are able to control things. They are able to comprehend things. They are able to analyze things. They are able to understand things. Without recognizing that this intelligence is coming from the Lord. Some people have lot of abilities, some skills, some talents. And people glorify them for that. And they really believe that yes, they have done it. They have made it. You see any newspaper when some sports people, they won some trophy or some match or something like that. Invariably there is some interview. Man of the match. How did you do that? So they will invariably end up glorifying. I thought I should be very patient and I should stick on to it. And they glorify themselves. I thought like this. I did like this. And that's how it happened. But so many people think. So many people plan. But then things don't unfold necessarily based on your planning, your thinking. So here Prabhupada mentions that unless that tinge of devotion is there, the understanding is there that I am Lord's. Whatever I have. That is the essential difference between a materialist and a devotee. Now devotee, there is a big spectrum. A pure devotee and a mixed devotee and a Kanishadikari and so many other things are there. So here it says, unless one has that tinge of devotional service, recognition of the Lord, all is so-called atonement, so-called karma khanda, so-called jnana khanda. It's all kevala vishara bhanda. It's all pot of poison. E bhalo e mando sakalara. This all is mental. At a mental level. This is good. This is bad. It is from absolute perspective, unless we bring Lord into picture, it is all bad.

---
#### Krishna consciousness, dormant knowledge, revival, hearing
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=1084
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This Krishna consciousness, recognition that I am Lords. I belong to the Lord. Everything what I have is coming from the Lord, This recognition, this consciousness is already there. It's dormant in all of us."
**Summary:** This segment explains that our job is to revive the dormant Krishna consciousness within us, which is covered by material consciousness, comparing it to dormant sexual desire in children, and emphasizing the importance of hearing about Krishna from a pure devotee to revive this consciousness.

So our job, if we analyze what we are up to in after having joined Prabhupada's mission, our job is. Prabhupada also mentioned that this consciousness. This recognition of the Lord is not an artificial imposition. This Krishna consciousness, recognition that I am Lords. I belong to the Lord. Everything what I have is coming from the Lord, This recognition, this consciousness is already there. It's dormant in all of us. And the reason why in all of us, you're the Lord, you're the mana, you're the crystal, you're the invite to the Lord. This recognition, this consciousness. This Lord, this Krishna consciousness. And the reason why today we feel, we see a lot of people who are atheists, who don't believe in God, who are very hardcore materialists, because this consciousness is now covered. Prabhupada gives another example that just like sexual desire is dormant in everyone, in every child. You see children, boy, girl, they're playing, they're completely oblivious, they're not very, even so-called gender conscious, because that desires have not got yet fructified. That's why that age is called adolescent age, 12 years, 13 years, 14 years, then it starts dawning on them. Again, some hormonal changes happen in the body. Adolescence. At the biological level. So how that these desires are dormant, and then you don't have to necessarily go to college to learn about how to get attracted. It's dormant. Just like at the right age, in the right circumstances, at the right time, they start manifesting. Likewise, this dependence on the Lord. This recognition of the Lord, this recognition that Krishna is the Supreme Controller, He is the Supreme Lord. This recognition also starts manifesting in the consciousness when the circumstances, the situation is right. And that's why it is said, Shunvata, Sukhatha, Krishna, Punya, Shavana, Krishna, Kirtana, Hrida, Antas, Toya, Bhadras, all that Abhadras, all the dirty things, which is absolutely wrong. Actually covering our real knowledge. If we have to uncover, if we have to revive our pure consciousness, Krishna consciousness, recognition of the Lord, then the process which Bhagavatam is recommending is Shunvata, Sukhatha, Krishna. We have to hear about Krishna from a pure devotee of the Lord.

#### chanting, attentiveness, Prabhupada, example
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=1379
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Good that you are chanting, I hope you are also listening."
**Summary:** This segment recounts an anecdote about a devotee assisting Prabhupada and chanting outside his room, where Prabhupada emphasized the importance of listening while chanting, followed by a story about a devotee who didn't chant the full mantra.

There was one time a devotee who was assisting Prabhupada. Prabhupada used to travel extensively so there were devotees who would take turn to you know there were some secretaries and he also devotees of the temple he would go and Prabhupada at times would be late in the night early mornings would be transcribing correspondence writing letters so one time early morning about 2-2.30 one devotee was waiting for Srila Prabhupada just outside his room Prabhupada was busy writing letters responding to the letters and there was very dimly and there was a light in the room dimly and this devotee was waiting outside 2.30 in the morning and the normal thing is this Prabhupada wants something he would the devotee can assist so for the time being So, while he was waiting and it was early morning, so no noise, no sound, pin drop silence, Prabhupada dictating something on the dictaphone and this devotee was outside the room chanting Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna and it was so silent that Prabhupada could hear what the devotee was chanting. So then at one point Prabhupada called the devotee and said, good that you are chanting, I hope you are also listening. So momentary if somebody tells us, you know, that are you listening, so immediately we get into defensive mode, yes, yes, yes, I listen, I listen, I chant. It happened with one devotee in. I was speaking to him. So another devotee came and told me about him that Prabhu, I have gone with him in the vehicle. I have also heard him that he does not chant full mantra. Now this is very interesting, the conversation we had. So he said, does not chant full mantra. I have heard him many times and he also says that his chant, one round gets over in six and a half minutes. And this is very interesting. So he said, does not chant full mantra. I have heard him many times. And he also says that his chant, one round gets over in six and a half minutes.

#### chanting, attentiveness, humility, improvement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=1538
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We all are here to purify. If someone points out our improvement area or mistake, it is good for us. Otherwise how will we improve?"
**Summary:** Continuing the story, the speaker recounts how he addressed the devotee about his chanting, emphasizing the importance of being open to feedback and striving for improvement.

And this devotee who told me was relatively a younger devotee, junior devotee. So he did not have the courage to go and tell him, Prabhu, your chanting is not, you are missing some words. So in one conversation, I called this devotee, not specifically for this, I was having a conversation. So I told Prabhujit, little bit be conscious. This is what this devotee has told, please do not feel hurt about this thing. We all are here to purify. If someone points out our improvement area or mistake, it is good for us. Otherwise how will we improve? So this devotee has told me that you do not chant, you are missing some words. So first response was, no Prabhu. In fact I have, I very attentively chant. I am very conscious that I hear each word. So I said, okay. So how much time does it take? He says, I said, he said six and a half minutes. And he says, if I chant slowly, then my mind wanders. Therefore I chant fast and I finish it in six and a half minutes. So then I said, let us do one thing. I am not here to prove that, in fact it is very good if you are chanting very attentively. Let us do one thing, just a small test. So I said, okay. Let us stop watch. Chant nine mantras full at the speed what you are chanting and let us measure. Again my mood was not to prove that he is wrong, just to help him. Sometimes we all have blind spots and because of our ego, we do not want to accept our mistakes or our shortcomings. Sometimes somebody points out, we feel angry, we get upset. Why did he complain? So I said, okay. So we wrote, I said every time you complete one mantra, one, two, three, I will write down and stopwatch is there in milliseconds, we will be able to measure. So I said you chant at the fast pace, whatever you are chanting. So he started chanting one mantra, two mantra. I was noting down at nine mantra, we stopped the stopwatch.

This is the most important thing. #### chanting, purification, Kali Yuga, attentiveness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=1833
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Of all the devotional activities, the most important is chanting the holy names of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes chanting as the most important devotional activity in Kali Yuga, highlighting the need for attentiveness and proper execution of the process for effective purification.

The process in this Kali Yuga is to chant. Sarvabhauma Bhattacharya, when he went to Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, after he was touched by, he was transformed in Chaitanya Charita, I had mentioned. So he goes and asks Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, of all the devotional items or activities which is the most important? Chaitanya Mahaprabhu says, of all the devotional activities, the most important is chanting the holy names of the Lord. No doubt, Shravanam, Kirtanam, Vishnu Smaranam, all these are important. Prabhupada emphasizes that anyone for that matter. We have examples of devotees, just by listening, just by chanting. Just by praying, just by offering service, they got purified. But in Kali Yuga, specifically, Kirtanadeva Krishnasya Mukta Sangha Param Rajed. So, very, very important for us, many times, we may be, there are two different things actually. We may be chanting, but may not be listening. We may be chanting, but we may be skipping the beads. We may be chanting, but may not be chanting full mantra. And, many a times, if you are not chanting attentively, if you are not listening, because that is a process, if that process is not properly followed, then the process is to help purify our consciousness. Adipa Kriya by Shri Ramakrishna. That whole process will take a longer time. Nama Bhasa and Shuddha Nama, so we will be in a stage where we are chanting offenselessly and it will take time, purification will take time.

#### chanting, progress, mind, concurrent chanting
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=1962
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That's why Prabhupada says when you chant, listen very carefully."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the challenges of maintaining attentiveness while chanting, addressing feelings of stagnation and the importance of concurrent chanting and hearing to focus the mind and serve the Lord through the holy name.

And if you are not conscious at times, because the progress is very slow, we may feel that I am not progressing or lusty desires are still haunting me or so many years I have been chanting, still I am like that only, I don't see much difference. All these kind of thoughts can start cropping up in the mind. So in chanting, so MPP has been talking about in his seminars on japa workshop, so many of us have also heard and trying to implement few of his, realization whatever he is sharing with the devotees. So one may be chanting but he is not listening, this is possible. That's why Prabhupada says when you chant, listen very carefully. So in one of the seminar he talks about concurrent chanting and hearing, concurrent. Concurrent means it is not that we are chanting and once in a while we are hearing. Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna and once in a while we are hearing. Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna and once in a while we are hearing. Concurrent means our mind should be, it is meditation. Meditation means you are focusing on something. Just like typical meditation means it is candle light or focusing on some point. It is all about focusing. But in our case chanting is much more than just focusing. It is serving the Supreme Lord who is descended in the form of holy name. How do we receive that Supreme Lord by giving our attention? How do we give attention by hearing distinctly, clearly each and every word? With me it used to happen that sometimes I used to measure. Even now I measure. I chant in groups of four rounds. So sometimes it generally takes 34 minutes, 35 minutes, 36 minutes. I have noticed some days it will finish in 29 minutes. So what has happened? It is not that suddenly on that particular day you chanted fast. Quite possible. Because we are not listening. Suppose mind goes here and there you may skip the beats. Before the mantra is over you have already moved to the next beat. So and it is a very big challenge. More so because mind is lot of thoughts keep coming. So we invariably ask when chanting time we are thinking of our service. We are thinking of our service because we are not listening. Mind cannot have 10 thoughts simultaneously at one time. Just like multiprocessing they say multitasking. Even multiprocessors in the computer parlance. Although they may process millions of instructions per second. But actually at one time one action is happening. So mind also cannot be thinking of something and listening the Hare Krishna mantra. Cannot. That means if we are getting some thoughts and we are thinking about our service. That means at that moment we are not listening. That mantra or that word we are not listening. So chanting is. Is a very very powerful process. It is the recommended process. It is

This is the 13th verse from the 9th chapter. mahātmanastu mām pārtha daivim prakritim āśritah bhajantiyananya manaso nyātvā bhūtādim avyayam Am I audible in the back side? Translation. O son of Pritha, those who are not deluded, the great souls are under the protection of the divine nature. They are fully engaged in devotional service because they know me as the supreme personality of Godhead, original and inexhaustible. So, mind you, the word used is those who are not deluded. So, in other words, what is the opposite of it? Those who are deluded, what will they think? They will think of opposite of what is happening here. They will be under the protection of, again, as we cover, it will become quite evident. The first sign of a Mahatma, as is mentioned in this verse, he is under the protection of divine nature.

Weekly once he is allowed to make a phone call to his family members, after five minutes the call cuts. He wants to speak more. He wants to meet them. He wants to go home. He wants to have home food but there are restrictions. Not everything what he wants is fulfilled. So if you analyze from that perspective, even this material world imposes a lot of restrictions on us. We wish that we don't become old. We wish that we don't die. We wish that our near ones remains with us for all time to come. Its not fulfilled. Uncalled from nowhere miseries land up in our lives. In spite of all our efforts to be happy, to live, to live, to live, to live, to live, we do not find any hope. We do not find any hope. **Summary:** The speaker continues the prison analogy, comparing Durga Devi to the jail superintendent who enforces restrictions in the material world, also called Durga Dham or Devi Dhama. He reiterates that Maya imposes misery and that it is very difficult to surmount.

to objectively look at what the world is. Just like prison house, to say that prison house, the prisoner is meant to undergo some kind of punishment is not pessimism. It's objective reality of a prison house. So, just like in a prison house, there is a jail superintendent who is the government servant. The same government which is supposed to serve the citizens, the same government constructs a prison house and appoints a jail supervisor whose duty to ensure that all the prisoners are kept within captivity and they are not allowed to go out. Moment somebody tries to get out of the prison house, in fact, the jail superintendent will become even more cautious and watchful. So, without getting more into further discussion, otherwise the whole session can go on. So, just like you have a prison house, you have a jail superintendent, likewise this material world which is also called a prison house in one sense, this Durga Devi is like the jail superintendent. Durga means what? Fort, Killa. Fort. Just like to get inside the fort or outside, huge, tall walls or thick walls are there so that enemies can't enter unauthorized. So, likewise this world, material world is called Durga Dham, Devi Dhama. And Krishna says mamma maa yaad dhurad deyaay, it imposes lot of misery. Lot of people say no, God does not exist. Yes, because you are not under be Prakriti, be under the influence of Maya. And Maya's duty is to impose all kinds of misery on the living entity. And Krishna says, it is my energy. Mamamaya duratya, it is very difficult to surmount.

#### directions, surrender, reformation, freedom
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Nms25Z1eY5w?t=948
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If you surrender to Me, I will be able to do it only if you surrender to Me."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Durga Devi's ten hands representing the ten directions, symbolizing captivity. He quotes Krishna, stating that surrendering to Him is the only way to be freed from Maya, similar to reforming oneself to be eligible for release from prison.

It is said Durga Devi has ten hands. Each hand represents ten directions. What are the ten directions? Up, down, left, right, four corners and front and back. So, from all sides you are you are held captive, you are being watched. Then Krishna says, mam evaye prapadyante mayam etam tarantite, if you surrender to Me, I will be able to do it only if you surrender to Me. If you become a law-abiding citizen, if you reform yourself, just like we have in a normal prison house, you go into a prison house because you have violated the state laws, you have indulged in some criminal activity. When you get reformed, then you are eligible to come out of the prison house. Therefore Krishna says, maam eva ye prapadyante, if you surrender to me, mayam etam taranti te, I will free you from maya.

He states that Mahatmas are under the influence of Daiviprakriti, not mahamaya, and asks why we are entangled in the material world.

Now there are two types of maya. One is called mahamaya. Mahamaya is what we experience in this world and one is yogamaya. Yogamaya is Daiviprakriti, Lord's internal potency. So right now we are under the influence of mahamaya. The Mahatmas, great souls, are under the influence of Daiviprakriti. They are not under the influence of mahamaya. Ichcha, dvesha, samuthena, dvandamohena bharata. When one develops Ichcha, desire to enjoy independently. How do we desire? **Summary:** The speaker continues the analogy, describing how wild elephants (matanga) are captured using trained female elephants that arouse them through touch, leading them into a pit.

Then matanga. Matanga means elephant. You know in especially wild forest, how the elephants are caught, wild elephants? They make a pit. But how does the elephant get into that pit? Covered it with, but why will that elephant go there? So there are trained female elephants. And especially behind this head, there is a spot which is a very, very sensitive spot for that elephant. The female elephant is trained to pass by that elephant, a wild elephant, completely untamed and brushes that elephant on that particular spot. So there are trained three elements. And the moment that elephant is touched at that spot, it gets completely untamed. But it gets completely aroused, agitated. And this she-elephant is then further trained to lead him to a particular path. And on that path, they have a pit, a huge pit covered with a green grass. So this elephant who has got aroused, who has got agitated, he just madly follows that she-elephant. And then that she-elephant circumvents that pit. And this elephant passes through that pit which is covered by green grass and falls into it. Then small little mehut, 8 feet, 6 feet, goes and captures him, tames him. So which sense of that elephant led to its downfall? Touch. Skin.

Sometimes you would have seen in national geography, you know, a tiger chasing a deer or something. It is very fast. And it is very, very difficult to catch. Very, very difficult to hunt. And the hunters know one weakness of deer. What is that? So the deer plays, this hunter plays a very, very melodious music. And when that music is played, that deer which is running just stops like a painting on a wall. Completely stationary with eyes closed. Completely absorbed in hearing that melodious song. Some song. We'll play that song after the sing. Okay, Bhringa. What does Bhringa means? So this Bhavara or bumblebee, it gets attracted to the beautiful smell of the lotus flower. And you know what happens when the sun sets? The lotus petals. This Bhavara, which is so powerful, it can bore a hole in a tree, gets so intoxicated with that smell, keeps smelling, keeps playing around and on that fire and by the time the sun sets, the lotus unfolds, folds its petals. And then still, still it doesn't figure out that the petals have closed and it's got captive, keeps intoxicated in that. And by the time the lotus hovers in the morning, it's dead.

So today we're going to discuss from the 10th chapter, verse number 8 to 11, who is a wise man based on the teachings of His Divine Grace AC Bhaktivedanta Swami Srila Prabhupada. We'll again chant this verse together. Translation: I am the source of all spiritual and material worlds, everything emanates from me. **Summary:** The speaker discusses common perceptions of Hinduism as pantheistic, contrasting it with the relative clarity of monotheistic religions like Islam and Christianity, and addresses the idea that any object of faith is sufficient for worship.

If I talk about Islam, which book comes to your mind? Loudly, everyone. If I talk about Christianity, what book comes in your mind? If I talk about Hinduism, what book comes in your mind? Now all of us have a feeling that there are many, many gods. At least in Christianity there is some clarity. They all talk about Jesus. In Islam they talk about Muhammad, they talk about Allah. But see the fate of all us Hindus, and all of us are talking about different gods, different devis, different devatas. We've talked about it earlier. We sometimes feel that Hindu religion is pantheistic. Pantheistic means which promotes worship of many, many gods. And many people have deep devita is to diva, means I resonate this guide, so I worship this car. And there's another school of thought, you need someone to worship, doesn't matter whom you worship. Some icon should be there, some item should be there to put your faith on. Doesn't really matter who that person is.

**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the pantheistic view with the Bhagavad Gita's assertion that Krishna is the source of everything, addressing potential criticisms of the Krishna Consciousness movement and emphasizing Prabhupada's adherence to the Gita's teachings.

So different understandings are there. Now let's see what Gita is saying. Gita, which we all consider as a revered scripture, just like Islam has Quran, Christianity has Bible. In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna is saying, I am the source of all material and spiritual world. Everything emanates from me. This is the definition of God. Sarva Karana Karanam, the cause of all causes. The supreme creator. When there was nothing, he was there. And Krishna in this verse is saying, I am the source of all material and spiritual world. When Prabhupada in his books very emphatically talks about Krishna or he has named this society as International Society for Krishna Consciousness, sometimes it appears fanatical. What do you people say about Krishna, Krishna, Krishna, Krishna? But Prabhupada is sticking to what Gita is saying. Gita is very clearly, Krishna is emphatically saying, I am the source of all spiritual and material world. Everything emanates. Everything emanates from me. The wise who knows this perfectly engage in my devotional service.

**Summary:** The speaker defines 'Buddha' as wise and intelligent, emphasizing the importance of emotional connection and devotion in realizing Krishna as the supreme source, and then transitions to discussing the Vedic literatures and their compiler, Vedavyas.

And worship me with all their hearts. Now, Buddha, Buddha means wise, one who is intelligent, one who has understood things as they are. Bhava, Bhava means emotionally, emotionally with feelings, with love, with devotion, Samanvitaha, not just indifferently, not just, you know, very half-heartedly. One must realize that the Lord Krishna is the supreme source of everything. Everything is emanating from him. Such wise person, such learned person, worships me. How? Buddha, Bhava, Samanvitaha. Samanvitaha means with, together with, accompanied with. All Vedic literatures agree that Krishna is a source of Brahma, Shiva and other devatas. In fact, all the Vedic literatures, who is the compiler? Anyone can help me with an answer? Who is the compiler of all the Vedic literatures? Vedavyas. Vedavyas has compiled many Vedic literatures. And can anyone help me with what is the final, the final work of Vedavyas? After having written different Vedic literatures, the final work, the last book what he wrote, which is more of the essence of all the Vedas, what is that? It is Srimad Bhagavatam. Srimad Bhagavatam. The Vedas is a vast body of knowledge meant for a huge spectrum of people. On one side, we have borigins, cannibals, completely unregulated, uncultured human beings, more or less like animals. No conception of God, no conception of rules, regulations, tapasya, austerities, sacrifices.

#### karma kanda, material desires, Vedas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jgSvxU7s64I?t=373
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "God is a means to an end. What is the end? Your desire's fulfillment."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the karma kanda portion of the Vedas, which offers material benefits in exchange for worship, and how Vedavyasa compiled it to elevate materialistic people towards God consciousness.

The other side, we have completely perfect, pristine, pure Paramahamsas, pure devotees. So Vedas have some instructions which are very important, which are meant for these base people to move them to the next level of their consciousness. So that's why there is a karma kanda portion in the Vedas, which says, you do this sacrifice, you do this yagya, you will get this material benefit. You worship Uma, you will get good wife, you worship Saraswati, you will get learning, goddess of learning, you worship Lakshmi, you will get lot of prosperity and wealth. Nothing to do with God. God is a means to where there is an end. What is the end? Your desire's fulfillment. And there is a place for this instruction, because not everyone is ready to accept the highest thing. There are people who are completely materialistic, now to bring them to God consciousness, all allurement is given. You worship, you worship, you will get this. Worship, get it. So that portion of the Vedas is called karma kanda. And Vedavyasa has compiled that, because he wanted to elevate everyone. Vedas means knowledge, meant for everyone. So after having compiled all the Vedic literatures, Vedavyasa was despondent, he was very sad, he was not satisfied, he was not feeling contented, for having done this compilation.

#### Srimad Bhagavatam, Narada Muni, uncompromising devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jgSvxU7s64I?t=463
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You have not spoken directly, in an uncompromising way, about the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts how Narada Muni, Vedavyasa's spiritual master, recognized his despondency and advised him to compile a literature focusing directly on the Supreme Lord without compromise.

So Narada Muni, who is his spiritual master, when he happened to meet Vedavyasa, he could immediately understand the cause of his despondency. And he said, you have created, you have compiled all these Vedic literatures, you have told so many things, but it is like beating around the bush. You have not spoken directly, in an uncompromising way, about the Supreme Lord. And it is quite possible that people whom you are trying to convince, that people whom you are trying to elevate, by giving them such instructions, karma kanda portion, they can go tangent, they can get misled, they can think that this is the ultimate purpose of the Vedas, to do the sacrifices and have material prosperity and enjoy life comfortably. That is not the purpose. So then Narada Muni said that they can get distracted, they can feel that this is the end. Ultimate objective of religion is to do yagnas, to do the sacrifice for your material prosperity. So then Narada Muni told Vedavyasa, now you compile a literature where you speak about the Supreme Lord in an uncompromising way. Do not beat around the bush. This is not the words of Narada Muni, but the essence was that. Now speak about the Supreme Lord. So Srimad Bhagavatam, which is the final work of Vedavyasa.

---
#### Srimad Bhagavatam, cheating religions, Krishna's pastimes
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jgSvxU7s64I?t=557
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "All the religions in one sense, they call cheating religion, because there is a promise of material prosperity, material well-being, and God is a means to that end."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Srimad Bhagavatam rejects religions that promise material prosperity, emphasizing that loving God should be the ultimate goal, and highlights the Bhagavatam's focus on the pastimes of Krishna.

Starts with the verse that this Srimad Bhagavatam will reject all cheating religions. dharma projita kaitavatra paramo nirmatsranam. Now all the religions in one sense, they call cheating religion, because there is a promise of material prosperity, material well-being, and God is a means to that end. Service to God is not the end. Loving God is not the end. God is a means to an end. What is that? The end is your own fulfillment of desires. That's what generally religiosity is all about. People do, when there is a house warming ceremony, they do some pooja, so that there is peace in the house. When there is some exam is there, people go to the temple and worship. Some elections are there, politicians throng to the temple. It's all about blessings I need of God for my desires to get fulfilled. So in that sense, it is not pure religion. It is dharma projito kaitava. Kaitava means cheating religion. So in the Srimad Bhagavatam, Shukadeva Goswami now starts delineating directly about who the Supreme Lord is and what is the ultimate purpose of religion. And then he talks about different incarnations, incarnations of the Lord, and in the first canto, there are twelve cantos, in the first canto, Vedavyasa says, iti chamsa kalapumsa krsnastu bhagvan svayam, indrari vyakulam lokam hridayanti yuge yuge. Although many many incarnations come from time to time, there are many many incarnations, krsnastu bhagvan svayam, and the entire Srimad Bhagavatam is dedicated to describing the pastimes, the leelas of the Supreme Lord Shri Krishna.

---
#### Krishna, supreme controller, source of Brahma and Shiva
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jgSvxU7s64I?t=682
**Verse References:** BG 10.2, BG 10.8
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is the source of Brahma, Shiva, and all the demigods."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses potential misunderstandings about Krishna's pastimes, emphasizing that Krishna is the source of Brahma, Shiva, and all demigods, and that He effortlessly manages the material and spiritual worlds through His energies.

The Dasma Skanda, the tenth canto, is about Krishna's esoteric pastimes with the gopis, with Radharani, which unfortunately, if heard without proper context, proper understanding of who the Lord is, we can get mistaken. Sometimes people say, you know, okay, I understand Krishna is there, supreme, why did he dance with the gopis? Why did he indulge in all these so-called frivolous activities? Why did he steal butter? Stealing is not considered something very good. How can such a person be God? So, the Vedic leaders agree that Krishna is the source of Brahma, Shiva, and all the demigods. In fact, in the Gita itself, Krishna says, aham adir hi devanam. Aham means I, adir means the source, the creator, the creator of devanam. In the Moksha Dharma, Krishna also says, prajapati cha rudra capi aham eva srijami. Srijami means I create. Prajapati means, whose prajapati is? Brahma. Rudra, whose rudra? Lord Shiva. Capi aham eva srijami, I am the creator. Aham sarvasya prabhavo matta, matta means from me, sarvam pravartate emanates, comes about. Now, one who understands Krishna in such a way, having understood that he is a samambonam, the source of everything, including me, including my ability, including what I have, is coming from him.

#### service, devotion, maya

**Summary:** The speaker explains that a wise person understands that everything comes from the Lord and engages all their abilities in His service with love and devotion, emphasizing that we are always serving something, either the Lord or maya.

All my energies. When a person gets a paralytic stroke, he has a desire to move his hand, cannot move. We have been given an ability, it's there with us for a certain period of time, and when the time comes, that ability is withdrawn. So one who understands that, that everything what I have is coming from the Lord, he is the ultimate source, he with that understanding, a wise man, engages all what he has in the service of the Lord. And how does he engage? Buddha, bhava samanvitaha, with love, with devotion, with feelings, with the right understanding that he is the one whom I have to serve. Now somebody may say, you have been talking about serving the Lord, all what we have, we have to use in the service of the Lord, why? It is said that we cannot live without serving. If we don't serve the Lord, we serve, we serve maya. So Buddha, a wise man, voluntarily engages in the service of the Lord. Now how he is engaged? With bhava samanvitaha, with feeling, with love, with devotion.

One time, there was a devotee, who is now a devotee, he was from London, he happened to come to India in search of God. Generally, anybody who is spiritually oriented, they all have a feeling that, yes, India is a place where you get lot of yogis, God men, Himalayas, and all those things. So he happened to come to India in search of God. And then he visited many, many temples. And in all the temples, he would say, you know, there were, there were different deities, different Gods being worshipped. And he was a keen observer, because his objective was to figure out who is the Supreme and who is the Supreme Lord. So he went to Durga temple, and then Shiva Devi was there, sitting on a line, and then she had all those weapons, and she was killing a demon. And went to other temples, or Shiva temple, and Shiva is sitting, you know, with this Rudraksha bead, and all that, meditating. Like that, he went to different temples. And finally, having visited all the temples, including Aradha and Krishna temple, he concluded that Krishna is the Supreme Lord. Can anyone guess what was the basis of his conclusion? What did he observe? What did he see? This is which he concluded that, yes, Krishna is the Supreme. After having visited all the temples, what did he see which made him come to that conclusion? Okay. Anybody else? All the Gods were given some? Okay. Anybody else? Yes, Ritwik. He doesn't have any weapons. He has a revolver on the side.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Why Our Senses Fail to Reveal Truth | Sri Amitasana Dasa | ISKCON Bangalore Sanga
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### limitations of senses, spiritual knowledge, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Lxx7DF7xBbM?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Our scriptures are saying that we cannot perceive the reality with our senses because our senses, we all have four inherent defects."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the limitations of our senses in perceiving reality, citing scientific theories about extra dimensions and emphasizing the need for spiritual knowledge and surrender to overcome these limitations.

I'll share with you a quote from a very eminent scientist. He says, all the scientific theories do not dictate that there has to be only three dimensions. Although we can't see with our eyes or feel with our fingers, additional dimensions of space are a logical possibility. Our senses register only three dimensions. So, extra dimension might sound incredible. Even the dimension in the world we are living, the earth planet we are living, in Hyderabad city what we are living, what we are seeing is not necessarily complete reality. Forget about where we have not gone. But our scriptures are saying that we cannot perceive the reality with our senses because our senses, we all have four inherent defects. So, with this limitation, inherent defects in our senses will never be able to perceive the reality. So, when we get spiritual knowledge, we understand how small, insignificant we are coupled with humility. I am a small, insignificant Jeeva. I have limited sense perception. Can't even control myself. How can I control things? When that living entity develops that humility, surrenders to the Supreme Lord, surrenders to a bona fide spiritual master, OM AGYANATI MIRAN DASYA GYANANJANA SHALAKE, we open our eyes with torch of knowledge, we are able to sense some bit of the reality.

#### frustration, material world, impermanence

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker explains that our desire for permanence and enjoyment is constantly frustrated in the material world, which is a place of suffering (dukha alayam) and impermanence (ashashvatam).

Our desire to be the Lord, to be the enjoyer, to achieve permanence will always be frustrated because Krishna declares this material world is a prison house, is dukha alayam ashashvatam. Just like Himalaya, Himalaya means, Him means barf, snow, a place which is full of snow. So, granth alaya, a place which is full of granth. Anath alaya, a place where orphans are there. Dukha alaya, a place which is full of dukha. Now somebody says, no, no, you are saying dukha, you know, He is happy. Yes, between some dukha, some sukhah is also packed. But that sukhah is ashashvatam. It's temporary. So in that sense, it is like a prison house.

#### desire, suffering, knowledge

**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Our desire for that life is getting frustrated at every step."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses how our desire for a life of permanence, bliss, and knowledge is constantly frustrated, leading to suffering, and how a wise person questions the source of this suffering.

Our desire is to live a life of permanence, to live a life which is full of bliss, enjoyment, full of knowledge. And that's why Krishna says, our desire for that life is getting frustrated at every step. And we are getting frustrated after many, many births. A person in knowledge, a person who is little bit wise, he will say, why am I suffering? Why this happiness which I want to achieve is elusive? Why there is so much of struggle in this world? Actually, animals cannot figure out, neither they can ask these questions.

---
#### suffering, knowledge, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ufB-4WxO0oM?t=93
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Real knowledge means to understand, why am I suffering?"
**Summary:** The speaker explains that questioning suffering is not pessimism but a sign of seeking real knowledge, which leads to understanding one's identity and ultimately surrendering to Krishna.

Somebody, when we ask this question, why am I suffering? He is a pessimist. He is not strong. He is scared of life. He is a pessimist. Actually, it is not. Real knowledge means to understand, why am I suffering? Who am I? When a person starts, Bahu naam, Janma naam ante, Gyan van, a person who is in knowledge, who is sufficiently experienced, Maam prapadyaante. Krishna says, will come to me, will surrender to me. Now, this is not a binary logic. Zero or one. Surrender to means, we suddenly become a pure devotee. No. We pass through a stage where we become little awakened. We realize that yes, the Lord is there. Lord has to be glorified. Lord has to be served. I am not the Lord. The lord, I am the servant of the lord, we realize that but we are filled with material desires.

### With Our Polluted Mind How To Bring About Purification Of Consciousness
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the problem of practicing detachment with a polluted mind and highlights the inbuilt purification system.

Some fruitive, my mind is acting in that direction only, some owner, something I want out of it. So with polluted consciousness, how to practice this thing, how to bring this element of detachment? So this purification of consciousness is inbuilt in the system.

#### karma, false ego, enjoyment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0IK0mZBO3kA?t=12
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "After many times getting hit, finally I said, and now I realize I am not the enjoyer."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how the world is designed to backfire when one acts as an enjoyer or proprietor, leading to the realization of not being the enjoyer.

One thing is the world has been designed so beautifully by the Lord, when you act in a wrong way, it will backfire. When you want to act as an enjoyer, act as a proprietor, it will hit you back, even as devotees. That's why Krishna said, after many times getting hit, finally I said, and now I realize I am not the enjoyer.

---
#### devotional service, false ego, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0IK0mZBO3kA?t=52
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So we will start doing devotional service with that same consciousness, I am the enjoyer."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses how devotional service often begins with the desire for personal enjoyment and control, which is a natural starting point but not the ultimate goal.

So we will start doing devotional service with that same consciousness, I am the enjoyer. I want to enjoy peace of mind. I want to enjoy lording over bhaktas. We start with that. Cannot help it. Theoretically it will not help. And today onwards I will not enjoy. And today onwards I will not think I am the proprietor. It doesn't happen. It will be at a mental level.

#### devotional service, purification, humility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0IK0mZBO3kA?t=82
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Then you realize, my God, it's a fact, I am not the convincing."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of rubbing a diamond to explain how engaging in devotional service gradually purifies consciousness through repeated experiences of success and failure.

So we engage in devotional service, just like a diamond gets rubbed. So this rubbing will go on here also. So we will collect, we will think that I am a big collector. Finally you will get a hit. No, I am not the collector. You go for book distribution, one day you will distribute a lot of books. You get a feeling, I am good, I am very expert, very nice. See I convinced him. Next day you do all your convincing and nobody will buy a book. Then you realize, my God, it's a fact, I am not the convincing. This happens over a period of time.

And while we are engaged in devotional service, slowly, dhire dhire, dhire dhire, that purification happens. One comes to our senses. Unfortunately, to some extent you can fast track the process, otherwise it will take time. That's why it's a dhairiya, patience. You start with potency. You start with polluted consciousness. As we engage, the purification of consciousness is inbuilt in the system.

#### hearing, chanting, purification

**Verse References:** SB 1.2.6
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker explains that hearing and chanting (shravanam and kirtanam) are essential processes for removing impurities (abhadras) from the consciousness, using the analogy of polishing a diamond to illustrate the transformative effect.

For that, what we have to do, we have to, we have to, the process, shravanam, hearing, shunvata, swakataha, krishna, punya, shravana, kirtana, nashta, praya, shobhadreshu, nityam, bhagavata, sevaya. When we do that, all this abhadras, dirty things which is clinging to us, clinging to our consciousness, which is overpowered our consciousness, completely gets subsided. Just like diamond, it's actually a very precious stone, but when you see original, this thing, it is unpolished, uncut, it is not shiny, but because of that polish, because of rubbing, because of different treatment it goes through, it starts shining. Same is the case what happens with us.

### Worship of three types | BG 17.04 - 17.06 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### worship, diversity, understanding

**Summary:** Introduction to the lecture topic: the diversity of worship in India and the need for a fundamental understanding of the reasons behind it.

The topic is three kinds of worship. In India, we specifically notice that there are many types of temples, people worshipping different gods, different deities, different devatas, different festivals being observed. Every state has their own religious festivals. So in today's session, we will be discussing some of the fundamental concepts with respect to why so many gods, why so many religions. Although we have covered it at different times, nevertheless, it's very important for us to have a very good understanding of this subject. Dedicated to Srila Prabhupada, our founder and beloved spiritual master.

#### demigods, supreme Lord, empowerment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=52
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna very clearly demarcates or distinguishes between the Devatas and the supreme personality of Godhead, Bhagwan."
**Summary:** Explanation of the difference between demigods (Devatas) and the Supreme Personality of Godhead (Bhagwan), emphasizing that demigods are empowered representatives of the Lord.

Text 4th from the 17th chapter. Men in the mode of goodness worship the demigods. Who are the demigods? Devatas. Actually, in India, we have a concept that there are many gods and you can worship any one of them. Basically, you need something to worship. You need something as an object of your faith and whatever resonates with you, whatever you're comfortable with, you can worship and it's all one and the same. So in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna very clearly demarcates or distinguishes between the Devatas and the supreme personality of Godhead, Bhagwan.

---
#### Devatas, administration, living entities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=121
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Different Devi and Devatas are devotees of the Lord who have been empowered by the Lord to take care of different administrative affairs in this universe."
**Summary:** Analogy of Indian government ministers to explain how Devi and Devatas are empowered by the Lord to manage universal affairs, emphasizing they are also living entities.

Who are the Devi and Devatas? Devi and Devatas are empowered representatives of the Lord. Just like we all are Indians and some Indians by following a democratic process become cabinet ministers, some become state ministers, some become chief ministers, some become prime ministers. So they are Indians only like us, potentially we all can become prime minister of the country, we can become chief minister of the country, more so in present situation, deadlock. So different Devi and Devatas are devotees of the Lord who have been empowered by the Lord to take care of different administrative affairs in this universe. Like we have Sun God, we have Varuna, we have Chandra, we have all different Devi and Devatas. So they are also living entities.

---
#### liberation, material desires, empowerment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=199
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Devatas cannot give us liberation from this material existence. They can give us material benedictions."
**Summary:** Clarification that worship of Devatas can fulfill material desires but cannot grant liberation, using the analogy of a chief minister granting favors.

They are also Jeevatattva and worship of the Devatas is not equivalent to worship of the Supreme Lord. Devatas cannot give us liberation from this material existence. They can give us material benedictions. They can give us some, we have some desires to be fulfilled by worshipping different Devatas, they can be fulfilled. Material desires can be fulfilled because they are very materially empowered. Just like chief minister, if you are very close to him, if you want to, he can give you lot of blessings, lot of your work can happen because he is in an empowered position.

---
#### modes of nature, worship, ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=251
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Those in the mode of passion worship the demons. And those in the mode of ignorance worship ghosts and spirits."
**Summary:** Explanation of how the three modes of nature (goodness, passion, and ignorance) influence the type of worship people engage in, with examples of each.

So men in mode of goodness worship the Devatas. Those in the mode of passion worship the demons. And that's how you see different kinds of worship. Sometimes have you seen people kind of worship which is very very, people pierce through their tongue or through their face. How many of you have seen that? At least photographs, if not live. And then covered, smeared face with different kind and you know especially in Kumbh Mela, you see all kinds of Babas with all kinds of Vibhuthis, all kinds of ash, matted hairs. And those in the mode of ignorance worship ghosts and spirits. In India, there are places people go to remote villages and there are some trees which are said to be places where the ghosts are living and they worship them. Different kinds of black magic, this tantric kind of worship is practiced. So all kinds of situations are. So what motivates different people, it's the modes influencing them. If a person is predominantly in Satva Guna, he will be worshipping of one kind. His worship will be of one kind. If he is in Raja Guna, his worship will be of one type. In Tama Guna, another type.

---
#### modes of nature, transcendence, contamination
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=337
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even mode of goodness is better than passion, better than ignorance, but it's not the highest. It's a springboard by which we can jump to or move to transcendence."
**Summary:** Discussion on the limitations of even the mode of goodness (Satva Guna), explaining that transcendence (Shuddha Satva) is the ultimate goal.

Is Satva Guna worship the highest? So Krishna in the Gita talks about three modes. Satva Guna, Raja Guna and Tama Guna. And over and above, these three modes are the highest. One of the highest modes is Shuddha Satva. That's why he says Traigunya Vishaya Veda, Nishtraigunya Bhavarjuna. You rise above the flowery language of the Vedas. Even mode of goodness is better than passion, better than ignorance, but it's not the highest. It's a springboard by which we can jump to or move to transcendence. Transcendence means you transcend the mode. You cross over.

---
#### goodness, material motivation, ultimate goal
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=382
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Ultimate goal of religion is to come out of this material existence, cycle of birth and death, and go back to our original home, Kingdom of God."
**Summary:** Explanation that those in goodness worship Devatas with material motivations like Sukh Shanti and prosperity, contrasting this with the ultimate goal of religion: liberation and returning to the Kingdom of God.

Even Satva Guna is contaminating. So those who are in goodness, they will worship Devatas. They'll be very religious. They'll observe various ceremonies. They will get up in the morning. They'll do their pujas, but materially motivated. Predominant thing which is driving them is Sukh Shanti. Prosperity. And materially well situation. Which is not the ultimate goal of religion. What is the ultimate goal of religion? Anyone? Ultimate goal of religion is to come out of this material existence, cycle of birth and death, and go back to our original home, Kingdom of God. That's the goal.

---
#### agnosticism, confusion, contradictions
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=436
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Why so many Gods? Why so many religions? In the name of religion people killing each other and fighting and having all sorts of you know, unmentionable things happening in the name of religion. What is all this?"
**Summary:** Addressing questions that arise in the minds of agnostics regarding the multitude of gods and religions, highlighting contradictions and conflicts arising in the name of religion.

So now, we see people worshipping different Gods and visiting various places of worship. Yesterday, you know, the entire nation was engulfed with the news of Ayodhya verdict. And we have different temples, different Devi and Devatas being worshipped. Now, question might come in the mind of a person who is an agnostic. Who is an agnostic? Why so many Gods? Why so many Gods? Okay, either it could be because people are confused, no clear idea, so everyone is worshipping their own thing. Doesn't make sense. Why so many Gods? So many different kinds of worship. So is that kind of a, there is no concreteness, there is no clear understanding, everyone is coming up with their own understanding of God, own kind of worship. What is it? So why so many Gods? And a second fundamental question which can arise in the mind of a person, why so many religions? And that too, religions which contradict, the instructions of one religion might contradict with one. One religion says, okay eat meat. And they have some festivals which advocate killing of animals, like we have Bakri, that kind of thing. And there is another religion which says, don't eat meat. And in the name of religion people are fighting and killing each other. So person who is sceptic will say, kyaha hai? What is this confusion? Why so many Gods? Why so many religions? In the name of religion people killing each other and fighting and having all sorts of you know, unmentionable things happening in the name of religion. What is all this?

**Summary:** Discussing the confusion arising from multiple paths and gods, leading people to take the 'easy route' of accommodating everything without clear understanding.

So, another few questions which can be very perplexing, can be very bewildering is whom do I follow? There are so many types of God, so many types of worship. Whom do I follow? Whom do I believe? If you go to Christians, they will say Jesus is the only way. You come here and say Krishna. Yes or no? Everyone is a fanatic. Everyone says my way or highway. Whom do I believe? And everyone says it with a lot of confidence. And they quote too. In Bhagavad Gita it is said this, in Bible it is said that. Whom do I follow? Whom do I believe? And which God should I worship? And people take the easy route, they keep everything. That's why typical altar in an Indian house, you will find some 20-30. How many of us agree with this statement? There will be Lord Shiva, Lord Ganesha, Krishna, Laddu Gopal, Sai Baba, Puttaparthi Sai Baba, someone has given Shiva Linga, they will also keep it. Yes or no? So then the whole thing is, no one should get angry. So we take the easy route. This is also one of the reasons, I mean, okay you say, we don't have clear understanding, we keep everything. Those who are supreme in this will also be fine. Why we have to ascertain?

#### conversion, true understanding, one God
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=778
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "There is only one God. The definition of God is the Adi Purusha, the original person, the cause of all causes. When there was nothing, he was there."
**Summary:** Addressing the issue of religious conversion and asserting the true understanding that there is only one God, defined as the Adi Purusha, the cause of all causes.

We keep everything. Everyone claims that his path or his God to be the best. This is the problem and that's why people are converting. My God is better than your God. Better you worship my God. Christians are trying to convert others. They also want to make others Christian. They have their targets, they have their goals. My God is better than your God. And in the name of God, fighting. Now, the true understanding is just like we have one son, but called by different names in different languages, in different countries, likewise, there is only one God. The definition of God is the Adi Purusha, the original person, the cause of all causes. When there was nothing, he was there. Sarva Karana Karanam. So that being the understanding of God, the cause of all causes, the supreme person, the supreme being, the source of everything, the source of creation, the understanding is there is one and only one God.

#### different names, same truth, objective
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=866
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "All bona fide religions and scriptures, being word of that one God teach the same truth in essence. The ultimate objective of all religions is the same."
**Summary:** Explaining that the same God is addressed differently in various religions, and all bona fide religions teach the same truth with the ultimate objective of bringing us closer to God.

If that is being the so, the same God is addressed differently in different religions by name Allah, Father in heaven, Krishna, the supreme personality of Godhead, just like you have Surya, Ravi, Bhaskara and the same son in different languages, in different countries, in different religions being called by different names. All bona fide religions and scriptures, being word of that one God teach the same truth in essence. The ultimate objective of all religions is the same. The essence is the same. The principles are the same. What they are meant for is the same. The objective is the same. The purpose for which it is being propagated is the same. What is that purpose? What is the purpose of all religions? Irrespective of the details, details may vary from one religion to another. What is the purpose of all religions? What is the purpose of all religions? Bring us closer to God. Connect us with God. Now everyone is away from Lord or separated from Lord or distant from the Lord to different degrees, is forgetful of God to different degree. I am also forgetful of God, you are also forgetful of God, there are many others who are forgetful of God. The degree may vary.

---
#### degree, essence, laws
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=968
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "God is one, develop love for God, obey God's laws."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of mathematics knowledge to illustrate varying degrees of closeness to God, emphasizing the underlying essence of all religions: love for God and obedience to His laws.

Every one of us knows maths, but some people have done PhD in maths, some people have done elementary school, somebody else. Everyone knows 2 plus 2 is 4. You can say confidently, I know maths, but the degree varies. All religions are doing the same. There is the same work which is taking people closer to God, but the fact is all of us are away from God to different degrees and hence the instructions vary. So the essence, what is the underlying essence of all religions? God is one, develop love for God, obey God's laws. Every religion talks about, there are some laws. Sin no more. Everyone talks about. Be serious, be truthful, be honest, do not sin. Glorify the name of Lord. This is also in different religions, in different forms, it's there, Allahu Akbar. Render service to God, seva. Every religion has that. Because all these things, whatever we talked about, sin no more, chant the names of God, render service to God is to bring us closer to God. Return to the kingdom of God. So that ultimately we come out of this cycle of birth and death, this material existence and return back to our original home. That's the objective. Why Gita life? There's an objective, there's a purpose to spread the message of Gita. Likewise religions have an objective which is that to bring people closer to God. However, God gives his message. He gives his message. He gives his message. He gives his message. Directly or through his representatives, according to time, place, circumstances and according to the capacity of individuals to receive it. This brings the difference. God is one, the message is one, essential principles are the same.
