**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The same message is given by God and his representatives because the audience are in different time, different places, different circumstances and the capacity to understand is different."
**Summary:** Explaining why there are so many gods, religions, and forms of worship: the same message is adapted to different audiences with varying capacities for understanding.

But why so many gods? Why so many religions? Why so many forms of worship? Because the same message. The same message is given by God and his representatives because the audience are in different time, different places, different circumstances and the capacity to understand is different. And hence you have plethora of instructions. Apparently contradictory instructions. Details vary from religion to religion. Essence is the same. Objective is the same. But because. Different people. Different people are different. Somebody is predominantly in Tamoguna. Somebody is predominantly in Rajoguna. Somebody is even lower than Tamoguna. Almost like animals. Barbaric. Atheist.

#### instruction, understanding, circumstances
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=1147
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Teacher instructs according to level of understanding of students. Likewise, different religions teach the same principles, but according to time, place and circumstances."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of a math teacher instructing students at different levels to explain how religions adapt their teachings to the understanding and circumstances of their followers.

So, there is some instruction for them. So, Prabhupada gives a very good example. In elementary school, a child is taught very basic addition, subtraction, multiplication. So, Prabhupada gives a very good example. In elementary school, a child is taught very basic addition, subtraction, multiplication. 2 plus 2 is 4. 2 plus 3 is 5. 3 minus 2 is 1. Now, if the child asks the teacher while being taught some subtraction, 3 minus 2 is 1. 5 minus 2 is 3. What is 2 minus 5? The child asks. What will the teacher say? Teacher will say, it is not possible. In elementary school, teacher who has done B.Ed, who is quite well versed, knowing that the child does not know negative numbers, time, place, circumstances and the level of understanding, teacher says it's not possible. And the teacher will tell also, if you have 5 apples, how can you give 8 apples to someone? So it is not possible. The same teacher in high school will say it is minus 3. And the same teacher when it addresses students in, let's say at graduation level, will talk about even advanced concepts of mathematics. So all levels are being taught Maths by a Maths teacher. But the level at which the Maths is being administered, the lessons are being administered is different. Teacher instructs according to level of understanding of students. Likewise, different religions teach the same principles, but according to time, place and circumstances.

**Summary:** Explaining that while typical worship is for material gain, Kunti Maharani prayed for calamities to remember Krishna, highlighting a higher level of devotion.

Why I am saying this, while I am saying this, I am aware of the fact it may sound fanatical, just like. People say Bible is the ultimate thing, Jesus is the only way, it may appear like that. Now to substantiate what I am saying, I'll give you couple of examples. In the Bhagavatam, typically when we go to God, when we pray, when we go to the temples, why do we go to temples generally? When we pray, typically, a typical mentality for praying, going to God, worshipping is what? Why do we do that? What's the objective? For some material gain. For some material gain. For some material gain. For some fulfillment of our desires. For peace. Am I right? Is there any other reason why we worship? Before exams, before elections, some problem has come, your flight is about to, you are stuck in traffic and your flight is about to miss. That time we worship God. So, Kunti Maharani is praying, you see the level of instruction in the Srimad Bhagavatam, that's how I am saying that the level is different. Kunti Maharani is telling Krishna, when Krishna, they got the kingdom back and Krishna is living for Dwaraka, sorry Krishna is leaving, and she says, I wish that those calamities would happen again and again. For we can see you again and again. For we can see you again and again. Who worships God for calamities? Who worships God that yes, I may get problems? Kunti Maharani, knowing that ultimate goal is to get freed from the cycle of birth and death, for which we have to remember the Lord. He says better let the miseries be there because miseries are an impetus for us to remember you. When we have good time, everything is comfortable, we are in the kingdom, now there is no troubles, no demons attacking us, we will forget you. What is the use of that kind of prosperity? So better let there be more and more calamities so that we can remember you. You see typical all so called religious people are worshipping so that our calamities go away. So that we become peaceful and we enjoy life. When there is a problem in our enjoyment, we bring God, worship a little and put him aside. Isn't it? It's a use and throw kind of a thing. Our plans for enjoyment is independent. When some intervention is required, we call him, use him and put him in the side.

Lord Chaitanya, he is saying, O Lord, I have no desire to accumulate wealth, nor to enjoy beautiful women, nor do I want any number of followers. Na dhanam na janam na sundarim kavitam ba jagadishya kaam aye. O Jagadisha, kaam aye means I do not desire, that's not my objective, that's not what I am worshipping you for. And then what I want is your causeless mercy of devotional service. In my life, birth after birth. It doesn't matter where I take birth. All I want is opportunity to serve you, opportunity to remember you. See how these instructions are different from typical religious understanding you have in the society. Typical religious this thing is, I want money, I want wealth, I want name, I want fame, I want followers. He is saying, I don't want all this. All I want is your bhakti. Let my Lord trample me or break my heart by not being visible to me. He can do whatever he likes, but still he alone is my worshipable Lord unconditionally.

#### habits, habit cycle, reward
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B5ZGqlvV19Y?t=1816
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "In this book, he talks about the habit cycle, how the habit gets formed."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the concept of the habit cycle (cue, routine, reward) from Charles Duhigg's 'The Power of Habit,' using brushing teeth as an example.

Facebook is an addiction. Some of us may not be aware of it. There is a book written by Charles Dweck. The name of the book is Power of Habits. In this book, he talks about the habit cycle, how the habit gets formed. So, we have a cue or a trigger that triggers us to take some action. And that action, let's say we call it routine. For example, when we get up in the morning, we go to the bathroom, there is a trigger. We put up a toothbrush, put the paste. We are not conscious of it and we do the routine of brushing our teeth. And in the process, we get a reward. What is the reward we get when we brush our teeth in the morning? Freshness. Gush of freshness. Therefore, it has become a habit. We get up in the morning. We go to the bathroom. It's a cue. It's a trigger. The moment we step into the bathroom, even if we are very sleepy, even if we are very tired, we will pick up a toothbrush. We will pick up a toothpaste. Even if we are sleepy, we will put it there and start brushing our teeth. That's a routine. And the reward what we get is the freshness. And because we get this reward, we look for this trigger that is next day in the morning. And because we do this repeatedly, it becomes a habit.

Now, why we are doing it? We have an answer. Hi guys, Dr. Berg here. In this video, I want to talk about this very interesting thing called dopamine. Dopamine is a neurotransmitter which is a hormone-like communication or message that travels through the nervous system versus hormones are communications that travel through the blood. And this neurotransmitter has the effect of giving you pleasure. So it's a pleasure neurotransmitter. It does other things that are involved with reward and even motion. So if you have a problem with dopamine deficiencies, you might have resting tremors and Parkinson's, which is a different thing. But it's mainly pleasure. It's a feel-good hormone. It also gives you that vitality. So if you could imagine, not that you ever took cocaine, but the sensation of taking cocaine, what you would feel like, this heightened pleasure, you know, ability to conquer everything. It's only because cocaine and other drugs stimulate dopamine. So the actual physical feeling that you have is coming from dopamine, not really the drug. The drug is only the trigger that creates more dopamine in the body. So my thought is, why can't we just increase, make sure this is high naturally, right?

So why a person is checking 2,617 times in a day? Why? Of a pleasure. Suppose somebody has sent a message. What has he sent? Something I have posted. How many people have viewed it? How many likes I have got? When I see that, I get a pleasure. Actually, it's not the social media. It's the dopamine which gets released. And I get habituated to that release of dopamine. I crave for that happiness chemical. It becomes a craving. Digital distraction works in a similar way. That's why I played the video of trapped. Are we in the process of getting happiness somehow unknowingly, unintentionally getting trapped? It's very scary. So we have a cue. We have a routine. A mail comes in a mailbox or a message comes. We want to check it. There's a reward. There's a feel-good factor. Somebody has liked my post. And there's a craving for distraction

#### addiction, habit, anticipation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B5ZGqlvV19Y?t=2209
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "When a smartphone vibrates with a new notification, the brain starts anticipating the momentary happiness that opening WhatsApp Facebook provides."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how the brain anticipates happiness from notifications, leading to habitual phone checking.

Now there's a craving for distraction. If I don't check my phone, I feel something is missing. When a smartphone vibrates with a new notification, the brain starts anticipating the momentary happiness that opening WhatsApp Facebook provides. It starts anticipating. It's a queue. It's a trigger. And because we have done it many, many times, what has happened? It has become a habit. A notification came. We know that we should not be checking it. We know that I have scheduled time for it. But it has become a habit. Habitual response. Habitual response is I pick up my phone, swipe it and check what it is. A question basically of what information Facebook's collecting, who they're sending it to, and whether they were asked me in advance my permission to do that. Is that a fair thing for a user of Facebook to expect?

#### Facebook, dopamine, addiction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B5ZGqlvV19Y?t=2280
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The thought process was all about how do we consume as much of your time and conscious attention as possible?"
**Summary:** The speaker shares insights from the former president of Facebook, highlighting how the platform is designed to consume time and attention through dopamine hits.

We didn't take a broad enough view of our responsibility, and that was a big mistake. And it was my mistake. And I'm sorry. I started Facebook. I run it. And I'm responsible for what happens here. If the thought process that went into building these applications, Facebook being the first of them, to really understand it, that thought process was all about how do we consume as much of your time and conscious attention as possible? And that means that we need to sort of give you a little dopamine hit every once in a while because someone liked or commented on a photo or a post or whatever. And that's going to get you to contribute more content, and that's going to get you more likes and comments. It's a social validation feedback loop. Turn it off. That's what I would say. It's hard for young people now because they're hooked. They're addicted. If you don't think you're addicted, and I'm talking about anyone from the highest to the lowest, if you don't think you're addicted, then see if you can turn it off for a week. It got quiet in here, didn't it? Didn't it get real quiet? It's a tool, so we should use it. God has blessed us with free will. Now it's free will magnified, free will on steroids. You're free to go in any direction you want. It will allow you, and it's not the enemy. It's just a reflection of our own free will. And we all want to be liked,

#### digital world, dopamine, validation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B5ZGqlvV19Y?t=2390
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "We start seeking that pleasure, and it becomes a routine, it becomes a habit."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the long-term effects of excessive involvement in the digital world, seeking pleasure through dopamine release and validation.

If we are too involved in the digital world, seeking that pleasure, which we know is a chemical called dopamine, every time we have a validation feedback, somebody liked our post, somebody commented on what we posted, or liked our picture, it's a validation, and it gives us pleasure. We start seeking that pleasure, and it becomes a routine, it becomes a habit. And that's how we keep checking our phones again and again, again and again.

---
#### brain function, neuroplasticity, habits
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B5ZGqlvV19Y?t=2574
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** scientific
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The brain can and does change throughout our lives. It is adaptable, like plastic."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the concept of neuroplasticity, explaining how the brain can change throughout life and how habits are formed through well-traveled neural pathways.

Now this is how the brain works before we understand how to go about handling this challenge. Not so long ago, many scientists believed that the brain did not change after childhood. That it was hardwired and fixed by the time we became adults. But recent advances in only the last decade, now tell us that this is simply not true. The brain can and does change throughout our lives. It is adaptable, like plastic. Hence neuroscientists call this neuroplasticity.

---
#### neuroplasticity, rewiring, change
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B5ZGqlvV19Y?t=2616
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** scientific
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "This process of rewiring your brain by forming new connections and weakening old ones, is neuroplasticity in action."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on neuroplasticity, explaining how new pathways are formed and old ones weakened through repeated actions and thoughts.

How does neuroplasticity work? If you think of your brain as a dynamic, connected power grid, there are billions of pathways or roads lighting up every time you think, feel or do something. Some of these roads are well travelled. These are our habits. Our established ways of thinking, feeling and doing. Every time we think in a certain way, practice a particular task, or feel a specific emotion, we strengthen this road. It becomes easier for our brains to travel this pathway. Say we think about something differently. Learn a new task or choose a different emotion. We start carving out a new road. If we keep travelling that road, our brains begin to use that road, and use this pathway more, and this new way of thinking, feeling or doing becomes second nature. The old pathway gets used less and less and weakens. This process of rewiring your brain by forming new connections and weakening old ones, is neuroplasticity in action.

---
#### neuroplasticity, habits, dopamine
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B5ZGqlvV19Y?t=2687
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "With repeated and directed attention towards your desired change, you can rewire your brain."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the ability to change habits through neuroplasticity, highlighting the importance of persistence and replacing old habits with new ones that produce dopamine naturally.

The good news is that we all have the ability to learn and change by rewiring our brains. If you have ever changed a bad habit, or thought about something differently, you have carved a new pathway in your brain, and experienced neuroplasticity first hand. With repeated and directed attention towards your desired change, you can rewire your brain. When we do something repeatedly, it creates neural pathways in our brain. The same action which was very difficult earlier, becomes easy to do. It's because of the pathways. That's why they say practice makes a man perfect. We have examples of eminent personalities who play well, who sing well. It's just that they have, if not in this life, previous life, as a result those pathways are there. So, when we check our phones quite often, it can be an addiction. We can have the neural pathway formed in our brains, and it can become difficult to change. But the good news is, with persistence, with conscious effort, we can form new pathways, and change our habits. By constant practice of replacing the current habits with new ones that produces dopamine naturally.

---

And it is not only with this habit, there are many other habits which are basically, we fall free to those habits or get addicted because of that feel-good factor, the dopamine chemical which gets released. By repeated performance of new habit, new neural circuits get formed, replacing old habits. Meditation for de-addiction and natural happiness. We all are pleasure seeking being, nothing wrong about it. We all are seeking pleasure. Anandamayo Bhyasat. Because we are not experiencing natural pleasure, intrinsic pleasure, we are seeking pleasure in the external world. And, that is the only definition of pleasure and many of those pleasures are addictive. So when we, tap into that internal source of pleasure, the reservoir of pleasure which is within us, we don't have to be like beggars, seeking pleasure from the external world.

#### meditation, mind control, focus

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public

Now, a question is, which meditation to practice? There are many many kinds of meditation. Meditation is a very generic term. I am sure all of you would have heard about meditating on candle light. Meditating on some point. Different forms of meditating on the breath, incoming and outgoing breath. Basically the mind which is chanchala, which is completely out of our control, which is just jumping like a monkey from one thought to another. We have to focus that mind, on a particular thought, on a particular object, on a particular thing, so that it starts getting tamed. That's the basic generic understanding of meditation. There are many meditations, where we can achieve that purpose of focusing our mind, let's say on a particular object, or a particular, let's say point. Pardon.

## HG Atma Tattva Dasa

### Sri Tattva Darshan Dasa: A Disciple’s Journey from age 12 to 70 | Sri Gaura Mandala Parikrama
**Speaker:** HG Atma Tattva Dasa

#### renunciation, absolute truth, spiritual seeking
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=0
**Verse References:** BG 7.16
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "At the age of 12, somehow or another, I rebelled against the system which I was brought up into."
**Summary:** The speaker describes his early life, starting at age 12, his rebellion against the material world, his search for the absolute truth, and his renunciation of wealth.

I guess it began when I was 12 years old. Now I'm 70, and without knowing in this life, without the knowledge of what is Krishna consciousness, what was Srila Prabhupada, at the age of 12, somehow or another, I rebelled against the system which I was brought up into. I became a vegetarian. I started searching for the absolute truth. The Bhagavad Gita tells us that three types of persons come to Krishna: those who are seeking money, those who are in distress, and those who are seeking the absolute truth. So I don't think I fell into the category of those who were seeking money, because I had a lot of that, and I gave it all up. It was self-made, but some help from the parents and the family. And distress, yes, we were all in distress in this material world. We didn't know which way to go, what to do, our seemingly happiness with our material life, but actually seeking the absolute truth. I was looking in the mountains, I was looking in the forests, I was looking in the swamps, I was living in the ghettos with the black people, I was prompting with kings, I was living rich, I was living poor, I was traveling from place to place, with vows of poverty, trying to understand. And I met what I could call my Vartma Pradaksha guru.

---
#### initiation, devotee association, Krishna's arrangement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=124
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So they said, when you come to Krishna, you should bring an offering."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts meeting Baba Ramdas, being introduced to the Hare Krishna mantra, meeting Vishnu John Swami, reading Back to Godhead magazine, and eventually meeting devotees in Phoenix and offering fruits and flowers to Radha Damodar.

He was the one, Baba Ramdas. He wrote a famous book called, Be Here Now. He had journeyed to India. Anyways, he introduced me to the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra indirectly. And I did that, and we traveled as we did, many people did in that time, among the hippie communes and things. And probably first heard of Prabhupada way back in 1968, when he was in San Francisco. And like this, life went on and on. And the travels led me to a bus that was in a house that I was staying with. We were all, a lot of people staying in a house in the house. The bus had Vishnu John Swami in it. And he's camped out in our house in Fort Lauderdale. And we did some park, we did some kirtan with him. And he was only there a night, so there wasn't much, but what is this, right? And they gave me a book, a Back to Godhead magazine, which I threw on the bed and it went underneath the water bed, later it came out, and I dried it and I read it. And it was a great book. And, of course, chanting Hare Krishna. Then again I met him by the will of providence, meeting devotees in different places, not really understanding, in rock festivals and in downtowns and book distribution, but already chanting Hare Krishna, but not really knowing anything about the Krishna consciousness movement. Then again it really began. I met Vishnu John Swami in 2012. I met him on Krishna Goswami in a bus, in a park in Phoenix, Arizona. So they said, when you come to Krishna, you should bring an offering. So that day we went out with a station wagon, big cars with the back of it. We went all over the fruit orchards and the flowers and the dates, and we picked them from everywhere around Phoenix, which is a famous growing area, and we filled up the whole car. And we brought it to the park. That's it. The park where Vishnu John Swami was. And we offered it to the deities of Radha Damodar there. So then Mr. John said, you've made this offering, you have to come now, back to the temple with us. So we went back to the temple and I stayed a couple of days with them. They were leaving for India, early days. This was probably the beginning of 73 by that time.

---
#### surrender, transformation, early devotee life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=286
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I'm from the temple of the world."
**Summary:** The speaker describes how Krishna took him from New Orleans to Miami, his transformation into a devotee, his initial struggles with chanting, and his commitment to stay in the temple.

Yeah. And, uh, so that began the journey very shortly, Krishna took me, he took me out of the house of the rising sun, which is a famous place in New Orleans, we won't get in any details. And he transferred me to Miami by airplane with a few bodyguards. And I got to Miami and I had an intimate relationship with it. My first devotee there, his name was Maha Sringa, he was Bhakta Mike then. He looked at me. By this time I had transformed from a long haired hippie with hair down to here. And I had on a dhoti, which was made out of a sheet. I had some beads which were tied up into knots by a rope. I had a sika that was funny shaped, it came down to my back, later became matted. Yeah. And, uh, I had some toothpaste which I had used to make tea lock. And I came from a jailhouse where I was only for 24 hours and the devotees were coming in, they had been picked up for distributing books. And they were coming out, they were getting immediately bonded. And they looked at me, I looked kind of funny, you know, I didn't really look like your regular Hare Krishna. And they said, what temple are you from? I said, I'm from the temple of the world. And they said, where are you going? And my brother was there with some of my friends and they said, he's coming with us. We have a party for him, he's been gone for some time. And I said, no, I'm going with them. And they said, yes, we'll be out in 10 minutes, you come with us. So then Maha Sringa's Bhakta Mike is there. He looks me over. Hmm. What kind of animal is this, you know? And he gives me a dhoti, he gives me a kurta, he gives me some toothbrush, toothpaste. That's all I was. That was it. There was nothing else. And he gave me a mat, it was in the Brahmachari ashram. And he goes, that way's east. It was 10 o'clock at night. Everybody was sleeping. I had no idea which way was east. That's what it meant. That's where you put your head when you sleep. So that began. And then I said, okay, I'll stay here 30 days and give it a try. Never left. That was in 73. So still to this day, trying to be a devotee, I haven't yet become a devotee, but I'm trying. Right? So they asked, when did you become a devotee? I haven't become one yet.

---
#### chanting, book distribution, service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=471
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Prabhu, you're cheating."
**Summary:** The speaker shares his initial misunderstanding of chanting rounds, his experience with book distribution, and his natural inclination towards farming and gardening.

So during the years, many things, like was funny thing in the very beginning, I was, I thought that one round was once and back again. I thought that was one round. So for the first month I was chanting 32 rounds, right? And I thought this is hard and everybody was finishing before me. Then one day I'm watching this Brahman, he's chanting and he gets to one round and he pulls down the bead. I watched him again. He did it again. He had his beads out too. I said to him, Prabhu, you're cheating. He said, what do you mean? I says, you're counting half a round as one round. So then he explained to me and then chanting 16 rounds became a little easier. And then after a couple of weeks they said, you should go out and try book distribution. And, uh, I said, okay. Okay. And, uh, some of our, some of the first devotees that are still with me in Krishna consciousness to this day, like Abhirama, Adridharana, Mahashringa, they were all there. We were a big band in Miami. So they said, you got to go out and try book distribution and you got to put on this wig, put on a hat, a wig, put on these Garmi clothes, this thing, and you got to go out and distribute books. I said, I'll go out, but I'm not going to do all this stuff. I said, I've changed my life now. So I went out with my Sankirtan leader and four or five other devotees and they were all out there and our friend Adridharana was there, but he was dressed up a little bit and he would point to people and he would say, we're distributing for India and collecting money for people in India. And here's one of the people from there that was so grateful to us. He came back to help us out because I was wearing a dhoti. So the very first day I went out, I collected more than my Sankirtan leader. I distributed more books and collected more money. So they told me, Prabhu, this is your propensity. This is what you have to do. But I also had an inclination toward farms and gardens and cows.

---
#### guru, initiation, obedience
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=603
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You must protect the cows."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts his desire for a personal initiation from Srila Prabhupada, his efforts to please Prabhupada, and his first direct instruction from Prabhupada to offer obeisances.

So I said, this isn't all I can do, right? And Srila Prabhupada had given me three personal instructions that I consider personal instructions. One was to offer obeisances. That was the first one. That happened in Philadelphia in the airport in 1976. I went there to New Vrindavan for initiation three years after I joined, two and a half years after I joined, because I didn't want a rittvik initiation. I didn't want it. I wanted Prabhupada to hand me my beads. Prabhupada was doing rittvik from the early 70s, right? And I kept asking my temple. The temple president thinks I want to go where Prabhupada is and I want to take initiation. We'll do by letter. We'll do this, that. You have to stay here and distribute books. That's what pleases Prabhupada. And we were developing gardens for some of the temples and I was sending Prabhupada coconuts and nim twigs and everything every day when he was touring around America. We'd climb up the trees and get them in Miami. So Prabhupada was coming. I went to New Vrindavan. I had a Sankirtan party. We had our own party. I was the leader. And I went to New Vrindavan and I told the Sankirtan party, you know, you guys go back where you came from. I'm staying here until Prabhupada comes. You can't. You're leaving the temple. I said, I'm leaving. I'm waiting here for Prabhupada. I'm not going to go for this anymore, you know? So they went back and I waited and Prabhupada came and he gave me initiation personally. That went really well. So when I first saw Srila Prabhupada, the very first time was when he came into the airport in Pittsburgh. You got to imagine the airport's a big circle like this. And on the top, the stairs come out and people come out from the different planes from the different side, right? So for months before Prabhupada's coming, Prabhupada's coming, Prabhupada's coming, he even asked, why is it every new temple, every temple I go to smells like fresh paint? Everything would always be ready. And that was the mantra, Prabhupada's coming, Prabhupada's coming. And we were just totally absorbed in it. Everything we did was. We're going to meet Prabhupada, Prabhupada's coming here. So there was a tumultuous kirtan in the airport. We could do kirtans at that time in the airport. And the Radha Damodar parties were there, the traveling parties, a lot of people because it was new Vrindavan. It was a big gathering. And of course there were some other passengers and airport officials around. So finally Prabhupada walks out. We're right down there in the middle of the kirtan, right in the center. Prabhupada walks out. And. Yeah. When he walked out, the whole kirtan stopped. Everybody hit the ground and did obeisances. But I didn't. I'm looking, Prabhupada, oh my God, he's come. And he goes like this. Looked straight at me and I was the only one, like this. So that was his first instruction was to offer obeisances.

---
#### cow protection, guru's instruction, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=790
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You must protect the cows."
**Summary:** The speaker describes his second personal instruction from Srila Prabhupada during a morning walk in New Vrindavan, emphasizing the importance of cow protection.

And the second instruction I got with him was on a morning walk. I was tending the cows. I was tending the cows in New Vrindavan and helping to build A-frames and do different things. And we were living up in the old Vrindavan farm. And Prabhupada came up. I went on many morning walks in Darshan. But in this morning walk, I'm standing, there's a lot of pictures, I'm right next to Prabhupada. And I'm herding the cows, so I got some boots on and got a t-shirt on instead of a dhoti and everything. I got a dhoti, but that, so I'm herding the cows. And this one cow kept hitting Prabhupada like this. She was a Friesian, she was a Friesian cow, black and white. She kept nudging Prabhupada. And Prabhupada was affectionate, pat her on the head and he would smile. And then Prabhupada turns to, and he says, how old is this cow? Hari Sori says, she's 10 or 12 years old, Prabhupada. I said no. I said, Prabhupada, I'm her cow herd. She's only four years old and her name is Kalindi. And she's just started giving milk. And Prabhupada looked at me directly in the eyes and he says, you must protect, he didn't say we should protect, he said, you must protect the cows.

---
#### dancing, devotional service, guru's pleasure
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=875
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Dance."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts his third personal instruction from Srila Prabhupada, where Prabhupada encouraged him to dance despite another devotee's attempt to stop him.

Second instruction. Third instruction, I like to dance. And I thought maybe Prabhupada would give me a name, something to do with dancing. And the day before initiation, we were in a Ratha Yatra in Cleveland, but Prabhupada couldn't go there. So it came in the front page of the paper, the Rathkart with Lord Jagannath there and everything. And somehow in the paper, in the front of the paper, I was dancing in front of the Rathkart, both feet off the ground, my hands up in the air and my Chatur flying. That came on the front of the Cleveland Press. And I was told later by Kulik Shekhar, Prabhupada's disciple, he said, oh, that devotee dances like Lord Chaitanya. Right? So I thought that Prabhupada maybe would give me some Nataraja, I didn't know, right? So we were outside the barn going into Radha Vrindavan Chandra's temple and we were dancing like everything. And a couple of times, Prabhupada looked at me and smiled, right? It was in Brahmachari cloth, there's pictures of that too. And you come into Radha Vrindavan Chandra's temple and there's a hallway and two devotees can be on each side. And then in the middle, Prabhupada would walk. So I was jumping up and down, I was right there as Prabhupada would come in just a few feet and I was dancing like anything and Prabhupada was ready to come in. And this devotee Charu, he pushes me down, he says, no, you may hit Prabhupada, you can't dance here. I said, I have to dance. And he said, no, you can't dance. And he pushed me the second row behind. And he said, no, you can't dance. And he pushed me the second row behind. And I said, Charu was his name. He later had some different qualities. So Prabhupada walked in and all the devotees were like this. They were a small kirtan. Nobody was really dancing when he walked in the hallway, right? Prabhupada walked over, right next to me, he stopped, he turned, he looked me in the eyes and he raised both hands, one had a bead bag and one had a cane. He said, dance. And he wasn't there when the devotee pushed me behind, but he was there outside. And he had seen that picture of Cleveland Ratha Yatra. So that was the third instruction I got from Srila Prabhupada.

---
#### sense gratification, Krishna consciousness, offering
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=1011
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna consciousness is like this rose."
**Summary:** The speaker shares an analogy Prabhupada gave about sense gratification being like salt and Krishna consciousness being like a rose, illustrating the importance of offering everything to Krishna.

Those were direct instructions. And then one instruction I thought I got from Prabhupada was we were in a garden with Prabhupada. He was giving a darshan, and he had a rose in his hand. He had just finished telling us that sense gratification is like salt. You have to have a little bit or you'll die. And he described the six Goswamis, how they had to take a pat of butter, a little bit of salt, or otherwise they would die too. And how they had to sleep one hour or two hours. They had to eat with a pat of butter. And he said, and for defense, they were always dependent on Krishna. And for mating, he said, there was no question. Then he had this rose. And there was one rose with smell, too. So Prabhupada had this rose. And he said, Krishna consciousness is like this rose. He says, if I have this beautiful rose, and I think, let me give it to Radha Vrindavan Chandra, Prabhupada smiled. He says, I'm going to Vaikuntha. Then he put down again. He says, but I have the same rose. And I think, let me give it to my girlfriend he goes i'm going to hell and he throws the rose and i was right there and the rose came right in front of me but this other devotee grabbed it right i didn't get the rose he took it and i didn't know whether papa was telling me that if i offer roses to robert johnson i'm going to vaikunth or i'm going to hell i didn't know which one and i'm still wondering so that was another indirect instruction.

there's no difference so many devotees had a lot of this is all the association i ever had with prabhupada never had more and you so many things have come from the books whenever we have a question #### spirit soul, matter, energy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k4VXR5266c?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "And what is it? It is the spirit soul."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the concept of the spirit soul as a superior energy exploiting matter, referencing the law of conservation of energy to support the idea of existence after life.

And Krishna says in the next sloka that there is a superior substance and matter, a superior entity. And what is it? It is the spirit soul. So the superior energy is one which is exploiting this matter. And it is, the name given by the Lord is Jitabhutam. And if the soul is energy, then the law of conservation of energy should hold good for the soul too. So that explains that yes, we do exist after life.

---
#### reincarnation, body, soul
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k4VXR5266c?t=66
**Verse References:** BG 2.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is saying yes, you exist after life, after old age, after death. You exist. And you go, you pass into another body."
**Summary:** This segment explains the concept of reincarnation based on Bhagavad Gita, highlighting the transformation of the soul into different bodies and contrasting it with the limitations of scientific understanding.

And Krishna makes it more clear in the second chapter. In fact, the Bhagavad Gita starts from here. Second chapter, thirteenth sloka. Dehino asliyatha dehe ghaumaryam yoganam jara. Krishna is saying yes, there is a matter. Deha is the matter. And there is a Dehi, which is energy, superior energy. And this energy goes under transformations in different phases. Ghaumaryam, yoganam, jara. What happens next? What happens after jara, after old age? Ghaumaryam said the end. He didn't say the end. He says Dehantara. There is a transformation. Next transformation is a new body. Tatha Dehantara praptir. Krishna is saying yes, you exist after life, after old age, after death. You exist. And you go, you pass into another body. Scientists got stuck here. They understood till here whatever Krishna was saying. Yeah, there is a matter, there is energy. This whole world is a combination of matter and energy. This part they cannot see. Tatha Dehantara praptir. They have no idea how it happens. Only Dheeras they understand and they don't get stuck. Dheeras tattva namuhyati. Not everybody will understand or everybody will appreciate.

#### ignorance, soul, perception
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k4VXR5266c?t=184
**Verse References:** BG 15.10
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Vimudanas cannot see this. Foolish people cannot see this."
**Summary:** This segment discusses why scientists and learned individuals are unable to perceive the soul's presence in the body and its departure at death, attributing it to their foolishness (Vimudana) as described by Krishna.

So why scientists got stuck here? Why they are not able to understand this also? There are so many learned people in the world. So many engineers. So many doctors. So many PhDs. They don't understand. And that also Krishna explains. Why they don't understand. Krishna says in the 15th chapter. Very clearly he says why they cannot see. budhkramantam sthambapi bhunjanam vagunanam guranvitam vimudana anupasyanti pasyanti jnani chakshusah. That's why Prabhupada says raska. Krishna used the word. What to do? Prabhupada speaks as it is. What other Krishna says, as it is. That's why Prabhupada says raska. Krishna is using the word. Vimudana. Muda means foolish people. Vimudana means too much. That's why they cannot understand. Utkramantam means how the soul leaves the body. Utkramantam means coming out. Emanating out. They cannot see. Sthitam. How the soul is currently staying in the body. That also they cannot see. There are doctors called cardiologists. Heart surgeons. They open the heart. They see everything. They can't see that the soul is sitting there. Sthitam. They cannot understand. Similarly when they are doing the surgery and suppose something goes wrong and the patient dies. They don't understand what goes wrong. Utkramantam. They can't see the soul leaving the body. The heart or body, whatever. They can't understand. Why? Prabhupada says raska. Because Krishna says. Vimudana anupasyanti. Vimudanas cannot see this. Foolish people cannot see this.

#### soul, perception, scriptures

How the soul is staying in the heart in this matter. But how it is experiencing the interactions of three modes. They cannot see. Finally when it leaves the body that also they cannot see. There is a book called Coming Back. In that book there is a letter by a heart surgeon. He wrote an open letter in the newspaper that I want to understand what is going on. And somehow one of the disciples of Prabhupada brought that newspaper to Prabhupada. That there is a heart surgeon and he wants to know. Because he had done so many heart surgeries. He imagined in that letter that I have experienced the life passing between my fingers. But he has no idea. Then Prabhupada wrote a letter back to him explaining what soul is, what are the attributes and how to perceive it. How to perceive it? Pasyanti Gyan Chakshasa. Pasyanti Gyan Chakshasa. By the eyes of Mahesh. Time of the word is Shastra Chakshasa. By the help of scriptures. That's where everything gets stuck.

#### senses, observation, limitations
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k4VXR5266c?t=420
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So how the product of this eye which is faulty and which is limited can be perfect and without limit."
**Summary:** This segment explains the limitations of scientific understanding due to its reliance on observation through faulty and limited senses, contrasting it with the potential of scriptures.

Because the whole process of scientific discoveries or understanding is based on observation. When I was in school, I remember in my science class, science when we go to the lab, we have a lab manual we used to write there. The first thing we write is observations. It starts with observation. Then comes the theory, then comes the experiment, then conclusion. And all our observations are limited by our senses. They are called Gyanendriyas. Knowledge acquiring senses. So whatever we know about our surroundings is because of these Gyanendriyas. They capture the information around the surrounding and that's how we get them out. And they are limited, so our observations are limited. They are faulty, so our observations are faulty. We cannot see everything with the eyes. And with the same eyes, we design telescopes. With the same eyes, we design microscopes. With the help of this eye which has so many limitations and so many faults. So how the product of this eye which is faulty and which is limited can be perfect and without limit. That's where scientists get stuck.

#### scriptures, absolute knowledge, limitations of science
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k4VXR5266c?t=532
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But whatever the fortunate souls have learnt from the scriptures billions of years back is absolute. Today and in the past and even in the future. It's not changing."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the significance of scriptures as a source of absolute and perfect knowledge, contrasting it with the limited and ever-changing nature of scientific knowledge.

And that's where the significance of the scriptures come into place. Something which you cannot perceive by your senses can be perceived by the scriptures. By this transcendental knowledge. By this transcendental knowledge. Because this knowledge is absolute. This knowledge is perfect. This knowledge is beyond limits. Whereas what scientists can tell us is limited because the senses are limited. Their observations are limited. And imperfect. That's why the new scientist comes and he says, no, whatever the previous scientist said is wrong. And this keeps happening after every 50-100 years. My grandfather, if he could have gone to university, and may he never bend. If he could have gone to university, he might be learning, my great grandfather might be learning whatever Dalton told. But the atom is indivisible. The most fundamental particle. But I didn't learn the same thing. I learned something else. No, there are subatomic particles. Electron, proton, neutron. Now next generation are learning something else. They are learning, there are sub-subatomic particles. They are learning about quarks. Future generations will be learning about interesting theories. And I don't know what else theory will come. What else they will be learning. And they will be telling me, after some 3rd generation, if they happen to meet us, they will tell, whatever you learnt is wrong. Pushpa will be just, they will say like this. If I happen to meet my great grandfather, I will say, whatever you learnt from Dalton is wrong. But whatever the fortunate souls have learnt from the scriptures billions of years back is absolute. Today and in the past and even in the future. It's not changing. That's why it's called absolute knowledge. It's not relative. Relative in perspective of time. Or in perspective of different places. It doesn't change. It always remains the truth.

Okay, so Krishna explained that yes, we exist. There is afterlife. The ahamdara happens. Only problem is, Muras cannot see it. But Gyan Satsusa can see. Okay, so there may be afterlife. But, what kind of afterlife? We just pass on. We just pass away. Or we come back. Or we pass away where we go. Or we take a U-turn and come back. That is still the question. #### rebirth, karma, desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k4VXR5266c?t=761
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "What Parikshit Maharaj is trying to do is, how not to come back. And what most of the people in this world is trying to do is, how to come back."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the two possibilities of afterlife: coming back (Aabhrittim) and not coming back (Anabhrittim), illustrating the common desire for rebirth with an analogy of hidden gold.

So there are two words used in Bhagavad Gita. Aabhrittim and Anabhrittim. Aabhrittim means, after you pass away, you come back. Aabhrittim. Comes. Anabhrittim. Not come back. So there are two possibilities. You may come back, or you may not come back. What Parikshit Maharaj is trying to do is, how not to come back. And what most of the people in this world is trying to do is, how to come back. You know, in villages, sometime back, my grandmother was telling, they used to keep gold in one pot, earthen pot. And they used to tuck the ground, and put that pot there. And, maybe nobody is aware that the home and the person passed away. And their next generation may not be knowing that there is a gold down. They may never have decked up. And this person might have kept with this intention, and next life he may get it back. So everybody is planning on to come back. But we all know, he will not get back. Somebody else will take over. So let's understand, what is Anabhritti and what is Abhritti. In what situation somebody can come back, in what situation somebody doesn't come back.

#### demotion, promotion, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k4VXR5266c?t=870
**Verse References:** BG 8.24-26
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You can come back, by being demoted, or you can come back by being promoted."
**Summary:** This segment explains that coming back (Abhritti) can happen in two ways: demotion for those who don't follow scriptures and promotion for those who do, setting the stage for further explanation.

In 8th chapter of Bhagavad Gita, it says that there are two options, coming back or not coming back. Jagdha sasvade mathe Ekaya, Ekaya means the first option. Ekaya yati anabhritti The first option, the first process is, you will not come back. Anabhritti Anaya, Anaya means the second one, the other one. Avar tehe punha, you will again come back. So there are two processes, two paths. The first one, is make you sure that, make sure that you don't come back. Another one is, make sure that you come back. Now this coming back, happens in two ways. One is called demotion, another called promotion. You can come back, by being demoted, or you can come back by being promoted.

#### demotion, lower species, karma

**Verse References:** BG 16.19-20
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** This segment details coming back by demotion, explaining that those who disregard scriptural laws are reborn into lower species of life with lower consciousness.

In the 16th chapter, Krishna talks about, coming back by demotion. So you may come back, but, you will be demoted, in the next life. In which case, it happens, it happens, when you don't follow the laws, of the scriptures. If somebody doesn't follow the laws, of the scriptures, in this life, they will again come back, but with demotion. Adamaam gathiyam Asunim Asunim means low grade. So those who don't follow the laws, of the scriptures, they will again come back, but, in a low grade. Some lower species of life. A lower consciousness. But there are some people, who follow the laws, who follow whatever scriptures say. They do lot of karma tanda, whatever rituals are mentioned.

They will go to higher planets. They will go to higher planets. Surya Naloka means, the planet of Indra. How? By following, the instructions of the scriptures. Because, scriptures also gives the instructions, to the people, who want to come back, and enjoy. There are so many people, who want to come back, so, scriptures give those, do's and don'ts, and that is called, karma tanda. So, they will enjoy. In the higher planet. Higher level of comforts. So, this is, the two examples of, abhirti, coming back. And this explains, why there is so much disparity, in this world. Lot of people tells me this. If there is a God, why there is so much disparity, in the world. Why there are people, who don't have any money, any food. And why there are people, who have so much money, and so much food, that they can't even, enjoy that. Too much. Now, you got the idea, why it is like this? Because somebody, who follows the laws, in this life, they will have, they will have lot of resources, lot of luxury, in the next life. Those who don't follow, they may not have. They may be poverty stricken, their consciousness may be low, they may not be so intelligent.

#### disparity, karma, free will

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** This segment attributes the disparity in the world to the law of karma and the free will of living entities, clarifying that God is not to blame for the consequences of their choices.

So, you got the idea, from where the disparity comes? Because there are two ways, of coming, coming back, in this world. Abhirti. Either by, coming back is Abhirti. Either by demotion, or by, promotions. If you have done good karma, you followed the laws, next life, you will be promoted. With lot of resources, intelligence, looks, knowledge, better consciousness. Because there are two ways, of coming back. But somebody may not get, those resources. So disparity is because, what we have done, in our past life. God should not be, playing for it. But what people do, they say, where is God? There are so many people, dying because of, so many sicknesses, critical illness, where is God? God is the one, who made the system. You want to get demoted, you can get demoted. He made the law of karma. And he gave a free will, to the living entities. Tatha ichchasi tatha kuru. Whatever you want to do, you do. Law of karma will take care of it. So, people come back, with different, kind of backgrounds. And that's why, there is disparity, in this world. So, who should be blamed, for it? The living entity, himself, and not the Lord.

#### transcendence, maya, liberation

**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** This segment introduces the second path, Anabhritti (not coming back), explaining that it is achieved by transcending the three modes of material nature and becoming situated in Brahman, thus escaping the grip of Maya.

Now, the second option, the second path, is called, anabhirti. Means, we will not come back. So, the second option, the second path, is called, anabhirti. In what situation, a person will not come back? Any idea? Just. If somebody is doing karma, he will be promoted. The karma will be demoted. If he does a karma, then he will go to the spiritual world. Good. A karma. In simple language, anybody who transcend, the three modes, of material nature. They will still be acting. But their acting, will neither promote, or demote. It will do, anabhirti. 14th chapter, Krishna mentions this. maam cha yohad vichare, na bhakti yogena sevate, sa gunaan samtite tan, brahma puyaya kalpate. They will transcend, they will transcend, the three modes. And they will be situated, in the Brahman. Then what happens? Then they will not be under, the grip of maya. Dehvi hi esa gulmayi, man maya duratvaya, maam eviye pripadyante, mayam etam tadantite. They will be out, of that scope of maya. The erudition of maya.

But it looks very difficult, how to do this. If we have to transcend, the three modes, it must be very tough, isn't it? And it must take, lot of time. You have to read, so many Vedas. You have to do, so many yagyas. Lot of tapasya. But Krishna says, no, not really. Krishna says, If somebody becomes a yogi, then he will get the result, of all these things, by default. You have to study, the Vedas. No need to perform, the yagyas. Or execute, tapasya, austerity. Just because, they become a yogi. They get, all these results, plus, they get, paramsthanam, the ultimate destination. Paramsthanam, bekti chadyam. They get, all these results, plus, they get, paramsthanam, the ultimate destination. **Key Quote:** "The best yogi, is one, who keep me, in his heart, who keep me, in his thoughts, in his consciousness, mat gaten antaraatmana."
**Summary:** This segment defines who a yogi is according to Krishna, clarifying that it's not necessarily someone with a skinny body in the forest, but rather one who keeps Krishna in their heart and consciousness.

How to become a yogi? Becoming a yogi, must be very tough. Must take, lot of time. Krishna says, no. Who is yogi? Krishna explains, who is yogi? Because, what, image of the yogi, we have is, different, what Krishna says, as a yogi. When, we think of a yogi, in our mind, what image comes? A person, with a skinny body, not eaten from so many, once, sitting under a tree, closed eyes, maybe half closed eyes. No, that's not what Krishna says, or Krishna mean by yogi. Krishna says, yogi naam api sarve saam, mat gaten antaraatmana. One, who thinks about me, who keeps me, in his consciousness, who keeps me, in his heart, antaraatmana. Sadhavan bhajade yomaam, saame yukta tanho mataa. The best yogi, is one, who keep me, in his heart, who keep me, in his thoughts, in his consciousness, mat gaten antaraatmana. To become that kind of yogi, you need not to do, such a big tapasya, sitting somewhere, in the forest, without food and water, no. Prabhupada says, just follow, my instructions, and you will become, the best yogi.

## HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

### Are We Part of the Drama of This World? | Sri Chanchalapathi Dasa | SB 2.5.26-29 | 19.07.2024
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### creation, elements, transformation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=0
**Verse References:** SB 2.5.26-29
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The whole process of creation is an act of gradual evolution and development from one element to another reaching up to the variegatedness of the earth."
**Summary:** The speaker reads verses from Srimad Bhagavatam describing the transformation of elements in the process of creation.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya. Reading from Srimad Bhagavatam, Canto 2, Chapter 5, Verses 26-29. I will read myself. Reading from Srimad Bhagavatam, Canto 2, Chapter 5, Verses 26-29. Because the star is transformed, the air is generated with the quality of touch. And by previous succession of the air is also full of sound and the basic principle of duration of life, sense perception, mental power, and bodily strength. When the air is transformed in course of time and nature's course, fire is generated, taking shape with the sense of touch and sound. Since fire is also transformed, there is a manifestation of water full of juice and taste. As previously, it also has form and touch and is also full of sound. And water being transformed from all life on Earth appears knew in order and as previously becomes qualitatively full of juice, touch, sound and form respectively.

---
#### creation, elements, sense perception
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=149
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The qualities of sense perception are fully represented in the earth and they are manifested in other elements to a lesser extent."
**Summary:** The speaker continues reading from the Srimad Bhagavatam, elaborating on the qualities of elements and their relationship to sense perception, emphasizing the role of air and the benefits of yogic exercises.

Aged 15 years. Burokar Katanyankaha, from thesavravabhyan gods, Baba Akram, Maharaja Sakyamundrya. Based on this bogotan image, light, the world and consciousness are not turned towards one conspicuous feeling effected by the devils. Avarikasat jna kya ajanya jna jnakatseya? As we will. The whole process of creation is an act of gradual evolution and development from one. HomКyaka kttur. The whole process of creation is an act of gradual evolution and development from one element to another reaching up to the variegatedness of the earth as so many trees, plants, mountains, rivers, reptiles, birds, animals, and varieties of human being. The quality of sense perception is also evolutionary, namely generated from sound, then touch and form, from touch to form, taste and order are also generated along with the gradual development of strife, air, fire, water, and earth. They are all mutually the cross and effect of one another, but the original cross is the Lord himself in plenary portion as Maha Vishnu lying in the trouser water of the Mahat Tattva. As such, Lord Krishna is described in Brahma Samhita as the cross of all crosses, and this is confirmed in the Vajra Dhrita 10.8 as follows: The qualities of sense perception are fully represented in the earth and they are manifested in other elements to a lesser extent. In the strife there is sound only, whereas in the air there are sound and touch, in the fire there are sound, touch, and shape, and in the water there is taste also along with the other perceptions, namely sound, touch, and shape. In the earth, however, there are all the above mentioned qualities with an extra development of order also. Therefore, on the earth there is a full display of variegatedness of life which is originally started with the basic principle of air. Diseases of the body take place due to degenerate, due to derangement of air within the earthly body of the living being. Mental disease result from special derangement of the air within the body, and as such yodit exercise is especially beneficial to keep the air in order so that diseases of the body becomes almost nil by such exercise when they are properly done. The duration of life is also in traces and one can have control over death also by such practices. A perfect yoti can have command over death and treat the body at the right moment when he is competent to transfer himself to a suitable planet. The birthday yodi however surpasses all the yodit yodis because by dint of his devotional service he is promoted to the region beyond the material sky and is placed in one of the planets in the spiritual strike by supreme will of the Lord, the controller of everything. So.

#### creation, drama, roles
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=372
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We are all the living entities, actors, and we have to play our role, some as men, some as women, some intelligent men, some foolish men, some intelligent women, some not so intelligent women, all kinds."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a film director and actors to explain the roles of Lord Brahma and living entities in the creation, emphasizing that we are all actors playing different roles in this cosmic drama.

The process of creation is being described in this chapter. The chapter is called the cause of all causes. So in this verse we are seeing today how sky is transformed, air is transformed, air is generated and air has the some of the qualities of touch and sound and so on. So actually this is a description and then we saw how from air, air and other different perceptions also come about. Suppose let's say I'm a film director and I'm planning a film, and in that film I want some of you to become the king, some of you to become the Queen, some of you to become warriors, some of you to become an invader villain who will come and do all nonsense, all wrong things, and some of you soldiers. And so now in filmmaking there is the one important step after the script, casting all that is done, that's called set design because it's a story of a king, queen, all this, we need a palace, we need a battleground, we need horses, we need elephants, we need soldiers. Next as a film director I'll start planning all of this so that when we turn on the lights and all of you can act out very correctly. King will play his role, Queen will play her role, and then the invader will come and all the different things will as per the story will unfold. So in the same way, Lord Brahma who is the director of this universe, creator of the universe, he is doing the set design as we read in the Bhagavatam. So we are all the living entities, actors, and we have to play our role, some as men, some as women, some intelligent men, some foolish men, some intelligent women, some not so intelligent women, all kinds. And for this drama to unfold, we need homes, we need buildings, we need plants, trees, planets. So Brahma is creating all of this.

#### drama, illusion, identity

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Very rarely people think, they are all busy with their roles, they have to play mother, father, husband, wife, and they want to play it out nicely so that everybody is impressed with the role I am playing."
**Summary:** The speaker poses the question of why we are part of this cosmic drama, explaining that although it feels real, it is temporary like a cinema, and people are generally preoccupied with playing their roles without questioning the purpose.

So a fundamental question is, why are we all part of this drama? But this is like a, this, okay, that's like a drama, cinema is all right, but this looks like real life. This doesn't look like drama. No, actually it is a fact. What is a drama, what is a cinema? After some time, the curtains will close and the lights are turned off and drama ends. So even in our lives, one day curtain will be drawn, lights will be turned off, our drama will end, but it doesn't appear like that. It looks like real, you know, this is my wife, this is my husband, this is my home, this is my father, this is my mother, these are my children, this is my house. Why all of this has come about? These are deeper questions. One is you are a king, play properly your role as a king, you Queen play properly Queen as a role as a queen, do your proper, be a strong king, all that is one thing, but a very fundamental deeper question is why have I become an actor in this drama? But very rarely people think, they are all busy with their roles, they have to play mother, father, husband, wife, and they want to play it out nicely so that everybody is impressed with the role I am playing. We rarely think why this drama is going on and why have I become part of this drama, what is all this?

We are the spirit soul, we are playing the role of a man, of a woman, of an animal, of a bird for some time and then the drama ends and then we will be an actor. After he finishes one drama he'll get commissioned to do another drama so we will get another opportunity, we will go to another situation. Now the Vedic literatures provides a very very significant perspective why have we become part of this drama, what is this going on and that is we are all spirit souls, we take on this body and the body has male body, female body, animal body has designed by a by Lord Brahma, the creator of all this. Why has the soul become entrapped like this and playing this different role?

#### Krishna, desire, independence

**Summary:** The speaker explains that we are all amshas of Krishna, like sunlight is to the sun, but unlike sunlight, we have developed a desire to be independent of Krishna, which is an unconstitutional desire.

Very nicely Prabhupada has written in the purport, previous purport. As an Amsha, we are all spirit soul. Amsha of Krishna. Just like there is sunlight. Sunlight has come into the room but some minutes back it was in the sun and it has come all the way and traveled here. Isn't it? We understand that. Now can the sun, sunshine, sunlight, is it possible for the sunlight to be independent of the sun? Can sunlight exist without the sun? Not possible at all, isn't it? Constitutionally, it is not possible. The sunlight is an emanation of the sun. In the same way, we are all living entities. We are all conscious entities coming from Krishna. We are all living entities. We are all conscious entities coming from Krishna. We are all conscious entities coming from Krishna. We are all conscious entities coming from Krishna. But, this conscious entities have developed one desire. Sunshine has no desire. It is just light energy. Whereas, we are also energy, Jeeva Shakti of the Lord. And this Jeeva Shakti has developed a desire, I want to be independent of Krishna. I want to be independent of Krishna. I want to be independent of Krishna. I want to be independent of Krishna. Krishna. Just like sunlight has come from sun, we are also Jeeva Shakti coming from Krishna, the Shaktimaan. But the difference is that is a inert, lifeless energy, light energy, heat energy. Whereas we are a Shakti which has got Chetana, consciousness. And this Chetana Shakti, Chit Shakti has developed a desire because it's Chetana. Chetana means has desire, has thinking, feeling. And this Chit Shakti has developed a desire, I must be independent of Krishna.

#### illusion, independence, unconstitutional desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=1129
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This desire of the living entity to be independent of Krishna is called unconstitutional desire."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the unconstitutional desire to be independent of Krishna, comparing it to a dream where sunlight exists without the sun, and explaining that Krishna creates the illusion of the material world to give us the impression of independence.

It is not possible. Sunshine cannot be independent of the sun. It is an emanation of the sun. This desire of the living entity to be independent of Krishna is called unconstitutional desire. It is not constitutional. It is not by our makeup. How we are composed, how we are formed, what are we made of? We are made of Krishna's energy. We have come from Krishna. So we can never sever that. But we have developed a desire. I want to be independent of Krishna. This is possible only in illusion. For example, we can think of sunshine without sun in our dream. We can say last night I had a dream, you know, there was so much sunlight. I looked up at the sky and I didn't see the sun at all. But there was sunshine. Yes, such a thing is possible in illusory dream. Dream will go away. It's a temporary existence in the night. And when we wake up, it's all gone. Right? In the same way, Krishna through Brahma creates an illusion of this world for the living entities to give an impression that we are independent of Krishna.

We are here in the material world. But among the living entities here, some of them have some God consciousness. So they recognize God, they go to temples, they worship, they follow some religion, some Dharma. But they follow religion, they follow Dharma. But they follow religion, they follow Dharma with an aim that God will bless me so that all my various desires I have with my family, with my profession, with myself, I can fulfill all those desires with God's blessing. This is the large population of the society thinks like this. Recognize God. But I have my desires and I should fulfill my desires, my ambitions with God's blessing. But then the reality is that we are a spirit, chetana shakti, coming from Krishna. We rarely think, what is Krishna's plan for me? What does Krishna want me to do? I have come into this world, I am playing different roles. But our spirit of independence is such that I am here, I am independent, I have my independent desires and ambitions. And God is there, I want God's blessing to fulfill my independent desires. You see, I want to be independent is manifested in the spirit of I want to have independent desires.

That's what we pursue. We have our plans. We get inputs from the society, from newspapers, from the heroes of the world, from different sources. And we form our ambitions and we have to do that. This is the kind of an independent desire that we want to have. Because we want this desire, the independent desire, the independent existence, which is unconstitutional. Krishna creates an illusion in the material world. Gives us different roles and gives us an opportunity to play out our role, trying to be independent. That is the situation of the material world. That is what is happening here. So we take on different roles. I am man, I am woman, this is the family, this is my business. I must have a house, I must have big house. I must have big car, I must have big bank balance. And we work for all these things. Rarely we think, if we are all children of God, what does God want us to do? When we start thinking, what does God want? Can I know that? Can I align myself with God's intention? That is called Sharanagati. That is the next level of recognition of God. In the first stage, where we recognize God, I have my independent desires, I have my independent plans. I want God's blessing so that I can succeed in my own plan. This is also recognition of God, but a relatively very minimal recognition. God as a supplier of my dreams, as a fulfiller of my dreams. My dreams. It's my dream. And God shall kindly fulfill my dreams. See, this is the large population of the world, in every religion. This is the consciousness of the living entity.

#### Krishna, surrender, relationship

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We must surrender to Krishna. We must align our desires, our plans, our activities, with Krishna's activity."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the next level of recognizing God, which involves surrendering to Krishna and aligning our desires with His plan, leading to the understanding of our true relationship with Him, contrasting it with the temporary relationships in the material world.

The next level Krishna teaches in the Bhagavad Gita, in the Vedic literatures, is that, if you are part and parcel of me, Krishna. We are all part and parcel of Krishna. We have come from Krishna. We are an energy of Krishna. What does Krishna want me to do? What is his plan for me? If I am his. If I am his. If I am his. So, this is how Bhagavad Gita ends, by bringing the concept of Sarva-Dharman-Paridhyajya. Ma mekam sharanam vraja. We must surrender to Krishna. We must align our desires, our plans, our activities, with Krishna's activity. And then what happens? Then, Bhagavatam describes further, after sharanagati, after you align, you will come to understand, you have a relation with Krishna. Relation. Relation is a very powerful thing. Just like somebody is a mother, there is a child. The mother is always conscious, I should take care of my child. And child also, playing around a little bit, if some difficulty, some danger comes, mummy, she will run to the mother, and hold on to the mother. That's the meaning of relationship. There is activity. There is feelings. So, now we, next the Vedic literatures inform us, we have a relation with Krishna. What about all these relationships in the material world? This is a drama. It's only a drama relationship. No substance. Just like in the dream also, we sometimes have so many relationships, so many activities. When we wake up in the morning, all the relationship, all the activities has no meaning.
