#### Srimad Bhagavatam, divine appearance, offense
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JGJcFBuz4gk?t=42
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord did not want further aggravation and further more something more should not happen to his devotees, the doormen."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the Srimad Bhagavatam verse describing the Lord's arrival with Lakshmi to address the offense committed by the doormen against the sages.

Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta purport. We are reading in the Srimad Bhagavatam, in this part, about the two doormen stopping the sages. And the sages becoming angry and we just saw in the previous verses, they cursed that they should go down to the earthly planet. And we also saw that the two doormen very graciously accepted the curse that was delivered to them. And in today's verse, we are seeing how the Lord responded when he was aware that this was going on at the seventh gate of his Vaikuntha. The Lord did not want further aggravation and further more something more should not happen to his devotees, the doormen. So, the Lord arrived there.

---
#### verse analysis, lotus feet, devotee attraction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JGJcFBuz4gk?t=180
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Walking on those lotus feet, which is sought after by the great transcendentalists and the great sages, the Lord arrived there."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the verse, focusing on the Lord's awareness of the offense, his arrival with Lakshmi, and the significance of his lotus feet being sought after by elevated transcendentalists.

And so here, the svanam sadatikramam vibhudhya. Svanam, his own servants, sadatikramam, they had, atikramam, they had committed an offense. They had gone beyond what they should do. Atikramam. Towards whom? Sadatikramam. Towards the great sages, the four sages. Sadatikramam, svanam sadatikramam vibhudhya. The Lord became aware of that. And, immediately, the Lord tasmin yaya. And, tasmin yaya, how did he go there? Charanau chalayan yaya. With his own feet, he arrived there. Sahashrihi, along with Laxmidevi. Now, what is the Lord's lotus feet like? There are, there is an interesting adjective here. Paramahamsa mahamuninam anmeshaniya charanau. Anmeshana, means to search out or to seek after that lotus feet. So, the lotus feet which are sought after by whom? Mahamuninam, Paramahamsa mahamuninam. Paramahamsa means the most elevated transcendentalists. And, mahamuninam, and very very great sages, transcendentalists, great sages, they seek out that lotus feet. Walking on those lotus feet, which is sought after by the great transcendentalists and the great sages, the Lord arrived there.

---
#### verse analysis, divine qualities, devotee delight
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JGJcFBuz4gk?t=304
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord who is the delight of the righteous, who is Aravindanabha, and whose lotus feet are sought after by Paramahamsa Mahamuninam Anveshaniya Charanah Chalayan Sahashrihi along with Lakshmi Devi, he arrived there."
**Summary:** The speaker continues explaining the verse, highlighting the Lord's qualities as Aravindanabha and Arya Hridyaha, emphasizing his delight to the righteous.

And, another expression that is used here is Arya Hridyaha. The Lord who is Arya Hridyaha, the Lord who is Aravindanabha, Bhagavan Aravindanabha, one who has a lotus in his navel, referring to the Garbhakshayi Vishnu, and from the lotus sprouts, from his navel sprouts the lotus on which Lord Brahma is situated and all the lokas are in the stem of the lotus, so he is known as Aravindanabha. And here he is also described as Arya Hridyaha. Arya, righteous, Prabhupada has used that word, Hridyaha, the delight of one who is attracted to. So, Arya Hridyaha, the Lord who is the delight of the righteous, who is Aravindanabha, and whose lotus feet are sought after by Paramahamsa Mahamuninam Anveshaniya Charanah Chalayan Sahashrihi along with Lakshmi Devi, he arrived there.

#### devotee concern, civilization, progressive values
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JGJcFBuz4gk?t=434
**Verse References:** BG 2.1
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Human life is meant for progressing in values in understanding and one who is conscious of this that human life we can achieve something which is very special which is not possible in other living entities."
**Summary:** The speaker transitions to discussing the Lord's concern for his devotees and introduces the concept of 'Arya' as a civilized person who understands the progressive values of life, contrasting it with modern superficial definitions of civilization.

So, very beautiful verse. Describing how the Lord so concerned about his devotees and what was happening in the Vaikuntha and he personally arrived there immediately along with Lakshmi Devi. So, there are some very interesting points here from the verse. The Lord here is described as Arya Hridyaha the delight of the righteous. This word Arya Prabhupada also describes in other places. Arya refers to a civilized person. Aryan refers to civilized. In the Bhagavad Gita when this word comes up in the second chapter Prabhupada explains the word Arya is one who knows the progressive values of life. So, human life is meant for progressing in values in understanding and one who is conscious of this that human life we can achieve something which is very special which is not possible in other living entities. So, human life and human consciousness is very special and so we must attain that higher understanding. One who is conscious of this they are called Aryans one who is conscious of the progressive values of life.

---
#### civilization, consciousness, taste
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JGJcFBuz4gk?t=548
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "A person has to be judged by his tastes. What are his tastes? What is he attracted to? What is he what delights him?"
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the Vedic understanding of a civilized person with the modern materialistic view, emphasizing that true civilization is defined by refined consciousness and elevated tastes.

Unlike in today's world you see who is called a civilized man? A man who is wearing a nice suit and a tie and a boot and going in a big car and living in a big house and these kind of things he is considered to be a very civilized man. That is what is thought of. But actually in the Vedic culture it is not just your attire. So, somebody may be in those kind of a very polished attire but in his consciousness may have some other kind of a very lowly understanding. So, that is not important. What is important is Arya Hridyaha and so the yardstick the test that is used here what Srila Prabhupada is setting forth is Arya Hridyaha a person has to be judged by his tastes. What are his tastes? What is he attracted to? What is he what delights him? You see. So, Arya Hridyaha means those who are very civilized who know the progressive values of life who have a refined consciousness who have actually refined their consciousness and how do we know they have refined their consciousness? Because their tastes have changed. That is the test. What is it that delights them? What is it that they are attracted to? What is it that they go after? So, that is an indication of the level of consciousness of the person.

#### consciousness, attraction, transcendental Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JGJcFBuz4gk?t=674
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They are not some lowly material objects of this world but the highest level of consciousness they are attracted to the transcendental Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the concept of 'Arya', explaining that it refers to someone whose consciousness is evolved, leading them to be attracted to the transcendental Lord rather than material objects.

So, Arya refers not simply external dressing and those kind of things but somebody who is evolved in his consciousness and hence his likes his what engages him what attracts him those objects have changed and they are not some lowly material objects of this world but the highest level of consciousness they are attracted to the transcendental Lord. And so, the Lord is known as Arya Hridyaha. In that sense Arya Hridyaha means those Lord who is the delight of the most civilized people. So, the Lord is the delight of the most civilized people. Most enlightened people. That is why Krishna says itimatva bhajante maam budha bhavasamanvitah itimatva bhajante maam budha bhavasamanvitah. They are enlightened Buddha and they are bhavasamanvitah their heart is filled with bhava for the Lord because they are enlightened and they know who is the Lord what is Krishna's position He is the source of everything. So, this is the position of the Lord.

**Summary:** The speaker analyzes a verse from the Bhagavad Gita, explaining that while abstaining from sense objects may suppress desires, only transcendental experience can eliminate the taste for sense enjoyment.

It's a very interesting verse the verse talks about two things niraharasya dehinam niraharasya niraharasya one meaning of the word nirahara means not eating food ahara nirahara so not eating food but here niraharasya refers to negative restricting principles negative principles don't do this don't do this vishaya vinivartante niraharasya dehinam rasavarjam rasopyasya here the niraharasya dehinam one who is abstaining following all of these negative restrictions niraharasya dehinam dehinam a person who has an embodied person a living entity niraharasya dehinam vishaya vinivartante rasavarjam vishaya vinivartante vishaya vinivartante means he is abstaining from the vishayas that's what the meaning of niraharasya he is not supplying the vishaya vishaya refers to indriyavishaya so he is abstaining from vishaya vishaya vinivartante niraharasya dehinam rasavarjam only vishaya vinivartante rasavarjam vishaya meaning rasa other than rasa rasavarjam means apart from the sense of enjoyment in other words the sense of enjoyment is still there so he may be niraharasya dehinam vishaya vinivartante but rasavarjam vishaya vinivartante he is still attracted to that rasa for enjoying the vishaya is there other than that other than that he is abstaining from the vishaya but rasopyasya paramdrishtva what is that paramdrishtva nivartante rasopyasya paramdrishtva nivartante but when you do paramdrishtva when you have the transcendental experience raso api nivartante even the desire even the sense of enjoyment goes away so it is not that he is only abstaining strictly on the basis of certain principles that no I must not one he has one who has developed paramdrishtva rasopyasya paramdrishtva nivartante rasavarjam rasopyasya paramdrishtva nivartante.

### Srimad Bhagavatam | HG Chanchalapathi Dasa | SB 3.15.38 | 26-02-2016
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### seeing the Lord, devotional service, maturity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "For ordinary persons the Lord is not visible, however when one can understand the significance of his holy name and one engages himself in the devotional service of the Lord, beginning with the tongue, by chanting and tasting Prasad, then gradually the Lord reveals himself."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the verse and explains how the Lord, initially unseen by ordinary eyes, becomes visible to pure devotees through devotional service and maturity in their love for God.

In this verse we find the word Achakshat Akshavishayam. The Supreme Lord cannot be seen by ordinary eyes, but he now became visible to the eyesight of the Kumaras. Another significant word is Samadhi Bhagyam. Meditators who are very fortunate can see the Vishnu form of the Lord within their hearts by following the yogic process, but to see him face to face is a different matter. This is only possible for pure devotees. The Kumaras therefore upon seeing the Lord coming forward with his associates, who were holding an umbrella and a chamara fan, were struck with wonder and they were seeing the Lord face to face. It is said in the Brahma Samhita that devotees being elevated in love of God always see Shama Sundara, the Supreme Personality of Godhead within their hearts, but when they are mature, the same God is visible before them face to face. For ordinary persons the Lord is not visible, however when one can understand the significance of his holy name and one engages himself in the devotional service of the Lord, beginning with the tongue, by chanting and tasting Prasad, then gradually the Lord reveals himself. Thus the devotee constantly sees the Lord within his heart, and in a more mature stage, one can see the same Lord directly as we can see everything else. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Parbat.

---
#### appearance of the Lord, paraphernalia, description
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=120
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** description
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They were carrying appropriate paraphernalia when the Lord appeared."
**Summary:** This segment describes the appearance of the Lord with his associates, highlighting the paraphernalia they carried, such as an umbrella decorated with pearls and chamara fans creating a pleasant breeze.

So we are seeing in this part of the Srimad Bhagavatam, the sages were upset with the two doorkeepers and they cursed them. Just then the Lord appeared along with his consort Lakshmi Devi. We saw that in the last verse and in this verse further, the Lord appeared with his associates and all of them carried many paraphernalia. Swapumbhi, his own personal associates, Pratihruta Aupayikam. They were carrying appropriate paraphernalia, Agatam, when the Lord appeared. And they, the sages, Achakam, Achakshata, the sages now saw Vishnu. Achakshata, Akshavishayam. Now he had become an object of their sight. Achakshat, Akshavishayam, Achakshata, Akshavishayam. They saw the Lord who has now become a subject matter of their sight. Akshavishayam, Svasamadhi Bhagyam, Svasamadhi Bhagyam. Formerly they had seen the Lord, but was in samadhi. Svasamadhi Bhagyam, so in their deep meditation and in their meditation they had seen Samadhi Bhagyam, Svasamadhi Bhagyam. And how did he appear now? The associates were offering chamar, Vya janayaha, Vya janayo, and there was a cool pleasant breeze coming from the chamar. So chamar should be done nicely. So there was a cool pleasant breeze, Shiva vayu, Vayu, Shiva vayu, very pleasant breeze and the two, they were offering them on either side and they looked like Hamsashriyo, like two white swans on both sides as the Lord was coming.

They were also moving gently and it looked like Shashi Shikara Ambhum. #### majesty, opulence, associates
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=456
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When the Lord appears, He appears with His consort most of the time, and with His associates."
**Summary:** This segment summarizes the description of the Lord's appearance, emphasizing the majestic opulence and regality that surround Him, noting that He always appears with His consort and associates.

So basically, this is a description of the grand appearance of the Lord. This should give us a sense about the Lord's majestic opulence, the regality that surrounds Him, and the opulences, and how He appears. He did not appear alone. When the Lord appears, He appears with His consort most of the time, and with His associates, Swashrihi in the previous verse, Sahashrihi, along with Lakshmi, and here, we are seeing Swapumbhihi, along with His associates, all of them carrying different paraphernalia, umbrella, and offering chamar, and in that way, the Lord appears. So this is to give us an idea, this is to help us understand the Lord's position, and how the Lord appears in Vaikuntha.

#### analogy, material power, entourage
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=534
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "When we see in this material world, a big person coming, it's marked with his entourage, and all of these kind of manifestations of his power and position. And that's natural."
**Summary:** This segment uses the analogy of the President of India's visits to temples to illustrate how even in the material world, powerful figures are accompanied by entourages and displays of power, suggesting that the Lord's appearance with associates is natural.

Some years ago, we had a president of India visiting our temple here, and we had gone and invited him in Delhi, and then he agreed to come, and when he came, there were a lot of preparations made, and security personnel, protocol officers, they would advise us how to, what he should do, etc. And finally when he came, he didn't come alone. There was a motorcade of 24 cars all over on Hare Krishna hill. By the time the long motorcade came, and he got off, and there were a few cars before, and a few cars behind him, and it was, and all of them stood, the next two hours or so that he was there, all these cars were parked on the road, and all big cars, and big officials, like that. And when the president of India comes, it is a protocol that the chief minister, or the chief minister himself must come, the governor must come from the state. So all of them were there. So it's not a small thing when the president comes. And quite recently we had president of India coming to our temple in Vrindavan, which is far away. And there when he came, three helicopters landed. And so we were all waiting there and receiving, to receive him. First the governor's helicopter came from Lucknow. So that was another one. And then these three Indian Air Force helicopters, massive helicopters. And they all landed there. So, when we see in this material world, a big person coming, it's marked with his entourage, and all of these kind of manifestations of his power and position. And that's natural.

#### anthropomorphism, human ideas, divinity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=690
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Some people think and say that our human ideas, we are imposing on God. So this is called anthropomorphic concepts."
**Summary:** This segment recounts an interaction with a doctor who suggested that concepts of pleasing Lakshmi to please Narayana are anthropomorphic, meaning human ideas imposed on God, a view the speaker intends to refute.

And so here we see, when the Lord is appearing, naturally, and he also has his associates and entourage, all of them appear, and it's a grand appearance. Long ago, I had, I remember one interaction with a doctor. I had fallen sick, not very sick, some extent sick. And I had gone to a doctor. And this doctor was a very friendly, very, very, he talks a lot, with, he engages in a conversation with the patients. That's his, one of his techniques and puts them in comfort and talks to them nicely. So he started talking to me, and he saw me because I was a devotee. Then he started asking, I can't remember the exact subject that we discussed. Something I mentioned about Krishna, Radharani, Lakshmi, Vishnu, and something like that. When you should please Lakshmi, then Narayana will be pleased. You please Radharani, Krishna will be, something like that we were talking. And then he heard all that and he said, very interesting. All of these are anthropomorphic concepts. Meaning, he was saying that, anthropomorphic means having human attributes. Like here we see, we have experience, if you want to get the check from a big man, our devotees often do. They go and please his wife. And when the wife is pleased, there is a good possibility you will get the check. So these kind of things are human experiences. And so some people think and say that our human ideas, we are imposing on God. So this is called anthropomorphic concepts.

#### mythology, human creation, acharya's view
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=828
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "These are all ideas that have come from human society, now we are mapping on to the spiritual domain. And this is a kind of a human creation."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the idea that some view religious concepts like Vishnu's four arms as mythology, human creations imposed on divinity, a perspective the speaker contrasts with the views of Acharyas.

So this is what he was trying to say. And of course, I don't remember what I said, maybe I was not well enough to answer this kind of a question. So anyway, so people think that these are all ideas that have come from human society, now we are mapping on to the spiritual domain. And this is a kind of a human creation. Basically, it's an idea that it's a human creation. So that's how some people think. And the Mayavadis also have a similar idea. And they say that these are all just stories. Vishnu, Vishnu having four arms. Okay, here we have a human experience of two arms. And for the, because he is God after all, he should have something more than us, and let's give him four hands. You know, these kind of ideas. So these are all just mythology. They dismiss it like that. And they think that you should see some ideas, some good message behind that that you must appreciate. So how do Acharyas explain this? If you study, none of the Acharyas, including Mayavadi Acharyas, will say like this. Especially the Vaishnava Acharyas. They never derided these as mythological stories. A human idea carried forward to a spiritual realm. This is not the kind of an expression. No Acharya has ever said these things. Even Shankaracharya, he said, Narayano Paro Vyaktad. He said, yes, Narayana's existence, Para, is transcendental. So none of the Acharyas have said these things. And Srila Prabhupada, also following this, the line of the Acharyas, Srila Prabhupada, none of the, our Acharyas in our Sampradaya, none of them have said that these are all mythological, imaginary, some human idea imposed on divinity. This is not the understanding.

#### transcendental, sense perception, limitations
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=976
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Vishnu or Krishna, he is transcendental. He is beyond material sense perception. He is not the subject matter of the mundane senses."
**Summary:** This segment explains Srila Prabhupada's perspective, emphasizing that Krishna or Vishnu is transcendental, beyond material sense perception and the limitations of human senses, mind, and intellect.

How does Srila Prabhupada explain this? Srila Prabhupada, like here it is explained in this verse. He is, Desa, Achakshata, Akshavishayam. Vishnu is not Akshavishayam. He is not a subject matter of these eyes. He is not a Indriyavishaya. But here, Achakshata, Akshavishayam, he, they began to see him. So, Vishnu's position, Krishna's position is, he is not Akshavishaya, he is not a Indriyavishaya. He is not seen, he cannot be accessed by our senses. This is again and again explained in the Vedic literature, Athashree Krishnanamadhi, Nabhavet Grahya Mindriyaihi. Indriyagrahya Nabhavet. Senses cannot access him. He is beyond the senses. He is beyond sense perception. This is what the Vedic literatures have often explained about Krishna's position. And Srila Prabhupada also says that, yes, Vishnu or Krishna, he is transcendental. He is beyond material sense perception. He is not the subject matter of the of the mundane senses. And he is beyond the range of human sense and mental and intellectual conceptions. This is the understanding.

#### inaccessibility, mundane senses, analogy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=1086
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Krishna's reality, the reality of Krishna or Vishnu, is inaccessible to our mundane senses."
**Summary:** This segment uses the analogy of a mobile phone unable to make international calls to illustrate that our mundane senses have limitations and cannot access the higher reality of Krishna or Vishnu.

This understanding is presented throughout in the Vedic literatures. And Srila Prabhupada also says the same thing. He is, Krishna's reality, the reality of Krishna or Vishnu, is inaccessible to our mundane senses. Our mundane senses can only access a certain amount of reality. Not even all of the material reality, right? Our senses cannot understand. Our eyes cannot see the mind and intelligence. But we know people have mind intelligence, and they are working, and it is working. We can see the effects of the mind and intelligence, although we cannot see the mind and intelligence. So our senses have limitation. Even our mind and intelligence has limitation. What they can access in this reality as material reality. So there is a certain reality beyond what is accessible to the mind, senses and intelligence. And Krishna or Vishnu belongs to that higher reality. So our senses don't have the powers to access that realm of reality. That is the important point. Just like suppose you have a mobile phone. And your mobile phone, you don't have international, you only have facility to make local calls, India calls. You can't make any call outside of India. And somebody comes and tells you, I have a friend in America, and this is the number of my friend. And you use your phone and try to call that number. It's an American number. Call will not go through. You try many times, it will not go through. Now somebody may say, no, no, you charge properly, then it may go through. You can have charge many times, still it will not go through. Somebody may say, no, no, you change your phone version, get a upgrade, a better phone. You can get a better phone, still you try, it will not go through. Go to a place where there is very good signal, and try. You can do anything you want, anything you want, you will not get the call. Then can you say, see, I have tried so much. I don't think you have a friend in America. Or even better, I don't think there is something called America. Because if there is, I must be able to make a phone call and talk. No, it is not so. Just because our phone, our instruments cannot reach out, we cannot say such a reality does not exist.

#### spiritual reality, access, process
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=1270
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This spiritual reality is beyond the access, beyond the powers of our senses."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes that spiritual reality is beyond the reach of our senses, but a process exists to perceive it, as described in Vedic literature and today's verse, involving samadhi and trance.

This is the principle that is used by the Acharyas, by Srila Prabhupada. Srila Prabhupada gave very contemporary articulation of this idea, that this reality, this spiritual reality is beyond the access, beyond the powers of our senses. This is the perception, this is the perspective that is presented in the Vedic literature. Now, if this is the reality, how do we ever know that reality? Is there a way to know that reality? Yes. Although the senses cannot perceive, atha shri krishna naam aadhi na bhaved grahyam indriyaihi sevon mukho hi jihvadau svayam eva sphurathi adaha. This is the way, this is the method that is given in the Vedic literatures, how to perceive the transcendental reality. And here in this verse, in today's verse, also it is described that he is, he became akshavishayam, although he is not akshavishayam, he is adhokshaja. Adhokshaja means not accessible to the senses. Because, and he was samadhi bhagyam, sva-samadhi bhagyam. Lord is seen in samadhi, in trance. This is the idea. The senses ordinarily cannot perceive, but there is a process. If we follow that process, then we will be able to perceive the Lord in samadhi.

#### purification, intelligence, truth
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=1377
**Verse References:** SB 3.9.11
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When one's intelligence is purified, then one can perceive the transcendental reality."
**Summary:** This segment references a verse spoken by Shukadeva Goswami, explaining that by meditating on the lotus feet of the Lord and purifying one's intelligence, one can perceive the transcendental reality, a process most people don't undertake.

There are many verses in the Bhagavatam, one of them is the verse spoken by Shukadeva Goswami in his prayers, which we used to chant, or we chant sometimes. yadangri abhidhyana samadhi dhautaya dhiyanupasyanti hi tatvam atmanah samadhi dhautaya anupasyanti dhiyanupasyanti. In the Vedic literatures, like in this verse in the Bhagavatam, it is describing how anupasyanti, pasyanti means to see. The Vedic literatures talks about tatva darshi vihi, those who have seen the truth. They are seeing the truth. So there is a way to see that truth. How to see the truth? yadangri abhidhyana samadhi dhautaya dhiyanupasyanti hi tatvam atmanah atmanah tatvam. Atma here refers to Paramatma. Vishnu. Vishnu Tatva. So tatvam atmanah tatvam anupasyanti. How one can see that? yadangri abhidhyana samadhi dhautaya Angri means the lotus feet of the Lord. Angri. yadangri abhidhyana. When one does abhidhyana of Angri, the lotus feet of the Lord, and becomes mature, samadhi, reaches samadhi, fully absorbed in the lotus meditation of the lotus feet of the Lord, and by such meditation, dhauti, yadangri abhidhyana samadhi dhautaya dhautaya, dhauti means to become purified. And when one's intelligence is purified, samadhi dhautaya dhia dhautaya dhia. When one's intelligence is purified, then anupasyanti hi tatvam atmanah. This is the way one can perceive the transcendental reality. And then, in this verse, Shukadeva Goswami says, vadanti chaita kavayaya tharucham. And most people don't have this, come to this realization. Most people don't take the trouble of actually following the process, purifying the senses, purifying their consciousness by intense meditation, absorption on the lotus feet of the Lord. And hence they don't have a perception of the truth. But they still talk about the truth, and they talk in their own way, without proper understanding. That is why innumerable ideas and opinions have come about in this world about God. Those who have seen God, truly experienced God, they have one understanding, all the bona fide acharyas.

---
#### opinions, experience, purity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=1559
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Those who have seen God, truly experienced God, they have one understanding, all the bona fide acharyas."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts the numerous opinions about God with the unified understanding of those who have truly experienced God, emphasizing the need for purity to perceive the Lord, referencing the Hamsa Guhya prayers.

So, vadanti chaita etat vadanti yatharucham vadanti kavayo. As they want, their own opinions, they talk. But the Lord can be perceived in this way, same mukundo bhagavan prasidatam. May that Lord Mukunda be pleased with me, and reveal His true nature. That's the prayer of Shukadeva Goswami. So, Lord can be, like it is explained here, samadhi bhagyam. They were seeing the Lord in their samadhi. And now He became akshavishayam. They were able to see Him. In another place in the Bhagavatam, which we also chant, we used to chant, probably we chant occasionally, called the hamsa guhyas prayers in the Bhagavatam, a similar idea is described. Hamsa guhyas prayers were actually spoken by Prajapati Daksha. And generally, Daksha is not considered to be a very exalted person. Of course, he is one, in one sense in the material domain, considered an exalted person because he serves the Lord in his mission of creation of this material world, and populating the world. But still, his prayers are considered very special, and it has become part of the Bhagavatam, because Bhagavatam explains that he did not compose those prayers himself. Already those prayers were there in the Vedic literatures since time immemorial. And so those prayers, which were already present in the Vedic literatures, which he was trained in, so he offered those prayers, and hence hamsa guhyas prayers have a transcendental significance, and it is part of the Srimad Bhagavatam.

---
#### purity, absorption, experience
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=1701
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Only ones whose heart has become completely pure, such people can experience the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment delves into the Hamsa Guhya prayers, emphasizing the Lord's purity and the necessity of complete purity and absorption to experience Him, aligning with the consistent message of Vedic literature.

So, in this verse, in the hamsa guhyas prayers, Prajapati Daksha says, yaiyate kevalayasva samsthaya, hamsa yatasmai shucisadmane namah. Here again, hamsaya, the Lord's position is completely pure. Hamsa, generally, hamsa in Sanskrit means pure. It also refers to the swan, which is very pure, white, pure. And that's how very exalted persons are called paramahamsas, because they are very very pure. hamsaya tasmai shucisadmane namah. shucisadma, shuchi means pure again, sadma means an abode of purity. Only ones whose heart has become completely pure, such people can experience the Lord. yaiyate kevalayasva samsthaya, yaiyate, yiyate means to perceive, just like anupashyanti, tatvadarshinaha, seeing the Lord, experiencing the Lord, yaiyate, to experience, to perceive the Lord. kevalayasva samsthaya, again, kevalaya means in a complete absorption, state of complete absorption, swasamsthaya, in his original position, yaiyate, can be experienced, that Lord, hamsaya, shucisadmane, when one is in a state of complete purity, one will be able to experience the Lord. So, this is the position that the Vedic literatures have consistently presented. Whether it is in the ancient prayers of Hamsa Guhya, whether it is in the prayers of Shukadev Goswami, or it is in the Padma Purana, which Prabhupada often quotes, ata shri krishnanamadhi nabhaved grahyam indriyaihi sevonmukohi jihvadam, jihvadam. In one place in the Bhagavad Gita, when Prabhupada is quoting this verse, Prabhupada translates the words, the words, sevonmukaha, as when one is spiritually saturated with transcendental service. When our consciousness is spiritually saturated, saturated, we understand that when it is completely absorbed, and nothing else is there, remember in chemistry we used to study, concentrated, saturated solution, means it won't, it is fully saturated, nothing else, you can't add any more salt and it won't dissolve anymore, because the solvent is now saturated. In the same way, when our consciousness is saturated, no material conception, no material desires, no material contaminants, no contamination, no anartha, when it is saturated, spiritually saturated, in that state, one can perceive. This is the message in the Vedic literatures consistently.

---
#### intensity, dedication, absorption
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=1894
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It requires a certain amount of intensity, there is a certain amount of dedication, and absorption, that is essential. It's not some kind of a frivolous thing."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the need for intensity, dedication, and absorption to achieve spiritual perception, warning against a frivolous approach and reminding devotees of the purpose of their existence amidst difficulties.

So now we can see, the Lord, the literature, Vedic literature is talking about Samadhi, here Samadhi Bhagyam, Dhyanupashyanti, Samadhi, Dautaya, and Shuchi Sadmane, spiritually saturated, which means if we want to have a perception, a spiritual perception, a transcendental experience, we have to have, these words indicate, there is a certain amount of intensity, there is a certain amount of dedication, and absorption, that is essential. It's not some kind of a frivolous thing. And if we take it in a very frivolous way, like Shukadeva Goswami says, vadanti chaitat kavayoyata arucham, we will have our own understanding on based on our mental speculation. But this is not a subject matter of mental speculation, spiritual reality, it is something that we can experience. And for that, it requires a certain dedication, certain intensity, certain absorption. So we should remember this, because life takes us through certain difficulties. When we are doing our service, even with devotees, very often difficulties will come about. Somebody will speak to us in a very harsh way, we will be hurt. Sometimes we will be discouraged by some experience, or we attempt something, and we don't get the result, some discouragement may be there. All of these kind of things will happen, and is bound to happen in the life of a devotee. At that time, we should remember what is the purpose of our existence? What is the purpose of our, what we are doing? Isn't it for that single purpose of that spiritual experience that we have come to Krishna Consciousness? It is not for some better material experience, for a more enhanced and amplified sensual experience we are here. It is not for some kind of an honor and distinction and adoration and good words from other people, that's not the purpose we are here. So we should remember that it is for that transcendental spiritual experience of the Lord, which the Vedic literatures are promising, the great Acharyas are offering. It is for that. And for that, it requires a certain amount of intensity, dedication. And when we remember that, and all of these material difficulties will become very simple and trivial. We will know how to face all these things. This is the greatest spiritual potency that comes about by our absorption. And that we can face any of the difficulties in this material world.

---
#### spiritual development, gradations, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=2085
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The highest conception of the absolute truth is not even Vishnu. It is Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment concludes by reiterating the sages' experience of seeing the Lord face to face, emphasizing the gradations of spiritual development, and highlighting that the ultimate conception of the absolute truth is Krishna, beyond even Vishnu's transcendental regality.

So, here we see that these sages now they saw what was actually, what they were seeing in their trance. Now they saw face to face. And throughout the purport, Srila Prabhupada has explained that yes, this is the way it is. At one stage of spiritual development, one will see the Lord in the heart as Paramatma. And when one becomes more mature, in other words, there are in our spiritual progress, there are gradations. We can become mature, more mature, more mature, and become most mature. And when one becomes most mature, Prabhupada is making a statement, just like we see other things, we can see. Thus the devotee constantly sees the Lord within his heart. And in a more mature stage, one can see the same Lord directly as we see everything else. You see. And Srimad Bhagavatam is presenting all of these ideas so that we gradually we understand the transcendental technique, the science and the technique of spiritual perception. And here in these cantos, early cantos of the Bhagavatam, describing about how Vishnu can be seen, and how the great sages saw Vishnu in all regality, transcendental regality. Because Bhagavatam further intends to present even more higher concept, higher aspects of the absolute truth. Because the highest conception of the absolute truth is not even Vishnu. It is Krishna. And that Krishna, when the Bhagavatam describes about Krishna, and when you see Krishna in Goloka Vrindavana, He will not come with the transcendental regality and the splendor that you are seeing in the Vaikunthaloka. He will just come with cows and calves, holding a flute, holding a stick, with simple cowherd boys. That is the highest conception of absolute reality. If you cannot have Vishnu conception, if you cannot experience Vishnu, no, forget it. You cannot have access to even higher concepts of the absolute truth. Krishna. So, we can see here how the Bhagavatam is gradually training us about the transcendental reality. Gradually understanding. And what is this happening here? With all the chamara, beautiful swan like, appearing like swan, and on all the different paraphernalia, with the associates in grandeur, Vishnu made His appearance. Actually, this is Shantarasa. This is Shantarasa and Dasyarasa, which is prominent in Vaikuntha. Now, if you want to know the highest aspect of the absolute truth, then you must be familiar and you must know even higher rasas, and what the transcendental interactions with the associates in higher rasa is like. So, Bhagavatam is gradually preparing us, telling us about the different transcendental realm. How this transcendental realm of Vaikuntha, how the interactions and the pastimes happen there. Then, we will be able to understand even further about Krishna and Krishna's reality. We will stop here. Grantharaj Srimad Bhagavatam Ki. Srila Prabhupada.

### Srimad Bhagavatam | HG Chanchalapathi Dasa | SB 3.28.7 | 03-09-2016
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### yoga, mind control, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ho8Mz4YmEyw?t=30
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna consciousness is the topmost yoga system."
**Summary:** This segment explains the general yoga process, emphasizing the importance of concentrating the mind on the Supreme Personality of Godhead and highlighting Krishna consciousness as the topmost yoga system, contrasting it with voidism and impersonalism.

The general yoga process entails observing the rules and regulations, practicing the different sitting postures, concentrating the mind on the vital circulation of the air, and then thinking of the Supreme Personality of Godhead in His Vaikuntha pastimes. This is the general process of yoga. This same concentration can be achieved by other recommended processes, and therefore, Anyais Cha, other methods also can be applied. The essential point is that the mind, which is contaminated by material attractions, has to be bridled and concentrated on the Supreme Personality of Godhead. It cannot be fixed on something void or impersonal. For this reason, so-called yoga practices of voidism and impersonalism are not recommended in any standard yoga shastra. The real yogi is the devotee because his mind is always concentrated on the pastimes of Lord Krishna. Therefore, Krishna consciousness is the topmost yoga system.

---
#### mind control, intelligence, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ho8Mz4YmEyw?t=129
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Intelligence is higher than the mind."
**Summary:** This segment focuses on Kapila's instructions on yoga, emphasizing the control of the mind through intelligence to detach it from material enjoyment and concentrate on Vishnu's pastimes.

So, we are reading Kapila's instructions to his mother on the subject of yoga and the different steps and stages of yoga practice. And in the last verse, we saw how one must concentrate on the pastimes of Lord Krishna, how one must control the vital air, the circulation of the vital air, and finally become absorbed in Vaikuntha Leela. Continuing that, here Kapila is saying, etair Anyais Cha Pathibhir. This is what is to be achieved: to control the mind and enable the mind to concentrate on the pastimes of Vishnu in Vaikuntha. Anyais Cha Pathibhir. Etair Anyais Cha Pathibhir. So this can also be achieved by other processes. To control the mind and concentrate the mind on Vishnu and Vishnu's pastimes can also be done by other processes, anyapathah, etair anyaishchapatibhir, mano dushtam asatpatham. But the important thing is this mind which is dushta, which is contaminated, unbridled. That mind should be brought away from asatpatham, asat, temporary, patham, the path, and Prabhupada has translated that as on the path of material enjoyment. So the mind has a nature, has a tendency to go into the asatpatham, because mano dushtam, and so this has to be controlled. How do we control? Budhya yunjitha, by intelligence we must control the mind. Intelligence is higher than the mind. Mind is a repository of thoughts, feelings, emotions, desires.

---
#### mind control, intelligence, human life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ho8Mz4YmEyw?t=274
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Controlling the mind is possible only in the human form of life."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the distinction between the mind and intelligence, explaining how intelligence should be used to control the mind, and highlighting that controlling the mind is primarily possible in the human form of life.

So mind comes up with all of these desires and thoughts, and it is the budhya yunjitha. Buddhi has to control. And it is the intelligence which tells us, this is okay, this is not okay, do this, don't do this. So we very clearly see the distinction between the role of the mind and the role of the intelligence. Mind comes up with that. And what mental element? I am so confused. Thoughts, just like we see a big tally of nice food, and the mind says, oh, nice food, we must eat. And it is the intelligence which will tell, no, no, we must control. We will have health problems if I eat this tomorrow. Remember last time I ate that, and then I had next three days I was suffering. So if I eat this again, that suffering will come. It's the intelligence which is functioning. The two are distinct, so buddhi should be used to bridle the mind, and it won't happen immediately, gradually, slowly, little by little, because for a long time we have left the mind uncontrolled, because controlling the mind is possible only in the human form of life. Animals, the moment they develop a thought, they will run after that thought.

---
#### mind control, alertness, yoga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ho8Mz4YmEyw?t=391
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Spiritual life is like a sharp razor, you have to be very alert."
**Summary:** This segment continues discussing mind control through intelligence and alertness, using the analogy of animal training and a sharp razor to emphasize the need for constant vigilance in spiritual life.

Of course, we can train the animal to some extent, but then they are just waiting. If you have seen animal trainers and how they just hold for some time, and the moment you let go, again the animal will run towards something that is attracting that animal. So mind can be controlled by the intelligence, and here jitaprana, fixing the prana, which is a yoga process, and here another word Kapila has used is atandritaha, and one should always be alert. So by these processes, as we have spoken about earlier, or any other process, anyaishchapatibhir, one must control the contaminated unbridled mind, yunjitâ mano duṣṭham, which is always attracted by material enjoyment, asatpatham, and thus fix himself in the thought of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. And Prabhupada has mentioned that clearly Kapila is saying this, controlling the mind with the intelligence and being alert, that is possible by Ashtanga Yoga, and it may be possible by other methods also. But the important thing is to control the mind with the intelligence, and that also requires the intelligence to be always very alert. Srila Prabhupada also mentions, there is this verse from the Upanishads which talks about how spiritual life is like a kshurasya dhara nishitha duratyaya durgam patastat kava yo vadanti. In this verse, it is described the spiritual life is like a sharp razor's edge, and just like when you are shaving, you have to be very alert. If you are not alert and you have a very sharp razor, you may cut your face. So what is needed when you are having a sharp razor, you have to be very alert. So the same principle, atandritaha. As spiritual life is like a sharp razor, you have to be very alert. But for those spiritualists who are trying to practice Krishna consciousness, we have to be alert. Atandritaha also means not lazy, not tired.

---
#### intelligence, scriptural guidance, mind control
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ho8Mz4YmEyw?t=570
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We should never trust the mind because the mind is dushtha mana."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of engaging both personal and scriptural intelligence in spiritual life, using examples of a hunter and walking barefoot in a thorny forest to illustrate the need for constant alertness and distrust of the mind.

So the intelligence has to be very alert. So we should lead a life in such a way that our mind and intelligence can be properly engaged in spiritual life, and especially the intelligence is expected to be very alert, watching over the thoughts, always discerning on the basis of intelligence. Here buddhyayun jita, buddhi has to control. Now the buddhi is one's own intelligence. Another aspect of intelligence is intelligence coming from the scriptures, because our intelligence is also limited. We cannot understand many things about this complex material world, so we need direction from the Vedic literatures. So on the basis of one's own intelligence and also on the basis of intelligence developed from Vedic knowledge, one should control the mind, and one should always be alert. There is another example given in the Bhagavatam when a hunter hunts an animal. It is said that after capturing the animal, he will always be very alert about the animal because the animal can run away again. So in the same way, one is to control the mind and also to be always alert, otherwise the mind will again asat patham, mano dushyam asat patham. Mind will once again tread on the path of asat patham. So another example Prabhupada gives is if you are walking in a forest under a lot of thorns, imagine you are barefooted and you are walking, how careful you have to be where you place your every step. You have to be alert. In the same way, we have to be aware, we have to be alert every step or every stage of our thinking and acting process. So in other words, alertness is very important, and in the same context, Prabhupada also informs us that we should never trust the mind because the mind is dushtha mana. Mano dushtham asat patham. So we have to be alert.

---
#### guidance, material attraction, contaminated mind
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ho8Mz4YmEyw?t=755
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The essential point is that the mind which is contaminated by material attraction has to be bridled and concentrated on the Supreme Personality of Godhead."
**Summary:** This segment discusses seeking guidance from spiritual intelligence, scriptures, spiritual masters, and experienced devotees to stay on the right path of Krishna consciousness, emphasizing that a contaminated mind is attracted to material enjoyment.

So never trust the mind, never feel overconfident about the mind because mind is dushtha. So as practicing devotees, we always seek guidance. Guidance means intelligence. Either we have developed a certain amount of spiritual intelligence and we seek that guidance, or we seek guidance from the scriptures, from the spiritual master's instructions, or we can also take guidance from a more experienced devotee in the form of association. All of these helps to be on the right path of Krishna consciousness rather than trust the mind and go by the mind's thinking and judgment. So this is an important principle. The next important thing in this verse and in the purport that Prabhupada mentions, Mano dushtham asat patham. The essential point is that the mind which is contaminated by material attraction, mind is contaminated by material attraction, has to be bridled and controlled, has to be bridled and concentrated on the Supreme Personality of Godhead. So let us think a little bit on this principle of material attraction. Our minds are attracted to material enjoyment, and here Prabhupada explains that is the symptom of a contaminated mind. Mano dushtham asat patham. The essential point in this verse and in the purport that Prabhupada explains that is the symptom of a contaminated mind. A contaminated mind is attracted to sense gratification. Now this attraction means our consciousness, something it grabs our attention, something it holds our attention.

---
#### attraction, consciousness, sense gratification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ho8Mz4YmEyw?t=882
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Attraction itself is not a wrong thing. Being attracted to Indriya Vishaya is a wrong thing."
**Summary:** This segment delves into the nature of attraction as a fundamental feature of consciousness, distinguishing between attraction to sense gratification (Indriya Vishaya) and attraction itself, and contrasting Vaishnava and Mayavadi philosophies on the role of attraction in spiritual life.

Now, this attraction. Attraction means our consciousness, something it grabs our attention, something it holds our attention, something that evokes our interest, our liking. And that's why we get attracted to something. Just like all of us have this experience. We go out in the auto or we go out in the car. When you're driving, you're looking at things outside. Immediately there are many, many, many things which attract our attention, right? Those are the objects of senses, Indriya Vishaya. It attracts us. It attracts our attention, and this attention is a feature of consciousness. This table has no attention. It has no consciousness. So moment we say there is a soul, and the soul has consciousness, and the soul has the ability to be attracted to different thing. The table is not attracted to anything. If there is an ant crawling on the floor, the ant is attracted to something and hence it is walking and crawling and heading somewhere because it is attracted. So this principle of being attracted itself is a feature of our consciousness. Now the mayavadi's approach is, this attraction is a wrong thing. The Vaishnava's philosophy is, no, no, no. Attraction itself is not a wrong thing. Being attracted to Indriya Vishaya is a wrong thing. The feature of being attracted is not a wrong thing. In fact, it is the feature of being attracted. We like it. We are drawn to it. We are charmed by it, and that's what gives us pleasure. So the feature of being attracted is not wrong. That's a feature of the consciousness. Table has no attraction. Every living entity will always have attraction.

#### attraction, material life, spiritual life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ho8Mz4YmEyw?t=1058
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The sum and substance of material life is attraction between man and woman. The sum and substance of spiritual life is attraction between Krishna and living entity."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts material and spiritual attraction, quoting Prabhupada's letter to highlight that while material life centers on attraction between man and woman, spiritual life is characterized by attraction between Krishna and the living entity.

Now we have to decide where we are, to what we are attracted. When it is mano dushtam, asat patham. When the mind is not dushtam, it is shuddham, then it is sat patham. The mind can be attracted to the right thing. So this is an important understanding. When the mind is contaminated, we are attracted to sense gratification and other objects of senses in the material world. But the principle of attraction is a very fundamental feature of consciousness. That cannot be negated. In fact, Shila Prabhupada brings out this in a very interesting way in a letter that Prabhupada wrote. He says, actually Prabhupada could express very profound Krishna conscious ideas so spontaneously, so easily, because he was always on that spiritual platform. He was fully conscious of all of these knowledge. So even when he is writing a letter, this letter was being written to an American. He was not even a devotee. Later on he became a devotee. And in that letter Prabhupada writes, the sum and substance of material life is attraction between man and woman. The sum and substance of spiritual life is attraction between Krishna and living entity. It's a very profound thought. Material life means there is attraction between man and woman, and it is actually this for the materialists, for the conditioned souls, for the ignorant living entities. It is this attraction which actually gives them pleasure and enjoyment. Attraction between man and woman. Now, the next thing Prabhupada is... And Prabhupada is saying this. The sum and substance of material life is this. Everything is centered around this. Attraction between a man and a woman. Now, this attraction is actually very... Is what makes life interesting. So, what is spiritual life going to be? No, no, no, don't think there is no attraction in the spiritual world. Spiritual life also full of attraction because it's the attractive things which make life interesting. In the spiritual world, spiritual life is attraction between Krishna and the living entity. It is such a great relief. The Mayavadis say... Material life... Mayavadis also say the first half of the sentence. They say sum and substance of material life is attraction between man and woman.

#### spiritual life, attraction, Vaishnava philosophy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ho8Mz4YmEyw?t=1278
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Spiritual life also full of attraction."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts the Vaishnava understanding of spiritual life, which embraces attraction between Krishna and the living entity, with the Mayavadi view that seeks freedom from all attraction, and uses Kunti's prayers as an example of being irresistibly drawn to Krishna.

But when it comes to spiritual life, they don't know that variegatedness, and they say you should be free from all attraction. All attraction for no attraction at all means you become a stone. This is the kind of an understanding that the Mayavadis have. And the Vaishnavas say no, no, no. That's not the truth. Spiritual life also full of attraction, and attractive features are there in the spiritual world. And that attractive feature is between the Lord, the living entity and the Lord in the spiritual world. There are wonderful pastimes. There are wonderful situations created by Yogamaya in the spiritual world, and the living entity is a participant in all of those activities which are extremely attractive. That is the view of spiritual world and spiritual consciousness presented in the Vedic literature. A very nice verse which tells us about this is Kunti's prayers. We have all chanted this verse. You are all familiar with this verse. Kunti Devi is saying Matir Madhupate Sakrit. May Matihi, my consciousness, my mind, may it be always be drawn to Madhupate, Madhupati who is Krishna. Ananya Vishaya and no other thought, no other subject but only Krishna. And how should it be? Rati Mudvahata Daddha. Rati means attraction. So I should be drawn. I should be attracted to Krishna. How I should be attracted to Krishna? With the force, like Ganga is flowing towards the ocean. If you go to Haridwar, any of you must have seen, and when you see the Ganga flowing there, one thing you can notice is the force with which Ganga is flowing. There is some kind of a force. There is some kind of a something drawing Ganga to run, rushing towards the ocean. Kunti is praying, may my consciousness also rush towards Krishna. You see that element of attraction. When you are attracted to something, it is irresistible. You are drawn to it.

#### raga-anuga bhakti, attraction, spiritual life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ho8Mz4YmEyw?t=1480
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They have no attraction for these things."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the concept of Raganuga Bhakti, highlighting Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's emphasis on being drawn to Krishna after purification, and illustrates this with a story about a temple supporter who prioritizes a cricket match over a puja.

So Kunti is praying that may my consciousness be also attracted like that. Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's teaching says, in spiritual life, initially we go through a phase where we follow rules and regulations, controlling the mind, controlling the senses, attentive, be very alert. That's a very essential phase, but that's not the end. The end of all spiritual life is not just that. When we are purified by doing this, we will come to a stage that Kunti Maharani is talking about. Ratimuddhva dada dha, when one will be drawn to Krishna, and that stage is called Raganuga Bhakti. These are very important contributions of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. It is there in the Vedic literatures. Prior to that, many Acharyas in many other Sampradayas, this understanding is not highlighted. It is there, but it is not highlighted. But these are special features. Chaitanya Mahaprabhu highlighted these concepts, these principles from the Shastras. To give you an illustration of what this is, I have mentioned this story. There was one, there is one supporter of our temple. One day he called and he said, Swamiji, we have built a new house. We want you to come and do a puja in my house. So I said, okay, he is an important man. We will send some, our devotees go and do Narsimha Yagna or something. He said, okay. And then he asked, Swamiji, how long will that puja be? So I said, maybe one hour, one hour fifteen minutes. He said, no, no, no, no, Swamiji, not that long. I said, okay. So shall we make it forty-five minutes? No, no, no, no, even forty-five minutes is too long. Then I could hear his wife on the background shouting at him. No, no, no, don't say all that to Swamiji, etc. And finally he said, fifteen minutes. Just see, they have no attraction for these things. They want some puja. They want some puja you have to do. We have opened a new house. Somehow finish it off, finish quickly, because they have no attraction. For them, it is some kind of a vidhi. We have to do what to do. My wife is pushing me to do. For her satisfaction, we have to do one puja. You see, so there is no attraction in that.

---
#### attraction, material vs spiritual, raga-anuga bhakti
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ho8Mz4YmEyw?t=1674
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Cricket match. Oh, so interesting."
**Summary:** Continuing the story, this segment contrasts the lack of attraction towards spiritual rituals with the strong attraction towards a cricket match, highlighting the difference between a ritualistic approach and genuine interest, and setting the stage for explaining Raga-anuga Bhakti.

The very same person, another day, I had to call him for something. And then I called him. For a long time he didn't lift the phone. Usually he takes the phone very quickly. He is always on the phone. So I was wondering. And then I stopped. And again after a few minutes, again I called. Again after a long time, he picked up the phone, and I could hear on the other side, there was a lot of excitement in their house. I think he was in the house, and I could sense a kind of... And then he is telling, Swamiji, if you don't mind. If you don't mind. No, we are watching India-Pakistan cricket match. If you don't mind, I will call you after 15 minutes. You see. So when it comes to a cricket match, maybe something interesting must be happening. You know, we are all disconnected from that world. We don't know. And so when it comes to a cricket match, they are drawn to it. They are attracted to it. So, and they think that spiritual life means all these boring process, pratha and all these puja, vidhi, all that you have to do. How can we do it in a very short while? Cricket match. Oh, so interesting. One hour, two hours, five days. All that they are ready to endure. You see, because there the element of attraction is working, and they, because they are not spiritually trained, they don't know about these things. They think that spiritual life or religious life, religious activities are all simply some kind of a boring rituals that one has to follow because it's in the tradition or the wife is troubling, and that's the only impetus for them.

#### raga-anuga bhakti, attraction to Krishna, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ho8Mz4YmEyw?t=1803
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You will come to a stage where you will be drawn to Krishna and you will be going in that direction."
**Summary:** This segment explains Raga-anuga Bhakti as a stage where one is spontaneously drawn to Krishna, following that attraction, and contrasts it with a forced or ritualistic approach to spiritual life.

Chaitanya Mahaprabhu says, no, no, no, spiritual life is not just that. That's an essential stage. It's a stage that is essential for purification. Then you'll come to the stage of raga-anuga bhakti. Actually, the word raga-anuga, anuga, ga means gamishyati, to go. Anuga means to follow. And follow what? Raga-anuga. Raga means attraction. So you will come to a stage where you will be drawn to Krishna and you will be going in that direction. That stage is called raga-anuga bhakti. Rati muddhva hata adha-gange vaugam udhanvati. It is not that Ganga is flowing down the river like that with great ferocity like that because they have put one big pump and the pump is pushing the water. No, no, no, no. No pump is required in Ganga when Ganga is flowing. Ganga is flowing spontaneously with such velocity. In the same way, we can come to a stage where our consciousness will be drawn to Krishna like that and not attracted to anything else. Another example that Chaitanya Mahaprabhupada gives is yadavadimamachetakrishna-padaravinde nava-nava-rasa-dhamya-udhyatam rantum asit tadavadibhatanarisangamesmaryamane bhavati mukhavikaraha sustu nishtivanam ca.

---
#### devotion, attraction to Krishna, spiritual world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ho8Mz4YmEyw?t=1914
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Spiritual world means constantly these kind of attractive activities between Krishna and his devotees are constantly going on in the spiritual world."
**Summary:** This segment concludes by quoting Yamanacharya's prayers to illustrate the devotee's aversion to material attractions once drawn to Krishna's lotus feet, and emphasizes Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's teaching that one can reach a stage of spontaneous attraction to Krishna, where the spiritual world is full of attractive activities between Krishna and His devotees.

Prabhupada often quoted this Yamanacharya's prayers, very beautiful prayer where he is saying that since the time my mind became attracted to, attached to the lotus feet of Krishna, yadavadimamachetakrishna-padaravinde, since my mind has become attracted to the lotus feet of Krishna, nava-nava-rasa-dhamya-udhyatam rantum asit. In my heart, newer and newer rasas are just rising in my heart. Udhyatam rantum asit. He is relishing. He sees this is the raganuga stage where that newer and newer nava-nava-rasa, newer and newer rasas, bhakti-rasas are rising, surging in my heart. And hence tadavadibhatanarisangamesmaryamane, the most interesting and enchanting thing in the material world for the materialists is attraction and association with a woman. That narisangamesmaryamane, if I just think about it, mukhavikaraha, oh, I shun from that. Susti nishtivanam ca, and I spit at the thought. Very strong words. Just see the position of a devotee. So like this, the acharyas have said this. The shastra says this. And so Chaitanya Mahaprabhu points out and this is the stage one can actually come to. So that is the position of a devotee. The position of Krishna consciousness that Chaitanya Mahaprabhu is teaching, that we can come to that stage when we will be spontaneously attracted to Krishna and the spiritual world is ratikeli. Spiritual world means constantly these kind of attractive activities between Krishna and his devotees are constantly going on in the spiritual world. Just see the the conception of spiritual life that Chaitanya Mahaprabhu is giving. Such a vibrant, very very attractive spiritual world and Krishna is inviting us to come there. And that is the very special message of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu and presented by Srila Prabhupada. We will stop here. Grantharaj Srimad Bhagavatam ki. Srila Prabhupada ki.

### Srimad Bhagavatam | HG Chanchalapathi Dasa | SB 3.28.8 | 14-10-2016
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### dhyana, meditation, holy places
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wXJ0bnY4EUg?t=30
**Verse References:** BG 6.11-12, SB 3.28.8
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In other words, Prabhupada points out that this dhyana process is practiced in such places."
**Summary:** This segment describes the process of dhyana as described in the Srimad Bhagavatam and Bhagavad Gita, emphasizing the importance of a secluded, sanctified spot and the impracticality of this process in modern cities.

Sitting in an easy posture is called swasti samasinaha. It is recommended in the yoga scripture that one should put the soles of the feet between the two thighs and ankles and sit straight. That posture will help one to concentrate his mind on the Supreme Personality of Godhead. This very process is also recommended in Bhagavad Gita 6th chapter. It is further suggested that one sit in a secluded, sanctified spot. The seat should consist of deerskin and kusha grass topped with cotton. So we are reading these scriptures, these verses from the Srimad Bhagavatam spoken by Kapiladeva to his mother Devahuti. And here he is describing the process of dhyana, meditation, and very similar to what is described in the Bhagavad Gita. Shuchaudeshe pratishthapya. One should go to a shuchaudeshe. Shuchidesha means a sacred place, a sanctified place. And vijitasana asanam. Sthasmin swasti samasina rujukaya samabhyaset. Arrange a seat and sit and master the sitting postures and sitting in an easy posture. Rujukaya samabhyaset, with one's back erect, and practice the breath control. And in this context, Srila Prabhupada mentions in the Bhagavad Gita, shuchaudeshe pratishthapya, immediately refers to going to a holy place, a sanctified place, like Rishikesh, Haridwar, banks of Ganges, banks of Yamuna, and such places, and says it does not refer to modern cities. So in other words, Prabhupada points out that this dhyana process is practiced in such places. Even in our country in the past we hear about, in the Puranas we hear about kings retiring from the kingdom. They didn't decide to stay in the kingdom itself somewhere. I'll do this dhyana process, but they went to the Himalayas. They went to some holy place, which will help them control their minds, because that is the process that is recommended in dhyana and not suitable for people living in modern cities, because this whole process requires another kind of, this is a different kind of an approach. So in this context.

#### dhyana, bhakti, absolute truth
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wXJ0bnY4EUg?t=261
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That's the purpose of the Vedic literatures, to help us understand that absolute truth which is not dependent on anything else."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the prevalence of dhyana in the Bhagavatam, its relation to bhakti, and the purpose of Vedic literatures to understand the absolute truth, contrasting it with relative truths.

We also see in the Bhagavatam there are many places this kind of a dhyana is mentioned, like here Kapila is telling Devahuti. We also come across Narada instructing Dhruva to do this kind of a dhyana, also going to a holy place, and Dhruva went to a holy place in Vrindavan. So many places this dhyana is mentioned. And so one may want to have an overall perspective of what the Vedic literatures are talking about. What is dhyana? What is with relation to bhakti? How does that relate? Because in the beginning process, in the beginning of this chapter, Prabhupada mentioned that dhyana is being described because it is one of the Vaishnava disciplines, one of the Vaishnava practices. Dhyana is a Vaishnava practice. So how do we understand all of that? This is nicely described in the Bhagavad Gita purports and also in another place in the same canto by Srila Prabhupada. By and large it is like this. There are two methods of approach in the absolute truth, to approach the absolute truth, to come in touch with the absolute truth, Param satya, and to experience the association or coming in touch with the absolute truth and an understanding of the absolute truth. That's the purpose of the Vedic literatures, to understand Param satya, what is the ultimate truth, what is the absolute truth, what is the supreme truth, because in our material affairs, we come across relative truths, truths which are relative, truths which are temporary, depending on another truth. Those are called relative truths, like the understanding that I am a person, I am a person living here in this world, is a relative understanding, because my existence is dependent on the existence of my parents, from whom I have come. So my existence is a relative existence. I exist, and it's a truth, I exist, but that truth is relative. Now, my parents' existence is also relative, because their existence is dependent on the existence of their parents, and so on. So what is that absolute truth, which is not dependent on anything else? So that's the purpose of the Vedic literatures, to help us understand that.

---
#### absolute truth, jnana, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wXJ0bnY4EUg?t=476
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Indirect approach is you organize the society in the material world according to varnas and ashramas."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the two methods of approaching the absolute truth: the indirect method through varnas and ashramas, detached duty, and gradual enlightenment, and the direct method through bhakti.

So now, there are different methods mentioned all over in the Vedic literatures, how to approach that absolute truth, and so on. So an overview of all of this is presented in by Srila Prabhupada, and that he describes as there is one direct method of approaching the absolute truth, and there is an indirect method of approaching the absolute truth. First let's understand what is the indirect approach. Indirect approach is you organize the society in the material world according to varnas and ashramas, and people have different natures, and then they do their respective duties according to the varna and according to the ashrama. And when they do their duties in a detached manner, and gradually enjoy the senses, dharma, artha, kama, all of that, then gradually, when they do that, there is a certain amount of detachment that they have to practice, and that will bring about a certain enlightenment in them. So this entire process where there is a certain sacrifice of phala, karma phala thyagha, and when you do karma phala thyagha, then there is a certain amount of enlightenment, and that is called jnana, when one begins to discern matter and spirit. And generally the jnana process helps them to understand one feature of the absolute truth called the Brahman. That's not the factor of absolute truth. That's not the totality of absolute truth. It helps them to understand an all-pervading, eternal, formless absolute truth, which is called Brahman. And.

#### dhyana, paramatma, bhakti
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wXJ0bnY4EUg?t=634
**Verse References:** BG 6.10-15
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The final understanding of the absolute truth is by bhakti."
**Summary:** This segment explains that if one practices dhyana, they can realize the Paramatma feature of the Absolute Truth, but the final understanding comes through bhakti.

If they practice dhyana as it is being described here by Kapila and in many other places in the Bhagavatam, including the Bhagavad Gita, in the dhyana there is the art. If the mind is not calmed, and if the mind is not, senses are not controlled, there is no possibility of making any spiritual progress. So by this one feature, the Paramatma feature present in the heart of every living entity, as well as present in every atom in the material world, and if one does dhyana, becomes original, Paramatma is in Vaikuntha, and I must transfer myself to Vaikuntha, and there I can render bhakti. So this is one. This finally they come to the process, final understanding of the absolute truth is by bhakti. And this is one method of approach of the absolute truth.
