#### karma, dhyana, bhakti
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wXJ0bnY4EUg?t=772
**Verse References:** BG 11.55
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The direct method is practicing regulated bhakti, sadhana bhakti."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts the indirect path of karma, jnana, and dhyana with the direct path of bhakti, emphasizing the importance of offering karma to Krishna and practicing sadhana bhakti.

Karma results in enlightenment. Dhyana advances to dhyana. Dhyana is higher than dhyana, and dhyana is higher than karma phala thyaga, varunashrama, and all of that. And in the varunashrama, there is also recommendation doing charity. And the direct method, where one does karma phala thyaga, but is directly introduced about the absolute truth. You offer the karma phala, not just be detached in absolute truth, mat karma krn, Krishna refers to in the eleventh chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, do my work, Krishna karma. So that is one kind. So it introduced to a certain devotional practices, bhakti practice of bhakti, and that is called sadhana bhakti. And when one does sadhana bhakti, one gets gradually purified and comes from the absolute truth, Krishna.

#### bhakti, kali yuga, direct process

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy

**Key Quote:** "Bhagavatam stresses the direct process and Chaitanya Mahaprabhu presenting that talks about the direct process rather than the indirect process."
**Summary:** This segment explains that the direct process of bhakti is most suitable for Kali Yuga, as the indirect method is risky due to attachment to sense gratification, and the Bhagavatam emphasizes this direct approach.

Broadly this is the whole program that is mentioned everywhere in the Vedic literature. These are the two methods, the indirect method that is all practices, and then you become more purified and achieve pure bhakti. So that's a direct process that is also described in the Vedic literature. For some stuff that is a kind of mind. This is the really a mind that is one that is a part of the life that is a true. It is some of those karmas and all of that there is a lot of karma kanda aspect in that so by and large a lot of people are interested in sense gratification so that process is there but then gradually one becomes enlightened jnana and then further dhyana and then dhyana further matures into bhakti so detachment understanding of brahman realization of paramatma and realization of bhagavan which is the complete realization so this is a gradual step-by-step process the other process is directly aiming towards Krishna by offering the results to Krishna working for Krishna practicing some regulated bhakti and that bhakti regulated bhakti leads to pure bhakti and by pure bhakti one understands complete absolute truth so these are the two methods that are recommended but for Kali Yuga what Chaitanya Mahaprabhu points out is that the indirect method is very risky one cannot practice Varanashrama properly one very attached to the sense gratification opportunities that are offered in the process and hence when Vyasa Deva had compiled all of these Vedic literatures when he met Narada and when he was feeling that something I have not done completely and then when Narada explains he says that this indirect method that you have recommended and this indirect method is very risky. By and large, in all of the Vedic literatures, it is a jugupsitam mahanvyatikramaha. It is simply an abominable, condemnable process. And it is a great mistake, blunder you have committed.

#### direct process, bhakti, dhyana
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wXJ0bnY4EUg?t=1123
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The other method is, one can offer the results of one's activities and work for Krishna. And you can do that process in any circumstance."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts the direct process of bhakti with the indirect process of dhyana, highlighting the impracticality of dhyana in Kali Yuga and the accessibility of offering one's work to Krishna in any circumstance.

And then Narada advises him to write about the direct process and that's how the Bhagavatam comes about. So Bhagavatam stresses the direct process and Chaitanya Mahaprabhu presenting that talks about the direct process rather than the indirect process. Now the indirect process. The direct process of dhyana, some of these are mentioned in the Vedic literature, in the Srimad Bhagavatam. Because dhyana, for instance, is also about understanding the Paramatma and transferring to Vaikunthaloka. It's almost there at the very high level. Because varnashrama, dhyana, dhyana and then bhakti, you are almost there. But the dhyana process... The dhyana process is not practical for this age, especially for the people living in the cities. Because it says, shucaudeshe pratishtapya. You have to go to a sacred place. And hence this is not very practical. The other method is, one can offer the results of one's activities and work for Krishna. And you can do that process in any circumstance. Including a circumstance like somebody is in a battlefield like Arjuna was in. He was able to fight because it was Krishna's work. And doing Krishna's work is that stage. So like this, the two methods have been recommended. And Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's recommendation is that one should follow the direct process.

#### karmakanda, ahimsa, kali yuga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wXJ0bnY4EUg?t=1239
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The indirect process is filled with risks and may not result in the ultimate realization of the absolute truth."
**Summary:** This segment uses the example of Karmakanda to illustrate the failure of the indirect process, where regulated meat-eating led to slaughterhouses, necessitating the appearance of Lord Buddha to teach ahimsa.

Another example of how the indirect process works. Another example of how the indirect process works. Another example of how the indirect process works. Another example of how the indirect process fails is the practice of Karmakanda by the people, because Karmakanda allows a certain amount of Debbie God worship and offering meet in a regulated way. And that is alone. And hence when people try to practice that at that certain state, they became so attached to eating meat and killing animals and they converted all the temples into slaughterhouses and when people questioned them, how are you killing animals? See it is given in the Vedas, we can kill the animals and so at that time Lord had to appear as Buddha in order to teach the principle of Ahimsa. So this is also explained by Narada and in the Narada Muni and Vyasa Devas conversation. When Narada said that Jagup Siddham, what you have done is abominable. So Vyasa Deva said no, no, no, I have also put some regulations. I have said that you can enjoy but in a regulated way. But people in Kali Yuga where Rajogunar, I have also put some regulations. I have said that you can enjoy but in a regulated way. But people in Kali Yuga where Rajogunar, Tamoguna are prominent, they will be so carried away by the spirit of enjoyment that is permitted in the Karmakanta. Tasya nivaranam, tasya manyate nivaranam janah. Na manyate nivaranam janah. Narada Muni says they will hardly care for your regulations, restrictions. Exactly as it happened and Lord Buddha had to appear in order to set right the thing. So the indirect process is filled with risks and may not result in the ultimate realization of the absolute truth.

#### direct process, sadhana bhakti, bhagavad gita
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wXJ0bnY4EUg?t=1371
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The direct process on the other hand is practicing regulated bhakti, sadhana bhakti."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts the slow indirect process with the direct process of sadhana bhakti, emphasizing Krishna's emphasis on the direct process in the Bhagavad Gita, despite the context of the Mahabharata.

And also it may take many, many lifetimes of progress. The indirect process is a slow process. The direct process on the other hand is practicing regulated bhakti, sadhana bhakti, offering the results of our activities to Krishna directly, and in this way approaching the Absolute Truth, which is the direct process. And now this direct process was referred to by Krishna in the conversation, was emphasized in the Bhagavad Gita. So, Bhagavad Gita, although it is Mahabharata, it is describing all the intrigues between the Pandavas and the Kauravas, and all that is happening, and Krishna involved in some of these things, all that is explained. In the middle of that, Krishna's dialogue, conversation, Krishna's instructions to Arjuna is full of the direct process, and not the indirect process. YAM IMAM PUSHPITI YAM IMAM PUSHPITI YAM IMAM PUSHPITI YAM IMAM PUSHPITI YAM IMAM PUSHPITI YAM IMAM PUSHPITI Right in the second chapter, Krishna is referring, there is another indirect process, and Veda, Vada, Ratha, Apartha, these people who are followers of the Vedas, they follow that, and they think that this is the highest thing. Boga, Ishvarya, Gatimprati, to make progress on the path of Boga and Aishwarya, that's the highest thing. Nanya, Dasti, Diva, Dinaha, and they consider this is the highest thing. There are people like that, that's referring to the indirect process. But then Krishna says, no, no, there is another process, Boga, Ishvarya, Prasaktanam, Taya, Apartha, Shetasam, Vyavasayatmika, Buddhi, Samadha, Unavidhyate. That Samadhi, complete absorption in the absolute truth, is not possible for those who are attached to the path of enjoyment and opulence, Boga, Ishvarya, Gatimprati.

#### surrender, bhakti, direct process
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wXJ0bnY4EUg?t=1496
**Verse References:** BG 18.66, BG 9.2
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "DHARMAN PARITYAJYA, referring to the indirect process. And now surrender to me, which is the direct process."
**Summary:** This segment discusses Krishna's instruction to surrender all dharmas, emphasizing that it refers to giving up the indirect process and embracing the direct process of bhakti.

So, he begins to tell, and finally, in the 18th chapter, Krishna is saying, SARVA DHARMAN PARITYAJYA MAM EKAM, SARVA DHARMAN PARITYAJYA MAM EKAM, SARVA DHARMAN PARITYAJYA MAM EKAM, SARVA DHARMAN PARITYAJYA MAM EKAM, What is that all Dharma Parityajya he is saying? I think I mentioned this story, once one person came, he came to our temple, and he said, I have found one mistake in Bhagavad Gita. I said, oh really? What is this Bhagavad Gita? No, so many Acharyas have written commentaries on Gita, nobody has said. Then his explanation was this, Krishna is saying, SARVA DHARMAN PARITYAJYA MAM EKAM, because earlier he said, DHARMA PRATISTHAPANA, DHARMA SAMSTHAPANARTHAYA SAMBHAVAMI, in order to establish Dharma Aikam, but later on Krishna is saying, DHARMAN PARITYAJYA MAM EKAM SHARANAM, it should be, SARVA ADHARMAN PARITYAJYA MAM EKAM SHARANAM, he should be giving up Adharma, that should have been the thing. He says, no, no, no. Krishna is saying, DHARMAN PARITYAJYA MAM EKAM, DHARMAN PARITYAJYA, referring to the indirect process. And now surrender to me, which is the direct process. Now, this direct process is not, is there here and there, because bhakti element has to be there in every process, whether it is dhyana, whether it is kriya, karma, whatever process, a little bit of bhakti has to be there for spiritual progress to come about. So bhakti element is required. But in all of those, there is a mix of bhakti. What about direct bhakti, pure bhakti? So here and there, this process of pure bhakti was known to many sages and they discussed this. But, knowing that there are different kinds of people in the society... Varanashrama, all of that, worship of the devatas... ...all that was relevant, because there are different kinds of people in the society, not everyone can practice it. So the indirect processes were by and large described. And... All such examples were cited in the Puranas and Veda Vyasa had compiled and anciently got completed. S 一 S 一 S worth it. And Veda Vyasa had compiled all those processes into a mix of experience. Two points, one point, cut off words,orie, etc., the economic intensity.

All of them so there was the karma there was the gana all of that was there but here and there there was also exalted devotee exalted personalities who knew about the direct process and the direct process is called the bhagavatam process. Bhagavatam means in connection with Bhagavan directly in connection with the Bhagavan this process was known so Vyasa Deva when he was instructed by Narada now you compile a Vedic literature now he recalled that I have myself spoken this direct process to my son Shukadeva Vyasa Deva knew that and he had spoken that to his son adhya ghat mahadakya adhya ghat mahadakya adhya ghat mahadakya nam so that my son I had spoken because I saw my son he was refusing to be born come out of the mother's womb for several years and finally he came out and then after he came out he walked away I was calling out my dear son no no you're my son I have to do the regular purificatory processes but he just walked away and I was feeling great separation and I was calling putra iti I was calling out my dear son my dear son all the tree all the trees that were there all reflected my words and they also called out putra putra iti in other words the trees were echoing and the son walked away so then I realized my son is a very exalted person and so I have to teach him the very exalted message the highest direct process and I taught him the bhagavatam and he learned it.

#### bhagavatam, direct process, kali yuga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wXJ0bnY4EUg?t=1776
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "And this is the essence of all the Vedic literatures and this is most suitable for Kali Yuga and this process has to be practiced."
**Summary:** This segment provides examples of how the Bhagavatam, emphasizing the direct process, was spoken on various occasions, highlighting its suitability for Kali Yuga.

There was another occasion the sages of Naimisharanya gathered and they wanted to know what is the ultimate essence of all the Vedic literatures Kali Yuga is coming and at that time there was one great devotee who knew this bhagavatam and he spoke that bhagavatam to them that is in the sages to the name sages of Naimisharanya Suta Goswami spoke. There was another occasion Pariksit he was cursed to die in seven days and when he renounced the kingdom and he was preparing to die many sages came and Pariksit asked him what is the process what is the duty of a man about to die and they all were coming up with different ideas there was a lot of confusion some were saying Dhyana some were saying you should do Arunashrama some were saying no no you should not do that some were saying no no you should not do that some were saying no no you should not do that some were saying no no you should not do that you should do something else and there was confused at that time Shukadeva came and Shukadeva knew the bhagavatam and he spoke and everyone's became silent and said yes what he is saying that's the ultimate thing suitable for a great person like Pariksit and there that conversation happened so like this there were many many occasions where Yama Raj spoke to Yama Dootas when the Yama Raj Yamadūtas tried to conquer Ajāmila and they could not. Some other strange personalities came and stopped them and they went back to Yamarāja and asked, what is this happening? And then Yamarāja told them, the bhāgavata knowledge. So like this, Vyāsadeva compiles all those instances where the bhāgavata knowledge, the direct process was explained by great personalities and all of those he compiled together and formed the bhāgavatam. And this is the essence of all the Vedic literatures and this is most suitable for Kali Yuga and this process has to be practiced.

#### direct process, pure devotee, chanting
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wXJ0bnY4EUg?t=1904
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You must come in touch with a pure devotee who knows Kṛṣṇa, who has that intimate understanding of Kṛṣṇa. That is the trigger for the direct process."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of associating with a pure devotee and chanting the holy name of the Lord for the direct process of bhakti to work effectively in Kali Yuga.

Now for the direct process to work, one or two important features are required. If you take all of these examples, when Śukadeva was instructed by Vedavyasa, Parīkṣit was instructed by Śukadeva, the sages of Naimishāraṇyā were instructed by Suta Gosvāmī or Yamadūtas were instructed by Yamarāja. And so many, Dhruva was instructed by Nārada. Like that, many, many examples if you take. In all of these, for the bhāgavata process, the direct quick process to work, one important feature is, you must come in touch with a pure devotee who knows Kṛṣṇa, who has that intimate understanding of Kṛṣṇa. That is the trigger for the direct process. And not always easy. And so bhāgavatam process for it to work, we have to come in touch with a pure devotee and serve a pure devotee. And another important feature for the bhāgavata process to work, which is an accelerated process, direct process, and that requirement is to chant the holy name of the Lord, to come in touch with the Lord directly. And for Kali Yuga, the Lord has appeared in the form of holy name and to chant the holy name.

#### devotional service, knowledge, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wXJ0bnY4EUg?t=2002
**Verse References:** BG 9.2
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Devotional service in Kṛṣṇa consciousness is the direct method."
**Summary:** This segment summarizes a purport explaining the two kinds of devotional service: regulated principles and full attachment, and emphasizes that Kṛṣṇa consciousness is the direct method, while the indirect method involves renunciation, knowledge, and meditation.

This whole thing is described in a very succinct manner. In this purport, I will read this one. As mentioned in the previous verses, there are two kinds of devotional service. The way of regulated principles and the way of full attachment in love to the Supreme Personality of Godhead. This is referring to the ultimate process. There are two kinds of devotional service. The way of regulated principles and the way of full attachment. The full attachment is having full bhakti, highest level of prema bhakti. If you cannot do that, to achieve that, you have to do regulated bhakti. For those who are actually not able to follow the principles of Kṛṣṇa consciousness, it is better to cultivate knowledge, because by knowledge, one can be able to understand his real position. Gradually, knowledge will develop to the point of meditation. Jnana to meditation. By meditation, one can be able to understand the Supreme Personality of Godhead by a gradual process. There are processes which make one understand that one himself is the Supreme, and that sort of meditation is preferred. One is unable to engage in devotional service. If one is not able to meditate in such a way, then there are prescribed duties as enjoined in the Vedic literature, for the brāhmaṇas, vaiśyas, śūdras, which we shall find later in the chapter of Bhagavad-gītā. Now coming back to the karma-pala-tyāga. One should give up the results of chapter of Bhagavad-gītā. But in all cases, one should give up the result or fruits of labor. This means to employ the result of karma for some good cause, like hospitals and such charities. In summary, to reach the Supreme Personality of Godhead, the highest goal, there are two processes. One process is by gradual development, and the other process is direct. Devotional service in Kṛṣṇa consciousness is the direct method. And the other method involves renouncing the fruits of one's activities. Then one can come to the stage of knowledge, then to the stage of meditation, then to the stage of understanding the Supersoul, and then to the stage of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. One may either take the step-by-step process or the indirect path. The direct process is not possible for everyone. Therefore, the indirect process is also good. It is, however, to be understood that the indirect process is not recommended for Arjuna, because he is already a saint. He is already at the stage of loving devotional service to the Supreme Lord. It is for others who are not at this stage. For them, the gradual process of renunciation, knowledge, meditation, and realization of the Supersoul and Brahman should be followed. But as far as Bhagavad-gītā is concerned, it is the direct method that is stressed. Everyone is advised to take to the direct method and surrender unto the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Kṛṣṇa.

---
#### direct process, bhagavatam, madhurya bhakti
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wXJ0bnY4EUg?t=2199
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Chaitanya Mahāprabhu helps us to get an overview of the whole Vedic process, how, what falls into, where all these things fall into place, and what is the position of Bhakti, what is the position of the Bhagavatam process."
**Summary:** This segment concludes by reiterating that the Bhagavatam is all about the direct process, summarized by Caitanya Mahāprabhu as Sādhanabhakti leading to Rāgānuga Bhakti and Prema Bhakti, and highlights the importance of Krishna's Vraja activities and Madhurya Bhakti.

So, this is the process that is recommended by Caitanya Mahāprabhu. And this is the process that the entire Bhagavatam is all about. How to take the direct process? And that was further summarized by Caitanya Mahāprabhu as Sādhanabhakti. And that leads to Rāgānuga Bhakti. And as we advance in Rāgānuga Bhakti, we come to the highest stage. The highest stages of Prema Bhakti. One will have complete understanding of the Absolute Truth. So, this is the overall picture that we get. And so, in that sense, we see that Dhyāna, Dhyāna was recommended, like Kapila is mentioning, because it was more suitable for other ages. Valmiki did, Dhruva did, and all of these examples are there because it was suitable for another age. And one could make gradual progress in understanding Paramātmā and then the ability to transfer to Vaikuṇṭha Loka. Now, in the Bhagavatam, another aspect that was also mentioned by Nārada Muni to Vyāsadeva is to disclose the highest aspects of Krishna and His personality, His nature, and His activities. And Krishna had just then appeared, 5000 years ago. And so, Vyāsadeva wanted to disclose about Krishna's activities. And among them, the Vraja activities, Krishna's activities, for those 10 years and 7 months, when He was in Vrindavan, those are considered even more exalted because there He exhibited the Madhurya Bhakti, pure Madhurya Bhakti. And so, those are also very, very confidential aspects and Vyāsadeva explains all of that. So, like this, Chaitanya Mahāprabhu helps us to get an overview of the whole Vedic process, how, what falls into, where all these things fall into place, and what is the position of Bhakti, what is the position of the Bhagavatam process. We'll stop here. Grantharāja Śrīmad Bhāgavatam ki Śrīla Prabhupāda ki.

### The Lord in the Heart as a Witness | Sri Chanchalapathi Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### Krishna consciousness, witness, surveillance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/p8kADDM697s?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is seated in the heart and he is watching."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of CCTV surveillance to explain how Krishna is always watching us from within our hearts.

You go to a big store and there'll be CCTVs in different places. They'll put a board: "This area is under CCTV surveillance." That means they put a CCTV and they're informing you somebody could be watching or somebody is recording. And if we see such a board there, this is a CCTV surveillance zone. We are careful. We become alert. We don't do some wrong things, right? In the Bhagavatam, it is said Krishna is the witness of the innermost core of our heart. And the highest yogi is one who is devoted to Krishna in the innermost core of his heart. That is why in Bhakti, we cannot cheat at all. We may cheat externally, others, but Krishna is seated in the heart and he is watching. So we are always in the surveillance zone of Krishna. Nothing is out of his purview.

---

**Key Quote:** "Prabhupada wants us to pursue a very sublime, very noble, very exalted goal that is love of Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of having an exalted mission in life, which, according to Vedic culture, is self-realization, culminating in awakening our relationship with the Lord, specifically love of Krishna.

We must have a mission in our life, so you should be preoccupied. There must be something very exalted in your life that you are pursuing. That's what gives meaning to your life; otherwise, life is miserable. I was reading one Australian psychologist. He says when you get up in the morning, that exalted thing that you are pursuing in life should be the foremost thing that comes to your mind, and that energizes you. Wow, let me get up and do the work. Vedic culture tells us that the most exalted thing in human life that we should all be doing is self-realization. There are different grades of self-realization, and then there is the highest level of self-realization, and that is awakening our own relationship with the Lord. That should be the dominant thing in our life when we wake up in the morning. That should come to our mind. Prabhupada wants us to pursue a very sublime, very noble, very exalted goal that is love of Krishna.

### Three Dangerous Diversions from Bhakti-yoga | Sri Chanchalapathi Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### bhakti-yoga, diversions, karma, mukti, yogasiddhi
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/j8IUXnNznv8?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If you are able to somehow resist the temptation of these three exits, then you can head towards the supreme personality of Godhead."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of driving on an expressway to illustrate how karma, mukti, and yogasiddhi can be diversions from the path of bhakti-yoga.

You're driving on the expressway, you're heading very quickly, but then you see exit, food court, and so you exit, and then you'll see so many different kinds of food all displayed there, and then you get absorbed and lost there, and then you missed the purpose that you were heading in the expressway. So, the shastras point out, when you are trying to advance in the mission of human life, you will encounter three exits. The first exit is called karma. The second exit is called mukti, and the third exit is called yogasiddhi. If you are not careful, you can take that exit, and you can be lost there completely, and forget coming back to the exit. So, you're driving on the expressway, and heading towards the goal that you set out. If you are able to somehow resist the temptation of these three exits, then you can head towards the supreme personality of Godhead. Because, if you want to associate with Krishna, you should be free from the temptations of karma, jnana, and yogasiddhi. That is a pure devotee, who only wants to serve Krishna. He is Krishna.

### What Is Our True Identity? | Sri Chanchalapathi Dasa | SB 2.5.31 | 09.08.2024
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### Srimad Bhagavatam, Creation, Mode of Passion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "By further transformation of the mode of passion, the sense organs, like the ears, skin, nose, eyes, tongue, mouth, hands, genitals, legs and the outlet for evacuating, together with intelligence and living energy, are all generated."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam describing the creation of sense organs, intelligence, and living energy from the mode of passion.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya. Coming from Srimad Bhagavatam, 2nd Canto, 5th Chapter, entitled The Cause of All Causes, text number 31. Taija Sat Tuvikurvanad Indriyanidashad Bhavan. Jnana Shakti Kriya Shaktir Buddhiv Pranascha Taija Sav. Shrotram Tvagrana Drukjikva Vagdor Medra Angripayavaha. Shrotram Tvagrana Drukjikva Vagdor Medra Angripayavaha. Translation in purport by His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Ishwara Prabhupada. Translation: By further transformation of the mode of passion, the sense organs, like the ears, skin, nose, eyes, tongue, mouth, hands, genitals, legs and the outlet for evacuating, together with intelligence and living energy, are all generated.

---

Now this passionate egoism generally in the material world also people say don't be so egoistic right if somebody is projecting oneself too much then we say you're so much ego you have yes although in a general sense it is a correct expression but in our spiritual understanding we have a very special and qualified meaning of the word ego that's why Prabhupada sometimes use the word false ego oh there is false ego which should imply there is real ego or true you ego ego or something that is not false yes these are all very carefully selected words of Srila Prabhupada in presenting Vedic knowledge false ego Prabhupada usually translated ahankhara ahem means a sense of identity and that means a sense of identity and that which ahankara, ahamkarana, that which creates a sense of identity for us is called ahankara. Krishna also describes this body as made of eight elements, and the eighth element is ahankara, panchabhuta, manas, buddhi, and the eighth element ahankara. Now you see this is an eighth element in the body, just like mind, we think intelligence, we solve problems, we discriminate, this is right, this is not right, these are all functions of intelligence. In the same way there is an element of ahankara in us which gives us a sense of identity, I am. But it gives a sense of identity in terms of the body. #### False Identity, Vedic Knowledge, Ahankara
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=773
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Vedic knowledge is dealing with these subjects about identity."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the false sense of identity based on the body (gender, nationality, intelligence) and how Vedic knowledge addresses these misconceptions.

This life we have taken a male form. In our previous life what we were? Maybe female form, maybe some other form. And in that body we think I am male, I am female. And because we have a male or a female human form, we think I am a human being. This is also part of our self-identity. You see, Vedic knowledge is dealing with these subjects about identity. What is going on in our mind? What is going on in our consciousness? Let's understand. What is the truth behind these things? And since we are born in India, we think I am an Indian man. I am an Indian woman. Someone else born in some other part of the country, they think I am American man. I am German woman or whatever. So, Ahankara. This is the kind of a false egoism that is so intrinsic. We live with it. It comes with us all our life. Many things are decided because of these things. I am a man, I am Indian, I am woman. And many other things. I am intelligent. I am smart. I am foolish. I am not so intelligent. Like this, many, many, many descriptions of ourselves, ourselves, in terms of the body, we create all through our life. This is the Ahankara manifestation.

#### Gunas, Enjoyment, Ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=896
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The most important thing that Rajoguna does is, it puts us in a framework as I am an enjoyer."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how Ahankara is influenced by the three modes of nature (Sattva, Rajas, Tamas), with Rajoguna leading to the desire for enjoyment, often mixed with enlightenment and ignorance.

Next thing the Vedic literatures are pointing out. Now this Ahankara can come under three kinds of influence: Sattva, Rajas and Tamas. And this verse is discussing about this Ahankara under the influence of Rajas. Rajoguna. The most important thing that Rajoguna does is, it puts us in a framework as I am an enjoyer. So, I am an enjoyer. I must enjoy. Now I have a body. I must enjoy with this body. Sattva Guna creates a certain kind of an understanding. It enlightens. But usually in the material world, Sattva Guna is never alone. It gets mixed with Rajoguna. So, with a certain kind of an enlightened outlook, we try to become enjoyer. And it is not just that, Sattva Guna and Rajoguna mix. There is also always an element of Tamoguna. And Tamoguna's influence is to create ignorance. So, a sense of enlightenment and knowledge and understanding with the fundamental desire to enjoy, but not covered with a certain amount of ignorance.

#### Knowledge, Enjoyment, Material World
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=1021
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We use our intelligence, we use our systematic thinking, we use our school and college education, everything, how to manipulate the material world, for my enjoyment."
**Summary:** This segment describes how individuals combine knowledge, the desire for enjoyment, and a degree of ignorance to manipulate the material world for their own gratification.

This is actually what each one of us are. There is some knowledge. And knowledge, when it is charged with enjoying tendency, we look at this world, and we look at, we develop knowledge about this world, and how to manipulate this world, with our knowledge and understanding, for our enjoyment. You see? That's why Prabhupada says in the purport here, the living condition in material existence depends more or less on one's intelligence and powerful living energy. Intelligence to counteract the hard struggle for existence is assisted by the senses for acquiring knowledge. And the living energy maintains himself by manipulating the active organs, like the hands and legs. But on the whole, the struggle for existence is an exertion of the mode of passion. That is why you see, today, there is so much knowledge of science, technology, economics, management. So, all of this is a certain kind of a knowledge. But, it is charged and influenced with Rajas. And so, we use our intelligence, we use our systematic thinking, we use our school and college education, everything, how to manipulate the material world, for my enjoyment. Now, this thinking that, I am a citizen of this world, and I must enjoy this world, this is actually an aspect of ignorance. Because we are not really a citizen of this world. We have come here. We are not these human bodies. We have got a human body. We see around us, there are so many other non-human bodies also. And there are living entities there also. So, this passion, knowledge, and ignorance, combine together, in different ways, and create the material world, of which we are also part.

We become so absorbed in these things, our whole life passes, without understanding the true position of the soul, the true position of the living entity, and understanding why this elaborate arrangement is all there. Who made them? What was the purpose of making this? So, this passionate egoism, taijasa, this ahankara, charged with rajoguna, is what we become, all of us become engaged in. And in Kali Yuga, this taijasa, passionate egoism, is predominant. It is very powerful. Now, the truth of the matter is, you see this passion basically puts us in a mode of enjoyment. We seek enjoyment in different ways. In the outside world, people are seeking enjoyment for sense gratification. More money, you go to a restaurant and eat, dress up nicely and have, go to a mall, go to a social function. And, that's what they are doing. And for maintaining a certain standard of that, they are working very hard. So, it's basically a sense of enjoyment through hard work, and expression, using their senses, in different kinds of enjoyment activities. For that, we need money. And, and then, if you have nice position, position of power, position of authority, position of fame, that can enhance your ability to enjoy your senses even more. #### Detachment, Devotional Service, Enjoyment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=1325
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I just want to render service to you, life after life."
**Summary:** This segment introduces Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's teachings on detachment from material desires (wealth, followers, beauty, fruitive activities) and emphasizes pure devotional service (bhakti) focused on Krishna's enjoyment, not personal gratification.

So, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu teaches, na dhanam, na janam, na sundareem, kavitam vajagadisha kamaye, kamaye, na kamaye, I don't want to desire these things, na dhanam, kamaye, na janam, kamaye, na sundareem, kamaye, na kavitam, kavitam here refers to, kavita means poetry. Vedas describe in a very poetic way that how you can enjoy now and in future in swargaloka. These are called poetic descriptions in the Vedic literature. Flowery, pushpitam vajam, Krishna says in the Gita, flowery words. This is the kavita refers to fruity activities described in the Vedic literatures. Na dhanam, na janam, na sundareem, kavitam vajagadishakamaye, mama janmani janmanishvare, janmani janmanishvare, janmani janmani, life after life. So, in spiritual, in Vedic culture, we learn these are all not, these are all fleeting things, all the pleasures of the senses, the pleasures of wealth, the pleasures of women, the pleasures of honor, and fame, and position, and all these things, they are fleeting. Even going to swargaloka, that is also fleeting. Next, we should aspire for moksha, so that we can end this birth and death cycle, and we can be situated in moksha, and in that way, in moksha there is ananda of moksha. Again, ananda came there, enjoyment came there. No. So, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu is saying, mama janmani janmanishvare, bhavata bhakti rahayi tukitvayi, I just want to render service to you, life after life. So, I do not want even moksha also. So, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu is establishing the principles of pure devotional service, pure bhakti.

#### Bhakti, Krishna's Pleasure, Constitutional Position
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=1488
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Our focus is not our enjoyment, our focus is Krishna's enjoyment."
**Summary:** This segment clarifies that pure bhakti focuses on Krishna's pleasure, not personal enjoyment, and emphasizes that serving Krishna is the natural, constitutional activity of the soul.

So, in pure, so somebody may say, oh with pure bhakti, you will get bhakti, ananda of bhakti, no? That is the natural ananda, in that, the specific thing about bhakti is, our focus is not our enjoyment, our focus is Krishna's enjoyment. And as a result of serving Krishna, giving pleasure to Krishna, I may get happiness, I may not get happiness, it doesn't matter. Whether I am in heaven or hell, I simply want an opportunity to please Krishna. This is a new mode. But of course, we are constituted in such a way that when we serve Krishna, we get happiness naturally. The other methods were not natural to our constitution. Just like, if the hand puts food into the stomach, the hand gets nourished. Natural. But the hand putting the food in the stomach is a natural constitutional activity. But if the hand says, no, I won't put food in the stomach, I'll only be touching the food. I'll only be mixing rice, chapati, dal, and just, I won't, why should I feed the stomach? Stomach should enjoy, is it? No, I won't allow. Only I'll be touching. Or I'll be only seeing food. We know that won't nourish us. You see, in the same way, all attempts of karma, all attempts of jnana, are attempts for happiness in an unconstitutional manner. Constitutional meaning, Swaroopa, our makeup, how we are made, how we are constituted. This is the most important thing that we learn in Krishna consciousness. That we are not the enjoyers.

#### Krishna as Enjoyer, Service, Disease
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=1628
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna alone is the enjoyer."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of the body and its parts, this segment explains that Krishna is the ultimate enjoyer, and our role is to serve Him, just as the hands and legs serve the body. A diseased condition arises when we become uncooperative and unwilling to serve Krishna's pleasure.

Krishna is the enjoyer. Krishna alone is the enjoyer. We have only business is to make him happy. Just like the hands, only business is to keep the body in a comfortable situation. We want to have bath, the hands and the legs will work to give bath. We want to dress up, we want to put tilak, hands and legs assist me to put the tilak. We want to eat, hands and legs help. See how hands and legs are parts of the body, they are constantly working for the upkeep and well-being of the whole body. If hands and legs don't cooperate, it is called a diseased condition. We have to go to a doctor, we have to take some treatment, we have to take some treatment. Hand is not working, I am not able to lift my hand, I can't bend, fold my hand. Oh, you have to go to your doctor, you should examine. See hand is not able to work for the whole. Today our material situation is, we are also components, parts of Krishna, but we are the diseased, uncooperative, unwilling to serve the pleasure, for the pleasure of Krishna. This is our situation. We are that ailing arm. We are the diseased part of the body. This is what we have become today. This is our position. And this is coming because of our spirit of enjoyment. And that spirit of enjoyment is not natural to us. It is infused through the Rajoguna.

To free ourselves from that enjoying spirit, first is our determination and our desire that yes, I want to be free from this. These forces of Rajoguna, they are all divine. So it's very powerful. On our own we cannot. That is why we must desire, we must make that desire sincere, and we must make that desire strong. And a strong desire is nothing but determination. And through that determination, we demonstrate to Krishna that I want to abstain from acts of enjoyment. We use our body, we act, we feed our body, take care of our body, to the extent of, to the extent required for self-preservation only. If you don't feed the body, we cannot do, the body will not have energy, we cannot do anything productive. So, some Shakti is required. So we eat, we sleep, we accept these some comforts, only to the extent of self-preservation.

#### Enjoyment, Krishna's Enjoyment, Devotee

### Who Truly Owns Everything? | Sri Chanchalapathi Dasa @ChanchalapathiDas
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### ownership, detachment, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/gjUWKTLaLSU?t=0
**Verse References:** BG 9.4, Isha Upanishad 1
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We must use all of this to serve Rama."
**Summary:** This segment establishes the central idea that everything, including our bodies and senses, belongs to Sri Rama, and we should use them in His service.

I have come into this world, I have this Panchabhuta Sharira, Antribhuta Sharira, Sukshma Sharira. To whom all this belongs? Am I the owner of all this? Am I the owner of this body and the owner of the senses? Am I the owner of myself? To whom do I belong? Oh, we have this body, whatever strength we have, may not be much, but all of this belongs to Sri Rama, and we all belong to Sri Rama. We must use all of this to serve Rama. This is the thinking of a Bhatta. This world belongs to the Lord. Isha Vasya Midam Sarvam. Everything that is there, everything, the Lord is the owner. The Lord is the owner. The Lord is the owner.

---

### Who is a Paramahamsa? | Sri Chanchalapathi Dasa  @ChanchalapathiDas
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### Paramahamsa, detachment, Krishna Bhakti
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVHjpcwTGxY?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Unless one comes to the stage of Paramahamsa, one cannot experience Krishna Bhakti in its full form."
**Summary:** The speaker defines Paramahamsa as a stage of detachment from the material world, necessary for experiencing Krishna Bhakti, and applicable to all individuals regardless of their social role.

Rishabhadeva, Paramahamsa in Avadhoota Vesha, he appeared. Unless one comes to the stage of Paramahamsa, one cannot experience Krishna Bhakti in its full form. Paramahamsa stage, where you are uncaring for the material world. You don't care for honour, dishonour, pleasure. You are no more searching for pleasure in this material world. Bhagavatam describes Parikshit Maharaj, who was an emperor, was a Paramahamsa. He was in kingly robes. Prabhupada writes, Paramahamsa is required in every field of human activity. Because Paramahamsa always thinks, how I can do good for others. But this Paramahamsa Dharma can be practiced in other ways. In Avadhoota Vesha also, somebody who is a ruler, he should be a Paramahamsa. Somebody who is a businessman, he should be a Paramahamsa. Somebody who is a housewife, she should be a Paramahamsa. Somebody who is a child, child can also be a Paramahamsa, like Prahalad Dhruvavahar. That should be the goal of human life, to come to the Paramahamsa stage. Because in that Paramahamsa stage, one can actually experience that attraction to Krishna.

---
