## HG Jai Chaitanya Dasa

### 140  Why Chaitanya Mahaprabhu recommended Srimad Bhagavatam
**Speaker:** HG Jai Chaitanya Dasa

#### relationship with God, intimacy, ecstasy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9FNaZJum3ew?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The relationship that we have with that ultimate source of everything is not a passive relationship, but it is a relationship of ecstasy."
**Summary:** Chaitanya Mahaprabhu emphasized that our relationship with the ultimate source is not passive but filled with ecstasy, intimacy, and strength.

Chaitanya Mahaprabhu made Srimad Bhagavatam his most important authoritative scripture for his teaching. He says that the relationship that we have with that ultimate source of everything is not a passive relationship, but it is a relationship of ecstasy. It's a very strong, intimate relationship; it is not a very distant relationship.

---
#### intimacy with God, knowledge, Bhagavatam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9FNaZJum3ew?t=28
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Bhagavatam is giving us knowledge that the source of everything, the ultimate cause, who is the cause of all prime ministers of all countries in the world, with him we have an intimate relationship."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts our distant relationship with a prime minister to the intimate relationship Bhagavatam reveals we have with the ultimate source, leading to ecstasy and happiness.

Just like there is a prime minister of this country, we all know his name. Anything more we know about him, we don't know. Can we say he is my close friend? No, we don't know; he is not our close friend. We hardly know him; we see his picture in the newspaper, that's all. But here, Bhagavatam is giving us knowledge that the source of everything, the ultimate cause, who is the cause of all prime ministers of all countries in the world, with him we have an intimate relationship. And that relationship is an experience; understanding and acting at that relationship is an experience of ecstasy, of great happiness.

---
#### Srimad Bhagavatam, knowledge, duty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9FNaZJum3ew?t=73
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Srimad Bhagavatam is the transcendental science, not only for knowing the ultimate source of everything but also for knowing our relation with him and our duty toward perfection of the human society on the basis of this perfect knowledge."
**Summary:** Srimad Bhagavatam provides transcendental knowledge about the ultimate source, our relationship with it, and our duty towards perfecting human society.

Bhagavatam is giving knowledge of that. Srimad Bhagavatam is the transcendental science, not only for knowing the ultimate source of everything but also for knowing our relation with him. So, that's two things: knowing the source of everything and our relation. No, one more thing is there. Srimad Bhagavatam is the transcendental science not only for knowing the ultimate source of everything but also for knowing our relation with him and our duty toward perfection of the human society on the basis of this perfect knowledge. This knowledge is not only for knowing the ultimate source and our relationship, but also our duty. Each one of us have a duty; that is a duty in this human form of life, our duty toward perfection of the human society.

---
#### human society, perfection, duty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9FNaZJum3ew?t=135
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "We are talking about perfection of human society; there is a certain standard of perfection of human society, and all of us have a duty towards that perfection, attainment of perfection."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the duty towards perfecting human society based on perfect knowledge of our relationship with the ultimate source, highlighting Prabhupada's packed philosophical knowledge.

So, the human society, there are many types of cultures that we can see in the world at different times in the history of the world. They had varieties of cultures, but now we are talking about perfection of human society. There is a certain standard of perfection of human society, and all of us have a duty towards that perfection, attainment of perfection. So, and our duty toward perfection of the human society, and what is the basis of this perfection of human society we are talking about? On the basis of this perfect knowledge, perfect knowledge, perfect knowledge of our relationship with that ultimate source of it. So, we see in this one simple sentence Prabhupada has packed so much philosophical knowledge and information which tells about the ultimate source of everything, our relationship with him, and also tells us how we must act in this world.

---

### 2016-01-28 | SB 3.18.14-15 | HG Jai Chaitanya Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Jai Chaitanya Dasa

#### rasa, free will, relationship with God
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=167
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Rasa comes when there are unexpected situations."
**Summary:** The Lord creates living entities to experience rasa and relationship, giving them free will to allow for unexpected situations and deeper enjoyment, even if it means some choose to misuse it.

So it's a battle for the demon, but it is a friendly fight, a playful fight for the Supreme Lord, because the Supreme Lord does not have to fight with anybody. Right? He can only fight with one person, that is Himself. There is nobody else to fight with. But being the Supreme person, being a personality with all the emotions in its most supreme, excellent position, He also has the tendency to fight. What does He do? Just like He has a tendency to exchange emotions, He has a tendency to enjoy rasa, raso vai saha. He is full of rasa, and He is the enjoyer of all the rasas. So, what does He do? He has to create Radharani. He has to create all the sakis. He has to create all the devotees. He has to create all the living entities, so that one personality becomes many for the purpose of relationship, for the purpose of enjoyment. But if there has been enjoyment, and if there has been a relationship, then one thing He has to give up. He has to give up one thing, that is free will. If He doesn't give free will, if He is controlling the living entities He has created, if He is controlling all the people He has created, then it becomes... There is no rasa in that. Rasa comes when there are unexpected situations. You don't expect something. Suddenly something comes up. Then the enjoyment is more. If you already know what to expect, then there is no enjoyment there. When you receive something unexpectedly, then the rasa, the taste is more. You suddenly go somewhere you don't expect, suddenly get nice prasadam. Even though it is not tasty, you feel it is very tasty. You are very hungry. You didn't expect. So, to give that rasa of the unknown, the Lord gives up the free will. So the living entities have only one thing, that is his free will. Everything else is Lord. He is also part of the Lord, part and parcel of the Lord. There is no difference between the living entity and the Lord, except for the quantum. Both are spirit. Quantity wise maybe. So He gives that free will. So that is the free will which is used by the living entities. And using that free will, the Lord makes the living entity give Him pleasure. The living entities are part and parcel of the Lord. So just like the part and parcel is used by the whole, the whole, the Supreme Lord uses the living entities by making the living entities act the way He wants. But many of the living entities are not qualified. All of them, like us, find place in this material world. So that is how the Supreme Lord exchanges relationships.

---
#### Lord's pleasure, free will, material world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=366
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "All the bad words you throw upon the Lord is received as a loving flower."
**Summary:** The Lord's interactions with living entities are like a father-son relationship, where the Lord provides everything, even the means to 'fight' Him, and finds pleasure in the living entity's success, even when they try to imitate Him, ultimately creating the material world for those who misuse their free will.

So for Him here, it is a friendly fight, just like a father would fight. The gloves also father has given to the son. Right? Everything that the son has to fight the father. Suppose the father is a king. He wants to fight with the son. He gives a sword to the son. All the elements to fight is given by the father. And the father says, now you fight. Right? Sometimes the father shows he is also defeated, because the father feels pleasure in the success of the son. Similarly, the Lord also feels pleasure in the success of the living entities. Only one thing is not possible when the living entities start imitating and acting like the Lord. That is not possible, because there is only one Lord. So when Hiranyakasya, Hiranyakashipu, all the living entities try to act like the Lord, then, still the Lord is loving. The Lord doesn't say, you are dismissed. You are banned. If He bans, where will the living entity go? So He Himself has to create the living entity's place. So He creates a material world. And He says, all of you go there and try to become like Me. So we have Hiranyakasya and Hiranyakashipu here, trying to fight the Lord. But what is the use of fighting the Lord? All the bad words you throw upon the Lord is received as a loving flower. All the beating that Hiranyakasya does to the Lord is also received to Him as pleasure. So you cannot overcome that. You cannot overcome the Lord in any way. That's not possible. The living entity can get overcome by the Lord's love. So that is the interaction between the Lord and the living entities. And this material world, of course, is created.

---
#### humility, Lord's incarnations, devotee's pleasure
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=493
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord doesn't mind even taking such an so-called abominable, so-called lowly, so-called not cared for form of varaha. He doesn't mind."
**Summary:** The Lord takes even the most 'abominable' forms, like Varaha, for the pleasure of Himself and His devotees, demonstrating that even seemingly lowly forms can be useful and serve a purpose in the divine plan.

And in this material world, the Lord comes in most abominable form also. He doesn't mind. Paraha. Hedgehog, they say. Most abominable. Like before we saw Hiranyakasya, he is saying, you are such a lowly being you are. Lowly being of this, of a hog, of a varaha. Wild boar. Nobody cares for a wild boar. Why did He take the form of a wild boar? Because earth is lost. It has been sniffed. It has been smelled and found out. And nobody is as expert as smelling like wild boar. You cannot keep anything away from a wild boar. It will sniff and find out. It is a very lowly animal, but very expert. His sense of smelling is so strong. Right? Hiranyakasya cannot even hide earth from it. He will smell and find out. That is why boar incarnation. Just smell out the earth. Right? So human beings don't care for this. The Lord doesn't mind even taking such an so-called abominable, so-called lowly, so-called not cared for form of varaha. He doesn't mind. Varaha is very useful also. The mud which is tilled by the horns, by the horns of the varaha is very useful. They say, when for worship, we use this soil from the anthill, we use soil from the tulasi root, we use soil from the horn. In the morning, in the night, the hogs come and they till the soil to take the roots and eat. So that soil is used. When we do Brahmosa, we do Ankura Arbana, we use soil from different places. One of the soil is the soil from the horn, tilled by the horn of varaha. So we may not understand the usefulness of varaha anyway. So the point here is, the Lord doesn't mind taking this kind of form also for the pleasure of His pleasure and the pleasure of the devotees.

---
#### transcendentalism, dodging death, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=628
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So the process of dodging death, that is the goal of life."
**Summary:** The Lord's dodging of the maze is compared to a transcendentalist dodging death by disconnecting from the material world and realizing they are not the body, which is subject to death.

So that is the situation here. But here, Sukte Goswami very clearly explains, the Lord is dodging the maze of hidden action. So if you look at it, He is dancing, sidestepping, maze fight. Duryodhana, Bhima, all these people are great maze fighters. So we look at the maze fighters, if you look at any fighter, you have to see, you have to block others beating, you have to avoid others beating, you have to give a punch. So it is a real fighting. There is a big war going on there. So the Lord is dodging the maze, maze blows of hidden action, just like a transcendentalist would dodge death. So He gives that comparison. So the transcendentalists are expert in dodging death. How does that happen? Well, in the sense, dodging death means you avoid the influence of the material world, because death is part of this material world. Soul doesn't die. So when soul doesn't die, who is dying here? Transcendentalist doesn't die. He is soul. How can soul die? It is the body which is dying. Body which is a part of the material world, which is dying. Body which is the product of the influence of the three modes of material nature, which is dying. And He was never that body. So transcendentalists never die. It is the living entities who think that He is a body, which has an end, think that there is death. For a spiritualist, there is no death. So the Lord is dodging this maze, just like a Vaishnava, a Brahmana, a qualified spiritualist who avoids death. Well, not only the Vaishnavas, even a Mayavadi tries to dodge death. Any transcendentalist, whether he is a Mayavadi, he is a yogi, he is a Brahmana, he is a Vaishnava, they all dodge death. But dodging death is possible only when you disconnect with the material world. Until that disconnect comes, because you think, I am the body. As long as we think that we are the body, and the body ends, we cannot dodge. So the process of dodging death, that is the goal of life.

---
#### goal of life, body consciousness, transcendental qualities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=780
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "All unhappiness is because you think this is going to finish."
**Summary:** The goal of life is to avoid death by realizing one is not the body, leading to freedom from anxiety and a state of satisfaction, understanding that as part of the Lord, one has everything.

For the living entities who are this material world. Most of the people in the material world do not know what is the goal of life. So if they ask us, we will say, the goal of life is to avoid death. A scientist will say, not possible, you are wasting your time, because you have to die, because he is thinking that he is the body. But a devotee is thinking that he is not the body. He knows he is not the body. The fact is that he is not the body. When he is a spirit soul, how does death overcome spirit soul? Death overcomes spirit soul as soon as the spirit soul mis-sanitizes himself as a dieable object. So people in the material world, when we are absorbed in sense gratification, absorbed in the body, conscious of the body, the consciousness is always thinking of the body, the body's pleasures, body's pains, avoiding body's pain, acquiring body's happiness, when the entire action in his life is centered around the body, then he becomes body conscious. When he becomes body conscious, then he becomes death conscious. A transcendentalist is not death conscious, because he doesn't die. He doesn't disappear into thin air. He is always existing. So when he understands that, the first thing that comes in his heart is happiness, because all unhappiness is because you think this is going to finish. All sadness, all anxiety happens because I think I am going to lose this. So when you know that you are always going to exist, there is no point in finishing, then you don't have anxiety. So first sign of transcendentalist is free from anxiety. First sign is his tripti, he is satisfied. Why? Because he has no anxiety, he doesn't finish. So he is always rejoicing, he is always happy. So the quality of a transcendentalist is this, he is free from this fear, he is free from this anxiety, he is free from this dissatisfied situation of material things that I have only this much. I have everything, because the Lord has everything. I am part of the Lord. Whatever belongs to the Lord is mine. There is no limit for a devotee. There is no limitation for a transcendentalist who thinks that he is not the body. So that is the qualification of a transcendentalist.

---
#### sadhana, dodging death, balance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=947
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The same way it is said that you have to maintain trigunas, balance of trigunas because mind is influenced by these trigunas."
**Summary:** The process of dodging death involves daily efforts to avoid disease by balancing tridoshas and to balance trigunas to maintain mental happiness, ultimately aiming to become free from the influence of the three modes of material nature.

The point, how does he avoid? So that is the process every individual person, a sadhaka, like us, the whole day, whole life is spent in how do we dodge? We dodge disease, illness. Nobody wants disease. Keep good health. I know they say, tridoshas, you have tridoshas. Any imbalance in this tridoshas creates ill health. So we maintain this balance of these tridoshas so that you don't become ill. There is no disease. The same way it is said that you have to maintain trigunas, balance of trigunas because mind is influenced by these trigunas. So as much as the balance of the trigunas are important for the body, the balance of the tridoshas are important for the body, the balance of trigunas are important for the mind because happiness is what? It is a mental concept. Body is unhappy when it is diseased. Mind is happy when it is free from the influence of three modes or it has more of sattva guna. So this is the daily fight a transcendentalist faces. How does he balance or rather how does he avoid the three modes of material nature? Triguna rahitaha. How to be free from the influence of three modes? So as soon as you become free from the three modes, you become free from death.

---
#### material world, perverted reflection, three modes
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=1039
**Verse References:** BG 15.1
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This world is nothing but an upside down of a good world."
**Summary:** The material world is described as a perverted reflection of the spiritual world, like an upside-down tree nourished by the three modes of material nature, with the living entities' actions feeding its growth.

For such a person, there is no death. Vaishnava has never died. In fact, no living entity dies. But Vaishnava has never died because he is totally free from this concept of death. He is free from the influence of the three modes of material nature. So that is why Krishna as in 15th chapter He says, a person who understands this material world, he understands very clearly what is happening in this world. Hence He says, what is this world? In 15th chapter of Gita, most important chapter, where Krishna says, urdhamulam adasyakam. You want to know what this world is? This world is nothing but an upside down of a good world. This is a perverted reflection of the right world. This is a wrong house. This is an upside down house. You belong to a house which is totally different. But I can try to explain to you, this world is an upside down world. And this is like a tree which is standing on a reservoir. The reflection is seen on the reservoir. The roots are up. The branches are down. The leaves are down. The leaves are the Vedas. The twigs are the fruits of the senses, objects of the senses, sense activities. And this tree also has new roots coming. This tree also has new shoots coming. And this tree is fed by the three modes of material nature, sattva guna, raja guna, tamma guna. It is like growing up a tree using manure. What is the manure for this tree? Urea for this tree is sattva guna, raja guna, tamma guna. These three gunas are the nutrients by which the tree is growing. And what is the fruit of the tree? The new twigs. And all this is resting in the action of the living entities. If the living entity is acting in this material world, using these three gunas, sattva guna, raja guna, tamma guna, which are the nutrients, naturally new roots will come. Just like in a banyan tree, new roots come. Very thin roots come. When they are very small, easy to pick and throw.

---
#### samsara, bondage, cutting roots
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=1174
**Verse References:** BG 15.3-4
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Actions in this material world is a cause of bondage."
**Summary:** The tree of samsara grows stronger with actions influenced by the three modes, creating bondage, but a transcendentalist can cut the roots of this tree with a knife of detachment and escape this bondage.

But as they touch the ground, and they also start sucking the three gunas, the nutrients, they also grow strong. So by the action of the living entities, by the influence of three modes of material nature, the nutrients are going to this tree called samsara. And this tree called samsara is growing and growing and growing. And it comes to a point where you cannot cut it anymore easily. The more nutrient is taken, the tree becomes very strong. You cannot cut it. A tree which is very small, you can easily take it away. But allow it to grow. Allow the extra roots to come. Who is feeding it? Nobody external is coming and feeding it. We ourselves are feeding it by our action. What kind of action? The actions which are influenced by either sattva guna, raja guna, tamma guna or a combination of these. So actions in this material world is a cause of bondage. And Lord says, he who knows this tree, he can dodge death. He can be free from the influence of this tree. Don't try to understand this tree. You figuratively know what this tree is, but don't try to go deep to the tree. But what the transcendentalist do? He doesn't go deep. He takes a knife and cuts the roots. As fast as possible, he can cut the roots and come out of this bondage to this earth. And you belong to a different world.

---
#### liberation, free will, dodging death
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=1271
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Spiritual life only means that taking that aspect of action and putting that action in a different place which gives better result and the result is liberation."
**Summary:** We belong to a world free from limitations, but are now limited by death. A transcendentalist cheats death by using their free will to act in a way that leads to liberation, rather than bondage.

Not that bhaa sheti, surya ho, na shashanko, na pavaka ha. A world where there is no need of any artificial light. A world where there is no need of any sadness, no sorrow, no limitation, no anxiety. A world which is totally free. So we belong to a world which is as free as them. As free as that. But now we belong to a world where there is limitations by death. Hence, a transcendentalist becomes expert in dodging death. Cheat death. How does he do that? The medicine is with him. His own actions. When a transcendentalist uses his action through his free will, in a particular way, he gets bound. When he uses his free will in another way, he gets liberated. So that one item what the Lord has given, that is a free will, action means free will. You are free to act whichever you want. So He has given that free will. The same free will with which you would give pleasure to the Lord is now misused to become sad, is misused to be limited by death, is misused to be mixed in anxiety. So spiritual life only means that taking that aspect of action and putting that action in a different place which gives better result and the result is liberation. And when these actions are used in the activity of liberation, then you are called a person who is dodging death.

---
#### dodging death, constant vigilance, cutting roots
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=1372
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You have to dodge death every minute because any second you can get stuck."
**Summary:** Dodging death is the goal of life, requiring constant vigilance and action to cut the roots of material attachment, as the material tree grows in our hearts through misused actions.

And that is the goal of life. Every minute, every second, that is a Buddha Yoga. This is not some artificial emotion, this is a Buddha Yoga. It is a minute to minute thing. You have to dodge death every minute because any second you can get stuck. Like in Gita, Krishna says any one of the senses, inattentively, can create another root. Root is going so fast. So you have to be a very smart fighter. Take the knife and you have to be very fast. Every time keep cutting, cutting, cutting. What am I doing this medieval? Keep cutting the roots. Thin the tree down. Don't allow the tree to go lost. And this tree, where is the tree growing? In our hearts. So if this material tree is made to grow thick by our misused actions, misplaced actions in the material world, then we end up in dying. The moment we take it out, we become free from death. No death. Hence just like a transcendentalist who is trying to dodge death, the Lord is fighting. So the cue here is how does a transcendentalist dodge death?

---
#### goal of life, Lord's emotions, struggle
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=1452
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This world is made so real by the Lord that we have to struggle."
**Summary:** The goal of life is to dodge death, and the Lord's actions and emotions, even fear, are real, reflecting the struggle and endeavor inherent in this world.

That becomes the goal of life. Apparently it looks like the Lord is in anxiety. He is trying to get freedom from this maze, powerful maze of Hirinaksha. He is scared of Hirinaksha. Lord also shows His emotions of fear. Arjuna caused so much of fear to the Lord. We know when Abhimanyu was killed. Arjuna was so struck with sorrow. He forgot what he see, what he should say. He said, if I don't kill this person tomorrow, I will die. He said, what are you saying? This is exactly what the Kauravas are looking at. Either to kill you or make a situation where you die yourself. Kshatriyas are like that. If they don't commit, if they don't do what they say, then they say, okay, I give up this. I give up that. I kill myself. I enter fire. Arjuna said, I will enter fire. The Lord said, the Lord said, I didn't sleep in the night. The Lord also doesn't sleep in the night. He also has anxiety. For what? For the cause of the devotees. So the Lord has this rasas. Why? Because this world is real. There is pain and pleasure in this world. There is endeavor in this world. There is hard work in this world. There is frustration in this world. This world is made so real by the Lord that we have to struggle.

---
#### endeavor, limitations, Lord's sanction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=1540
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Somebody is controlling this world. I am not free."
**Summary:** The world is characterized by endeavor, limitations imposed by time, past karma, and the Lord's sanction, leading to the understanding that someone is controlling this world.

Three things are there in this world. One is our endeavor. Every time we endeavor for something to success, achieve something. Free from hatred, free from happiness, free from unhappiness, or achieve happiness. These are two things we do. Endeavor is always there. But we are limited. We are not free to endeavor. Not that you do anything, you will get the result. You are limited by time, limited by our past, limited by our own karma. Right? And then, even if you have good karma, sometimes you may not get it. Why? Large interference is there. So this world is made so real, that one has to endeavor. While endeavoring, one is limited by one's past. And even if it is limited by the past, if the Lord's sanction is there, you get it. So by seeing this world, one understands there is something different. Somebody is controlling this world. I am not free. If I was free, I should work hard and I should get the result. But you see people working hard, opposite result. Viparyaya, you get the opposite. You try for something, but you get the exact opposite. Or sometimes people don't work at all, he is getting everything automatically. So big debate in Shastra is, what is the reason for result in this world? Is it endeavor? Is it karma? Or the Lord? Of course, it is a combination of all these three.

---
#### free will, karma, Maya, action
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=1645
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna has created this world where He makes the living entities endeavor."
**Summary:** Krishna has created a world where living entities endeavor using their free will, but are limited by karma, and Prabhupada advises devotees to be absorbed in expanding the Krishna consciousness movement to be free from Maya.

So Krishna has created this world where He makes the living entities endeavor. Work hard, work hard, work hard. Again action. Again free will. The same free will what He has given to the living entities, He is making the living entities work hard. Endeavor, endeavor, endeavor. But, you have come and gone many times here. So many pending accounts are there. It depends upon that. Even great athletes work hard day and night they work. On the day of the play, no result. So they say, I didn't have a good day. So having good day is important or struggling for so many years is important. Why struggle at all? Some people don't struggle at all. You have a good day by your past karma. So the world is made so real by the Lord, by a combination of all these things. And the living entities who understand this, he knows, I am limited by all these things. And finally, I am limited by death. So how do you dodge death? Say a person dodging death means he dodges all these influences of karma, influences of time, influences of the three modes. Then he can dodge. So this world is a very practical world. It is not just, and spiritual life is not just sentimentalism. Like Prabhupada said, it's an art of working. It's a place of action. A lot of activities are happening. The devotees are engaged. When devotees ask Prabhupada, what is the best way to be free from Maya? How to be free? It's very difficult to be free. We are sitting in Maya. Sitting in the influence of all these modes. Prabhupada says, totally be absorbed in expanding this movement. It's very difficult to be free. Totally be absorbed in expanding this movement.

#### action, Krishna consciousness, preaching
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=1758
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This is not a place of vishram. It's a place of action. It's a place of great battle."
**Summary:** Expanding the Krishna consciousness movement involves dealing with many problems, making it a place of action and battle, and Prabhupada's simple formula for dodging death is to engage in Krishna consciousness and its expansion.

Because expanding this movement means what? This problem, that problem, this thing, that thing, this devotee, that devotee, man, material, all kinds of problems are there. So sometimes we think, is this spiritualism? I thought ashram means vishram. Ashram means vishram. Prabhupada said, this is not a place of vishram. It's a place of action. It's a place of great battle. It's a place of great battle. Great problem. We don't think. We come in a temple, we sit and everything is there. Prasad comes in time. Dress comes in time. Vehicle comes and stops. So sometimes we forget. There is a great endeavor required to achieve all these things. But Krishna gives all these things free for the devotees. Why does Krishna give these things free for the devotees? So internally, He can engage all these things in Krishna's service. So Prabhupada says, we want to be from maya. So dodging death, a transcendentally sitting om, doing all these hatha yogas, controlling his breath, controlling the five ayus in the body, detaching the mind from the senses, or a mayavadi saying, neti neti neti, not this, not this, not this, and trying to be away from this material world. A devotee, free. Easily he can dodge death. What is it? Engage in Krishna consciousness. Engage in expansion of Krishna consciousness.

#### preaching, real world, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1sZaBCmjaYQ?t=1858
**Verse References:** SB 1.2.6
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This world is real. As much as its temporariness is understood, but it is real."
**Summary:** Prabhupada's simple formula for dodging death is to engage in Krishna consciousness and its expansion. Devotees understand the world is real, not unreal, because it gives the real fruit of liberation.

Such a simple formula Prabhupada has given. Dodging death? No problem. No need of hatha yoga. No need of mental speculations. No need of even Sankhya yoga. Sankhya yoga is also one of the methods of dodging death. Analyzing this material world and finding out the intrinsic. Sarvasya sarvaatmana atmavasthuna. The Lord is everywhere. He is in, He is the heart of everything and He is also the intrinsic aspect of everything. Intrinsic quality of everything. There is nothing other than Him. No need of that also. Simply act. Simply act in the activity of preaching. Expanding the movement. So it's a real world. In this real world, mayavadis say this world is not real. Devotees don't say that. This world is real. As much as its temporariness is understood, but it is real. Because if it is unreal, you won't get a real fruit. Just like you say, this is an image of a mango tree, but I got real mango. You can't get a real mango from an image of a mango tree. You have to go to a real mango tree, climb and get the fruits. So if liberation is the fruit of the actions in the material world, how can I get liberation from a false world? And a false world? Non-existing world? So the understanding of the devotee is that the world is Bhagavata understanding. Bhagavata conclusion is that the world is not unreal. World is real. Because it gives real fruit, which is liberation. That is the purpose of the world. You may not use it for that purpose.

### Gaura Mandala Parikrama 2024 | Virachandra Prabhu, Son of Lord Nityananda | Sri Jai Chaitanya Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Jai Chaitanya Dasa

#### divine intervention, devotion, forgiveness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mhmDSyTj43k?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Bhirachandra Prabhu then took the stone back to Kardaha by floating it into the Ganges, as if it was too heavy to board onto a boat."
**Summary:** HG Jai Chaitanya Dasa narrates the story of how Sri Bhirachandra Prabhu obtained the Shaligram Shila from the Nawab's palace in Murshidabad, how the food turned into flowers, and how he installed Sri Shamsundaraji in Kardaha.

It is stated in Prema Vilasa that Sri Bhirachandra Prabhu, after having received appropriate instructions from his father, Nithyananda Prabhu, to install Sri Shamasundraji in Kardaha, had set out to obtain the Shaligram Shila from the Nawab's palace in Murshidabad, from which the deity needed to be carved. Sri Bhirachandra Prabhu's reputation preceded him, and the Nawab greeted him with full honor on his arrival. However, the Nawab had evil intentions. Sri Bhirachandra's vow of feeding him meat. Hence, the Nawab thought that feeding meat to this great Vaishnava would end up derailing him from his religious path and shall thereby spoil his reputation. However, when Nawab attempted to serve him the non-veg dishes, the food automatically got transformed into roses and jasmine. The Nawab ordered his chief to bring new items. But again, the potency of Sri Bhirachandra the food got transformed into flowers. The Nawab got frustrated in his attempts and finally came to realize that Sri Bhirachandra Prabhu was a great devotee and no ordinary personality. Feeling remorseful and guilty, the Nawab fell at his lotus feet and begged him for forgiveness. Bhirachandra Prabhu, feeling merciful, forgave him. Bhirachandra Prabhu then informed the Nawab, "I saw you eating the food. I saw a huge stone above your front gate. I would be happy to have it so that I can carve a nice deity out of it." The Nawab was very happy to give his consent. Bhirachandra Prabhu then took the stone back to Kardaha by floating it into the Ganges, as if it was too heavy to board onto a boat. To the surprise of all, the Shaligramma stone floated down the river and ultimately reached the banks of Kardaha, where it was carefully recovered. While meditating at Kunjavatika, Sri Bhirachandra had received the beautiful darshana of Sri Shamsundaraji in his heart. In his trance, he received orders from his father, Nithyananda Prabhu, to fetch the Shaligramma Silla from the Nawab's place and subsequently install Shamsundaraji here in Kardaha. This concerned Shaligramma Silla had a long history. This was the same stone on which Kamsa, the wicked maternal uncle of Sri Krishna, had murdered the six children of Devaki over 5000 years ago. Somehow the stone had landed up in the custody of the Nawab. As Bhirachandra Prabhu entered the Nawab's palace, he noticed that the stone was perspiring and understood that it was the same Shaligramma Silla that he had come looking for. Sri Bhirachandra then engaged a sculptor who fulfilled his earnest desire by carving the captivating deity of Lord Shamsundara from this Shaligramma Silla. The first two deities that were sculpted didn't match the exact form of Shri Shamsundaraji that Bhirachandra had witnessed in his meditation. Hence, he sent these first two deities to Shirampur and Saiban, where they are worshipped today as Shri Radha Vallabhji and Shri Radha Nandudulalji respectively. The third deity which manifested from the Shaligramma Silla was an exact match, and Shri Bhirachandra then proceeded to install and worship it. Indeed, the people of Kardaha forgot their distress when they saw this most attractive deity. Bhirachandra Prabhu arranged a grand festival to install the deity with great pomp and show. And so, inviting many devotees and feeding all of them sumptuously, the most merciful Achyutananda, the son of Advaitacharya, performed the abhisheka of Shri Radha Shamsundaraji. Shri Nithyananda Prabhu ki. Shri Bhirachandra ji ki. Shri Radha Shamsundar ki. Jai.

#### Ramachandra Kaviraj, Lineage, Devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_VjnUMFWj3s?t=206
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Ramachandra Kaviraj is an eternal disciple and imparted Lord Gauranga's teachings, highlighting the missed opportunities for Krishna's service."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the lineage and qualities of Ramachandra Kaviraj, identifying his parents, his connection to Lord Chaitanya, his mastery of Vedic scriptures, and his surrender to Srinivas Acharya.

They are the two sons of Chiranjeeva and Sunanda Devi. Shri Krishnadas Kaviraj identifies Chiranjeeva, a resident of Shrikanta, as the 41st branch of Lord Chaitanya's desiratory of devotional service. Shri Ramachandra Kaviraj, an eternal associate of Lord Chaitanya, embodies Karna Manchari in Krishna's divine pastimes. He mastered Vedic scriptures and healed both materially and spiritually. Ramachandra, amazed by his beauty, renounced wealth and surrendered to Srinivas Acharya, who accepted him as his son. Ramachandra Kaviraj is an eternal disciple and imparted Lord Gauranga's teachings, highlighting the missed opportunities for Krishna's service.

---
#### Narottam Das Thakur, Enlightenment, Literary Brilliance, Devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_VjnUMFWj3s?t=265
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Ramachandra Kaviraj, honored as a poet by Sri Jeeva Goswami, graced Vrindavan with his literary brilliance, leaving behind timeless works."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses how Narottam Das Thakur enlightened Gandharayan Chakraborty and Janganath Acharya, the subsequent philosophical debate, and Ramachandra Kaviraj's literary contributions and devotion, culminating in his departure after Srinivasa Acharya.

Gandharayan Chakraborty, a scholar in Gamvila, was enlightened by Narottam Das Thakur's teaching and sought refuge at his Lord's feet. Janganath Acharya, a worshipper of Goddess Durga, was also enlightened by Narottam Das Thakur's teaching and sought refuge at his Lord's feet. Janganath Acharya, a worshipper of Goddess Durga, was also enlightened by Narottam Das Thakur's teaching and sought refuge at his Lord's feet. Janganath Acharya, a worshipper of Goddess Durga, was also enlightened by Narottam Das Thakur's teaching and sought refuge at his Lord's feet. Janganath Acharya, a worshipper of Goddess Durga, was also enlightened by Narottam Das Thakur's teaching and sought refuge at his Lord's feet. Janganath Acharya, a worshipper of Goddess Durga, was also enlightened by Narottam Das Thakur's teaching and sought refuge at his Lord's feet. Janganath Acharya, a worshipper of Goddess Durga, was also enlightened by Narottam Das Thakur's teaching and sought refuge at his Lord's feet. Envy grew among Smartha Brahmins leading to King Narasimha's intervention in a philosophical debate. Chakravarti disguised themselves as a potter and beet-leaf seller convincingly defeated both the Smartha Brahmanas and Pandita Rupa Narayana. King Narasimha realized his mistake and surrendered to Narottama who forgave and initiated them. Ramachandra Kaviraj, honored as a poet by Sri Jeeva Goswami, graced Vrindavan with his literary brilliance, leaving behind timeless works. His departure following the passing of Srinivasa Acharya marked the culmination of their earthly endeavors. The sacred Samadhis in Dheera Samira Kunj, Vrindavan stand as silent testaments to their everlasting devotion and contribution to spirit. Ramachandra Kaviraj and Sri Jeeva Goswami were the only ones who were able to do so.

### Gaura Mandala Parikrama | Srinivasa Acharya: His Birth & Spiritual Legacy | Sri Jai Chaitanya Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Jai Chaitanya Dasa

#### birth, divine love, nickname
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0EW4AFSZjrk?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When other devotees saw him intoxicated in divine love for Mahaprabhu, they nicknamed him Chaitanya Das, a name that stuck with him for the rest of his life."
**Summary:** This segment describes the birth of Srinivas Acharya's father, Gangadhar Bhattacharya, and how he received the name Chaitanya Das due to his intense devotion.

Srinivas Acharya was born in 1530 A.D. On the full moon day of the month of Vaisakha, in the village of Chakhandi, in the district of Nadia, his father, Gangadhar Bhattacharya, was a Brahmin of the Rarha class. The Bhakti Ratnakara describes the event as follows: When Mahaprabhu took sannyas in Kathua, Gangadhar Bhattacharya was overcome with grief and began to cry constantly while calling out the names, Ha Chaitanya, Ha Chaitanya. When other devotees saw him intoxicated in divine love for Mahaprabhu, they nicknamed him Chaitanya Das, a name that stuck with him for the rest of his life.

---
#### blessings, divine prediction, son
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0EW4AFSZjrk?t=65
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Indeed, his wife will give birth to a jewel of a son, who will be named Srinivas. He will be the manifest form of my love and will be non-different from me."
**Summary:** This segment narrates how Chaitanya Dasa and his wife received Mahaprabhu's blessings for a son, Srinivas, and the prophecy about his future role.

Chaitanya Dasa had no desire for the things of this world, but one day, he suddenly felt a strong wish to have a son and told this to his wife, Leshmipriya. She responded by telling him to immediately go to Puri and ask Mahaprabhu for his blessings. The couple left for Puri, stopping for a few days at the house of Balaram Vibhra, Leshmipriya's father, in Jaji Gram. When they arrived in Puri, Chaitanya Dasa and his wife paid their obeisances to Mahaprabhu, but before they could speak, the Lord himself said, understanding their desire, Jagannath Deva will surely fulfill all your desires. The other devotees were curious about what desire Mahaprabhu was talking about, and they asked his servant Govinda, but before he could say anything, Mahaprabhu called Govinda and gave him the answer: Chaitanya Dasa decides to have a son. Indeed, his wife will give birth to a jewel of a son, who will be named Srinivas. He will be the manifest form of my love and will be non-different from me. He will increase everyone's love. He will increase everyone's enthusiasm for devotion. Through Rupa and others, I will bring scriptures into the world, and through Srinivas, I shall distribute them.

---
#### birth, education, death of father
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0EW4AFSZjrk?t=157
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Srinivas Acharya was devoted to his parents. He was fortunate to hear about Mahaprabhu's divine glories as well as Radha and Krishna's Vrindavan pastimes from his father."
**Summary:** This segment describes Srinivas Acharya's birth, his devotion to his parents, his education, and the death of his father.

Once they had received Mahaprabhu's blessing, Chaitanya Dasa and his wife returned to his home, and when the auspicious moment came, a son was born to them. Chaitanya Dasa immediately offered the child to Mahaprabhu. Srinivas Acharya was devoted to his parents. He was fortunate to hear about Mahaprabhu's divine glories as well as Radha and Krishna's Vrindavan pastimes from his father. The two of them would go into an ecstatic state as they discussed these transcendental topics. Srinivas's mother trained him in Kirtan. He studied grammar, literature, and poetic theory from Dhananjaya Vidya Vachaspati and quickly acquired mastery of these subjects. Not long afterwards, Srinivas's father died. The loss of his devotee father's association greatly affected Srinivas.

---
#### loss, divine intervention, determination
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0EW4AFSZjrk?t=271
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Srinivas Acharya fainted at the news and when he came back to consciousness, he decided to commit suicide. However, the Lord himself appeared to him in a dream and told him to complete his journey to Puri."
**Summary:** This segment recounts Srinivas Acharya's reaction to Mahaprabhu's disappearance, his suicidal thoughts, and the Lord's intervention in a dream.

Srinivas Acharya fainted at the news and when he came back to consciousness, he decided to commit suicide. However, the Lord himself appeared to him in a dream and told him to complete his journey to Puri. Once in Puri, he again had a dream in which he saw Jagannath, Subhadra, and Baladev, as well as Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, with his associates. The incarnation of Mahaprabhu's pleasure potency, Srila Gadhadara Pandit Goswami, met him and both of them were immersed in an ocean of ecstasy. Srinivas also met Raya Ramananda, Paramananda Puri, Sakhi Mahayati, Sarvabhauma Bhatt, Sarvabhauma Pandit, Shikhi Mahayati, Sarvabhauma Pandit, Prakreshwara Pandit, and Srinivas Acharya. They met Govinda, Shankara Pandit, Gopinath Acharya and received blessings from them all. Srinivas Acharya stayed for some time in Puri, hearing Srimad Bhagavatam from Gadhadara Pandit Goswami. Gadhadara then told him to return to Gouda. When Srinivas returned, he heard that Advaita Acharya and Nityananda Prabhu had also disappeared. Once again, he determined to end his life, but the two Prabhus appeared to him in a dream and had him give up his intention.

#### grief, blessings, pilgrimage
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0EW4AFSZjrk?t=354
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Srinivas then wandered throughout Bengal, going to all the three parts of the associates of Mahaprabhu and Nityananda Prabhu, seeking their blessings."
**Summary:** This segment describes Srinivas Acharya's sorrow upon returning to Navadvipa, receiving blessings from Vishnupriya Devi and Satchi Devi, and his pilgrimage to various holy places in Bengal.

When he arrived in Navadvipa, Srinivas was once again plunged into a morose of sorrow at the loss of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. Vamshi Vadanananda Gotthakur saw him in this condition and approached Vishnupriya Devi and asked her to grant an audience to Srinivas and bless him. Srinivas Acharya was amazed to see the intense renunciation and deep faith of Mahaprabhu's widow. While in Navadvipa, he had a dream of Satchi Devi and received blessings from her too. Srinivas then wandered throughout Bengal, going to all the three parts of the associates of Mahaprabhu and Nityananda Prabhu, seeking their blessings. He visited Shantipur, Kardaha, Kanakula, Krishnanagar, Srikanta, etc., met Murari Gupta, Sivasa Pandit, Damodara Pandit, Shukkaam Brahma Brahmachari, Gadadhar Das, Parameshwari Das, Jnanava Devi, Vasudha Devi, Veerabhadra, Averam Thakur, Narahari Sarkar Thakur, Raghunandan Thakur. All these great personalities witnessed Srinivas' intense devotion and told him to go to Vrindavana. Srinivas then went to his mother and asked her repeatedly for permission to make the trip to Vrindavan, which she could not refuse when she saw his great eagerness.

#### pilgrimage, initiation, instruction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0EW4AFSZjrk?t=444
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He was initiated by Gopalabhatta and took instruction in the Vaishnava scriptures from Jeeva."
**Summary:** This segment details Srinivas Acharya's journey to Vrindavan, his encounters with prominent Vaishnavas, his initiation by Gopalabhatta, and his scriptural studies under Jeeva Goswami.

Srinivasa then travelled through Agra, Dvipa, Khatva, Maureshwara, Eka Chakra to Kashi, Ayodhya and Prayag. He spent a long time travelling and visiting all these holy places before finally arriving at Vraja. There he heard that Rupa Goswami, Sanatan Goswami, Kashi Shwara Pandit and Raghunath Bhatta Goswami had all disappeared. Raghunath Das Goswami, Gopalabhatta Goswami and Jeeva Goswami were still living. However, Srinivas met all three of them and received their blessings. He was initiated by Gopalabhatta and took instruction in the Vaishnava scriptures from Jeeva. In great affection for Srinivasa, Jeeva commended him to his deities, Radha Dhamodara. Srinivasa met Raghunath Das and Krishna Das Kaviraj in Radha Kunda and received their blessings. One day, Sri Jeeva heard Srinivasa explain a verse from Ujjwala Ninamani and was so impressed by his erudition that he bestowed the title Acharya upon him. Jeeva also gave the title. Jeeva gave the title Thakur to Narottama and Shamananda and Shamananda name to Dukkhi Krishna Das. Jeeva ordered Srinivas to tour the sites of pilgrimages in Braja area with Raghava Goswami. After Srinivas had attained mastery of the Gaudiya Vaishnava scriptures. Sri Jeeva and other Vaishnavas in Braja ordered him, Narottama and Srinivas to take manuscripts of these most remembered scriptures.

#### theft, divine plan, misunderstanding
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0EW4AFSZjrk?t=556
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The robbers seized the ox-cart and the books, thinking they were gold and jewels, and turned them over to the king."
**Summary:** This segment narrates the story of how the valuable manuscripts being transported by Srinivas, Narottama, and Shamananda were stolen by robbers who mistook them for treasure.

Valuable books back to bangal in an ox cart it was a long and dangerous trip but they were able to cover the greater part of the distance without any trouble upon arriving in the hindu kingdom of pana vishnupura they felt that the danger was over however the rumor had reached the capital of vishnupura here that a rich merchant was traveling to pulli with a valuable cargo of merchandise the ruler of the kingdom led a gang of high when highwaymen and when he heard that a cargo of great value was passing through his kingdom he asked an astrologer to divine the truth of the rumors the astrologer confirmed that this was indeed a cargo of great value the king immediately sent a group of robbers to steal it, specifying that they should kill no one. The robbers first worshipped the goddess Chandi, then sent out a spy who returned to tell them that everyone in the group accompanying the ox-cart was sleeping in exhaustion. After eating their night meal, the robbers thought that this was surely Chandi's mercy that a golden opportunity like this had come their way. They seized the ox-cart and the books, thinking they were gold and jewels, and turned them over to the king. When Vishnupura's king saw the large chest, he was delighted at the prospect of finding a large amount of valuables inside. How disappointed he must have been to see nothing but books inside. He turned to the astrologer and asked him how he could have made such a mistake. The astrologer answered, I cannot understand it. Every time I made my calculation, it came up with the same conclusion. This chest is full of priceless jewels. It is incredible. I don't know why my calculations have gone wrong. The mere sight of the holy books

#### purification, regret, hope
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0EW4AFSZjrk?t=687
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The mere sight of the holy books had a purifying effect on the king."
**Summary:** This segment describes how the king was purified by the sight of the holy books and regretted stealing them, and how Srinivas and his companions felt hope after initially being devastated by the loss.

had a purifying effect on the king. This king is King Virahambar of this place. We are in his kingdom. Be careful with any valuables you have. However, and he regretted having stolen them. Indeed, he desired to meet with the acharya of the books. The next morning, Srinivasa, Narottama, and Shamananda awoke to find the chest of the books missing. They searched everywhere, but when they failed to find any clue to the books whereabout, they felt so depressed that they considered killing themselves. Some local villagers saw the distressed condition of the three Vaishnavas and immediately suspected the robber king Veerahambira. Srinivasa made some further investigations and was assured that he would be able to find more information about the king himself. The three Vaishnavas felt some hope and abandoned their suicidal intentions. Srinivasa Acharya decided to remain in Vishnupura in the hope of finding the books, but he sent Narottama to Keturi and Shamananda to Orissa. While staying in Vishnupura, Srinivasa heard from a Brahmin named Krishna Vallabha that Veerahambira liked to listen to Srimad Bhagavatam and daily attended classes. On the next day, Srinivasa went with his Brahmin to the hall where the Bhagavata classes were held. The Brahmin introduced Srinivasa to the king as a great Vaishnava, and the king indeed observed the characteristics of the Brahmin. The king expressed a desire to hear Srinivasa speak on the Bhagavata, and Srinivasa agreed, thinking that this might be a good opportunity to recover the books.

**Summary:** This segment describes how Srinivas Acharya's lectures on the Bhagavata enchanted the king, leading to the king's confession of the theft and the return of the books.

So he started to give lectures on the Bhagavata on a daily basis. The king was enchanted by Srinivasa's unprecedented explanations of the Bhagavata in his sweet voice. Srinivasa's acharyas primarily preached Bahan Prabhu's Dharma through speaking on the Bhagavata and Kirtan, while Narottama and Shamananda primarily used the medium of Kirtan. The styles of Kirtan that these three acharyas used were named Manohara Sahi, Garana Hati, and Reneti. ViraHambira arranged for a secluded dwelling to be given to Srinivasa Acharya. One day, when he had the opportunity to find the king alone in his apartment, Srinivasa recounted the entire story of the journey from Raja and the theft of the manuscripts. The king immediately confessed, showing great sorrow for his misdeed, and had the chest of books returned to its owner.

In the course of time, Veerahambira and his entire entourage all took initiation from Srinivasa and dedicated their entire lives and wealth to him. After staying in Vana Vishnupura for some time. This place is Vana Vishnupura. The forest is totally different from the dry areas we came from yesterday. Very unusual atmosphere in this kingdom. After staying in Vana Vishnupura for some time, Srinivasa Acharya returned to his maternal grandfather's house in Jaji Gram. Then he set off on another tour to Kathua, Navadvipa, and other important towns in Bengal. Srinivasa Acharya is that person who donated his life to the local church. He is the chief priest of the holy place of the Vrindavan. The holy place of Vrindavan. Trip to the ancientynes of Dhaka. Srinivasa respected Narahari Sarkar of Shrikhanda greatly and was also very devoted to his saintly mother. When Narahari heard Srinivasa's mother express her desire to see her son married, he gave him the order to do so. Even prior to receiving this order, Srinivasa had received the same command from Advaita Acharya in a dream. Though he felt somewhat ashamed, he realized that he could not ignore the wishes of Narahari. His mother and Advaita Prabhu, he thus agreed to get married and was wed to Ishwari, the devoted daughter of Gopala Chakravarti of Jajigraha.

**Summary:** This segment describes Srinivas Acharya's grief over the passing of several devotees, his subsequent trip to Vrindavan for solace, and his participation in various festivals.

Srinivasa Acharya once again felt overcome by grief and made another trip to Vrindavan. Once again he met the Gopalbhata and Sri Jiva Goswamis as well as with Bhugarba and Lokarnath Goswami. And these great devotees spoke to him encouragingly, reducing his feelings of loss. Ramachandra Kaviraj and Shamananda Prabhu also joined Srinivas in Vraja on this occasion. When he returned to Gouda, Srinivas joined in the festivities occasioning the anniversaries of the departure of Gadadara Das in Katwa, Narahari in Srikhanda and Dujahari Das in Kanchanagiriya. From there he went to Budhari village where Ramachandra Kaviraj and Govinda Kaviraj hosted him with great uplands. Narottama Das Thakur had also left Vrindavan on Lokarnath Goswami's orders, returning to his home in Keturi. On the full moon day of Phalgunam, he established the worship of six sets of deities in the temple: Koranga, Vallabhikanta, Rajamohana, Shri Krishna, Radhakanta and Radharavana. Srinivasa Acharya performed the installation ceremonies of the first puja. Janava Devi was also present at this great event. After the festival, she went on to Vraja with her and Raj.

#### pilgrimage, divine order, marriage, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0EW4AFSZjrk?t=1129
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The pure devotee has no other objective in life than to fulfill the desires of the Supreme Lord. They are thus ready to do anything if it pleases the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment describes Srinivas Acharya's travels, his second marriage based on a divine order, and emphasizes the selfless devotion of pure devotees.

Upon her return, she met with Srinivas Acharya at Katwa and then went with him to spend some time in Jajigram. Srinivas Acharya made another trip to Navadip with Narottama and Ramachandra Kaviraj, performing the parikrama of the nine islands, each of which represents one of the limbs of devotional service. When Raghunandan Thakur entered the Nitya Leela, Srinivas Acharya went to Shrikanta to help conduct the funeral festivals in his honor. He returned with a heavy heart to Jajigram and from there went to see his disciples in Vanavishnupura. He was received by the excited king and the other residents of the temple. There, he received an order from Mahaprabhu himself in a dream to accept the hand of Gaurangapriya, the daughter of a certain Raghava Chakravarti. Raghava and his wife, Mahadavi, had been searching for a suitable husband for their daughter, with no success. They also received the order in a dream to give their daughter in marriage to Srinivasa. As a result, Srinivasa married for a second time. The pure devotee has no other objective in life than to fulfill the desires of the Supreme Lord. They are thus ready to do anything if it pleases the Lord. None of their activities has the slightest hint of material desire in it. Srinivasa Acharya is the Avesha incarnation of Mahaprabhu himself, and his transcendental activities can only be described by someone who has received his special blessings.

### Govinda Kaviraja: Lord Krishna’s Divine Poet | Sri Jai Chaitanya Dasa | Sri Gaura Mandala Parikrama
**Speaker:** HG Jai Chaitanya Dasa

#### lineage, early life, family
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Kos3wWxTRfM?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Shri Govinda Kaviraj, the brother of Ramachandra Kaviraj, was a great poet who subsequently surrendered his life at the lotus feet of Shri Radha and Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment describes the birth and early life of Govinda Kaviraj, including his family background and initial devotion to Goddess Durga.

Govinda Kaviraj. Shri Govinda Kaviraj, the brother of Ramachandra Kaviraj, was a great poet who subsequently surrendered his life at the lotus feet of Shri Radha and Krishna. At the time of Govinda's birth, Sunanda Devi suffered from intense labor pain. Damodara Sen was a devotee of Goddess Durga. When Damodara Sen learned about his daughter's predicament, he prayed to Durga Devi. He fed Sunanda the water from Durga Yantra. Soon, Sunanda gave birth to a beautiful child who was later named Govinda. A few years after Govinda was born, his father Chiranjeeva passed away. Being raised at his maternal father's home at Shrikanta, Govinda was brought up amidst Saktas or devotees of Maa Durga. As compared to his brother Ramachandra, who was a Krishna Bhakta, Govinda since his childhood was a worshipper of Bhagavati Devi, Goddess Durga. After Sunanda Devi passed away, both Ramachandra and Govinda shifted to Kumar Nagar. It was here at Kumar Nagara that Ramachandra Kaviraj got married to Ratnamala Devi and subsequently surrendered himself to Srinivasa Acharya. Soon afterwards, Ramachandra and Govinda decided to shift to the peaceful and blissful village of Talia Budhari near Bhagavangola.

---
#### devotion, Durga, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Kos3wWxTRfM?t=114
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He understood, later he understood that Lord Krishna is the worshipable master of Maa Durga."
**Summary:** This segment describes the events leading to Govinda Kaviraj's realization that Lord Krishna is supreme, including Ramachandra's desire to be near Srinivasa Acharya and the incident with the lotus flowers offered to Durga.

So Bhagavangola was not the birthplace of Ramachandra Kaviraj. This was the place they stayed most, where they already had some land. Ramachandra insisted on shifting to Telia Bandhura because he felt that over here he would receive more association with Srinivasa Acharya and would have to pass over Telia Budhari to reach to Sripata of Narottam Das at Rajshahi. As presented before, Ramachandra and Narottama subsequently became the best friends and could hardly tolerate separation from each other. It is mentioned in Bhakti Ratnakara, 'Talia Budhari Grame A Govindera Stiti, by Nirjana Sthaneti Prita Aati, Budhari Paschime Sri Paschima Paranama, Tata Sarva Rambhe Bhas Seho Ramya Sthana.' Devi Durga honors the offered lotus flowers in her hand. Adjacent to the residence of Govinda Kaviraj at Talia Budhari existed two ponds, which were commonly known as Jodha Pukura, pair of ponds. Innumerable lotuses were adorned these ponds. I don't know whether you noticed yesterday there was a small pond with lotus. Flower was not there but leaves were there. So once, while Ramachandra Kaviraj was passing by these ponds, he was enthralled to note its beauty. In his mind, he then offered a couple of lotus flowers unto Lord Govinda, his worshipable Ishta Deva. Who are the deities in Bhagwan Gola? So later, when Govinda Kaviraj plucked these same lotus flowers and offered them unto his worshipable Devi Durga, as he did every day, he was shocked to discover that Maa Durga was holding these lotus flowers in her hands, instead of them lying at her feet. So the lotus flower was offered at the feet, but she offered it to the Lord and it was held in her hand. He understood, later he understood that Lord Krishna is the worshipable master of Maa Durga.

---
#### surrender, liberation, Durga's instruction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Kos3wWxTRfM?t=283
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Only fools refuse to surrender unto Krishna for he is the supreme controller and the ultimate protector."
**Summary:** This segment narrates the story of Govinda Kaviraj's illness and his communication with Ramachandra and Srinivasa Acharya, culminating in Goddess Durga instructing him to meditate on the lotus feet of Lord Krishna for liberation.

And hence, anything offered to Krishna is sacred to all, including the goddess. One day, while Srinivasa and Ramachandra were sitting together, a messenger arrived carrying a letter from Govinda Kaviraj. In that letter, Govinda expressed that he was critically ill and requested his brother, Ramachandra, to come and see him at Telliya Budhari. Ramachandra did not want to take leave from his spiritual master, and so he hid the letter. A few days later, Govinda wrote a second letter expressing that his health has deteriorated further and wished to see Ramachandra and Srinivasa Charya. Actually, Govinda was a staunch devotee of Goddess Durga, and he prayed to the Goddess for his mercy. It is said that he had reached a perfectional stage in his worship of Goddess and had even seen her face to face. So when Govinda asked for liberation from her and requested her to bestow her mercy so that he could cross over the ocean of birth and death, the Goddess instructed him to meditate upon the lotus feet of Lord Krishna instantly. If he wished to be delivered, the Goddess revealed that she herself meditates upon and worships the reddish feet of the Supreme Lord Krishna. Krishna alone can deliver one from the clutches of material birth and death. Maa Durga added that there are hundreds of Durgas, Sivas and Brahmas who are constantly engaged in offering prayers and singing the glories of Lord Krishna, but they are yet to reach the end of Supreme Lord's unlimited glories. Only fools refuse to surrender unto Krishna for he is the supreme controller and the ultimate protector.

**Summary:** This segment describes Govinda's deteriorating health, his final plea to Ramachandra, Srinivasa Acharya's arrival, and Govinda's initiation into the Hare Krishna Mahamantra.

devi kahe bar bar govinda smarana kare highway nishtara translation suddenly govinda's health began deteriorating severely it appeared as if the time of his death had arrived he was suffering tremendously and the only visible symptom of his life was the outflow of his breath feeling helpless he prayed to his worship of the goddess my last days have now arrived please be merciful unto me and break my attachment with this material world speaking from the sky the goddess repeated again and again contemplate upon lord govinda krishna and only then will you achieve liberation from material bondage hearing this govinda was left shattered he could not believe his ears and realized that he has wasted his life not pursuing the path of devotion service unto lord krishna he remembered his brother ramachandra and wrote to him once more to meet him for the last time on receiving the letter ramachandra went to sinivasa acharya and requested him to accept govinda kaviraja as his disciple when he read govinda's letter shinivasa's heart softened and he decided to accompany ramachandra to talia budhari govinda was elated to hear this news and he ordered his son divya simha to make all the arrangements to welcome shinivasa banana trees were placed at crossings and mango leaves were strung at different places for decoration shinivasa arrived and went straight to meet govinda kaviraj the ladies made auspicious sounds while drums and cymbals were being played very sweetly govinda could not even sit up on his own and was helped by his relatives shinivasa acharya blessed him by placing his lotus feet upon his head after shinivasa bathed and accepted prasadam his remnants were fed to govinda kaviraj as soon as he honored the remnants govinda started feeling better the next day govinda kaviraj was given a bath and thereafter shinivasa acharya initiated him and whispered the hare krishna mahamantra in his ears shinivasa acharya taught him on how to meditate upon radha and krishna and instructed him to read the books composed by the goswamis.

Govinda gradually regained his strength and gave several gifts like gold cloths and other items unto his spiritual master in charity he condemned his previous life and was very thankful to brother because he was it was only for ramachandra that he had now received the mercy of shinivasa acharya govinda kaviraj lived for 36 more years and engaged himself completely service of shri guru and govinda expressed his earnest desire to glorify lord krishna and lord gauranga through his poems shinivasa acharya happily agreed and blessed him govinda kaviraj's poems are so powerful and so intense that many addressed him as the second vidyapati on the request of king govinda hari narayan he composed a beautiful book dedicated to his brother ramachandra the book was called shri rama charita gita on the request of king santoshadatta the ruler of keturi govinda composed a play known as sangeetha madhava nataka he was a regular visitor at the royal embassy assemblies of the kings of jeshore and pakkapalli jivago swami also desired to relish his poems and requested govinda to send his poems to vrindavan the senior devotees of vrindavan for great satisfaction in reading govinda's bhajans and they conferred the title kaviraj unto him.

### Haridasa Thakura: Acharya of the Holy Name | Sri Jai Chaitanya Dasa | Sri Gaura Mandala Parikrama
**Speaker:** HG Jai Chaitanya Dasa

#### holy name, Krishna, spiritual nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xfSVewiAPao?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The holy name of Krishna is transcendentally blissful. It bestows all spiritual benedictions, for it is Krishna himself, the reservoir of all pleasure."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the transcendental nature of Krishna's holy name, emphasizing its identity with Krishna and its freedom from material contamination.

This is a place, a deliverance of prostitute Lakshahira. There is this Madhavi tree nearby here, which Haridas Thakur chanted under, under which he chanted the holy name. The holy name of Krishna is transcendentally blissful. It bestows all spiritual benedictions, for it is Krishna himself, the reservoir of all pleasure. Krishna's name is complete, and it is the form of all transcendental mellows. The holy name of Krishna is not a material name under any condition, and it is no less powerful than Krishna himself. Since Krishna's name is not contaminated by the material qualities, there is no question of its being involved with maya. Krishna's name is always liberated and spiritual. It is never conditioned by the laws of material nature. This is because the name of Krishna and Krishna's name are the same.

---
#### Haridas Thakur, pastimes, location
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xfSVewiAPao?t=82
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Haridas Thakur had appeared in the village of Buron and by his mercy the nectar of the holy name became revealed in those blessed lands."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces Haridas Thakur, mentioning his birth and pastimes, and describes the geographical location of the lecture in relation to India and Navadvip.

Krishna himself is identical to Padma Purana. So, Dakur Haridas, one of the most important personalities. Buron gramete avatiruna Haridas. Se bhaagyase sabadeshe kirtanapratarsha. Katadhine takiyayaila gangatire. Asiyay rahila pulyas santipura. Paya tahana sanga acharya gosai, humkara karena anandera antanai. Haridas Thakur had appeared in the village of Buron and by his mercy the nectar of the holy name became revealed in those blessed lands. After staying many days and manifesting numerous pastimes, Srila Haridas shifted to Puliya, a place situated by the Ganges. Sri Advaita Acharya had his residence not very far from here at Shantipur. By the way, we are very very close to India border, close almost parallel to Navadvip.

And if you go to Haridas Thakur's birthplace, there is a small stream right across the river. Across is India, so you can see India there. Anybody feeling homesick, don't jump across. So we went all the way parallel to Padma river, north to Malda, across the Malda, and we came parallel down south again, almost parallel to Calcutta. Then we go to Dhaka and go further northeast to Shilad. Lord Brahma, Prahlad Maharaj and Richika Muni's son, Mahatapa had combinedly incarnated as Haridas Thakur. Srila Haridas Thakur is the Namacharya of our Sampradaya and is an eternal associate of Lord Gauranga. Srila Haridas Thakur had accompanied the Lord in most of his magnanimous pastimes. He was an empowered incarnation of the Supreme Lord, who had appeared just to set an example for all of us to follow. He had delivered innumerable suffering souls by bestowing upon them the nectar of the holy name. Though being born in a family of Muslims, Haridas Thakur widely propagated the chanting of Krishna's holy names to be the supreme spiritual practice and preached how the Lord and His holy names are actually non-different.

He was born, it's considered that he was born here. He lived his younger days here. Then he moved from here to Benapur, for his bhajan, before going towards Navadvip. Navadvip, West Bengal is not very far from here. It is right across the stream. This is the southernmost part we would be in Bangladesh. After this, we would move northwards.
